topics.title: Fundamental concepts and components of VR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT824 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamental concepts and components of VR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Virtual Reality (VR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Virtual Reality (VR)** is a computer-generated simulated environment that immerses the user by replacing their real-world sensory perception (primarily sight and hearing, and increasingly touch, smell, and proprioception) with synthetic stimuli, thereby creating the perception of being physically present inside a non-physical, three-dimensional, computer-generated world.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy, **Virtual Reality** belongs to the family of **Extended Reality (XR)** technologies and is formally defined by three orthogonal properties (originally proposed by Gigante in 1993, refined by Burdea & Coiffet in their seminal 1994 work *Virtual Reality Technology*):

| Property | Meaning | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Immersion** | Sensory envelopment of the user | Hardware-side (displays, haptics, audio) |
| **Interaction** | Real-time user manipulation of virtual objects | Software-side (input pipelines, physics) |
| **Imagination** | Conceptual richness of the synthetic scene | Content-side (modelling, narrative) |

A VR system is *legitimate* (i.e., it is VR and not merely 3D graphics) **only when all three properties coexist**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU 2024 Module-1 Syllabus Highlight):**
> - **VR** = User is fully cut off from the real world (opaque HMD).
> - **AR (Augmented Reality)** = Real world + virtual overlays (transparent HMD / passthrough).
> - **MR (Mixed Reality)** = Real and virtual objects interact bidirectionally and are spatially anchored.
> - **AVR (Augmented Virtuality)** = Mostly virtual world with some real-world elements (e.g., live camera feed of hands).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Dream-Catcher vs. The Cinema Screen"

Imagine a movie theatre:
- A **2D film** is like looking at a *window* into another world — your eyes are aware of the room, the chair, the popcorn.
- A **3D film with glasses** is like leaning *closer to the window* — you get depth, but you cannot walk through it.
- A **VR Head-Mounted Display (HMD)** is like **wearing the dream-catcher** — the moment the headset is on, the cinema, the chair, the popcorn *cease to exist perceptually*. You are *inside* the dream. You can turn your head and find that the dream extends in every direction. You can stretch out your hand and find the dream has objects you can pick up.

This is the precise cognitive shift VR is engineered to produce: **presence** — the psychological state of "being there" inside a synthetic environment. The engineering challenge of VR is therefore *not* the rendering of pretty 3D graphics (that is a solved problem since the 1990s), but the **plausibility of the illusion**, governed by the VR pipeline's latency, fidelity, and tracking accuracy.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Perceptual Constants (KTU High-Yield Memorization Table)

These constants are routinely referenced in KTU Module-1 derivations and are considered **must-memorize** for the ESE:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Interpupillary Distance (adult human) | $d_{IPD}$ | **63 ± 4 mm** | mm |
| Horizontal Field of View (human eye) | $FoV_{h,eye}$ | **~200°** (binocular) | degrees |
| Stereo overlap (binocular FOV) | $FoV_{stereo}$ | **~120°** | degrees |
| Visual acuity (Snellen 20/20) | $\theta_{min}$ | **1 arc-minute = 1/60°** | arc-min |
| Refresh rate threshold (VR) | $f_{refresh}$ | **≥ 90 Hz** | Hz |
| Motion-to-Photon latency budget | $t_{latency}$ | **< 20 ms** | ms |
| Human Vestibulo-Ocular Reflex delay | $t_{VOR}$ | **~7–15 ms** | ms |
| Foveal angular density | $\rho_{fovea}$ | **~60 PPD** | pixels/degree |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** The *Motion-to-Photon Latency* is the single most-examined parameter in Module 1. It is the elapsed time from the moment the user *physically moves* to the moment a *new photon* (rendered image) leaves the display panel. Exceeding **20 ms** in head-tracking latency causes **simulator sickness** (vection-induced nausea).

---

## 1.4 Brief Historical Evolution of VR (Context for ESE)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 may ask a 3-mark question tracing the historical milestones. Memorize the four anchors below.

1. **1962 — Sensorama (Morton Heilig):** First multi-sensory theatre (stereo sound, vibration, smell). Considered the *progenitor* of VR.
2. **1968 — "The Sword of Damocles" (Ivan Sutherland & Bob Sproull):** First HMD, so heavy it had to be suspended from the ceiling. Established the **see-through HMD** concept and stereoscopic rendering.
3. **1987 — "VPL Research" (Jaron Lanier):** Coined the term *Virtual Reality*; commercialized DataGlove and EyePhone HMD.
4. **2012 — Oculus Rift Kickstarter (Palmer Luckey):** Sparked the modern consumer VR era. The DK1 prototype established the modern HMD form factor.

---

## 1.5 Why VR Matters in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) Context

Since this course is **OECPT824 — AVR for CPS**, every concept must be linked back to cyber-physical systems:

> A modern **CPS** (e.g., a smart factory, autonomous vehicle, or surgical robot) requires a *human-in-the-loop* or *human-on-the-loop* supervisory layer. VR provides the **immersive telepresence interface** that allows a human operator to:
> - **Sense** the state of a remote or hazardous physical process.
> - **Manipulate** physical actuators through virtual handshakes.
> - **Predict** the future state of the CPS through digital twin visualisation.

This makes VR not just a *gaming* technology, but a **first-class CPS interaction paradigm** — a point the KTU examiner rewards heavily in ESE answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Pillars of a VR System (Burdea Triangle)

Burdea's *Virtual Reality Technology* (2nd Edition, Wiley-IEEE Press) — the canonical reference cited in KTU 2024 Module-1 — formalizes every VR system as a **3-I triangle**: **Immersion + Interaction + Imagination**.

### 2.1.1 Pillar 1 — Immersion (The "I" of Belonging)

Immersion is the *hardware* and *perceptual* envelope. It is decomposed into three sub-pipelines:

1. **Visual Immersion** — Driven by HMD optics, display panel (LCD / OLED / micro-LED), pixels-per-degree (PPD), field-of-view (FoV), and refresh rate.
2. **Auditory Immersion** — Delivered by spatial audio (binaural, ambisonics, head-related transfer function — HRTF).
3. **Haptic Immersion** — Force feedback, vibrotactile, and thermal feedback via exoskeletons, gloves, or mid-air ultrasound arrays.

> **The "Why":** Humans are *amphibious* perceivers. We trust sensory information that is *consistent across modalities*. If the visual system says "I am in a forest" but the vestibular system says "I am sitting in a chair", the brain rejects the illusion. Hence, immersion must be *multi-modal and synchronized*.

### 2.1.2 Pillar 2 — Interaction (The "I" of Doing)

Interaction is the *software* and *tracking* layer. It is decomposed into:

- **Selection & Manipulation** — Picking, moving, deforming virtual objects.
- **Navigation** — Travel through the virtual space (real walking, teleportation, fly-through).
- **System Control** — Issuing commands (menus, voice, gesture).

> **The "How":** Every interaction is implemented as a closed-loop: *User Intent → Input Sensor → Tracker → Software Event → Render Update → Display*. The **loop latency** determines whether the interaction feels *real* or *sluggish*.

### 2.1.3 Pillar 3 — Imagination (The "I" of Being)

Imagination is the *content* and *narrative* layer. It encompasses:

- **Geometric Modelling** — Polygonal meshes, point clouds, NURBS, voxels.
- **Behavioural Modelling** — Physics simulation, AI-driven NPCs, crowd simulation.
- **Narrative Design** — Story arcs, emotional beats, worldbuilding.

---

## 2.2 The Five Core Components of a VR System (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists these as the **core components**. Memorize in order: **(1) Input Devices → (2) Output Devices → (3) Software Engine → (4) Computing Hardware → (5) User / Human Factor**.

### 2.2.1 Component 1 — Input Devices (Sensors)

| Sub-Category | Examples | Tracking Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **Head Trackers** | IMU (gyro+accel+mag), Inside-out cameras, Outside-in (Lighthouse, Constellation) | 6-DoF (3 translation + 3 rotation) |
| **Hand Trackers** | DataGlove (fiber-optic flex), Leap Motion (IR depth), Controller IMUs | Finger-joint angles, hand pose |
| **Eye Trackers** | Pupil-tracking IR cameras (Tobii, Pupil Labs) | Corneal reflection / pupil centre |
| **Body Trackers** | Full-body IMU suits (e.g., Xsens), marker-based mocap (Vicon) | Skeletal pose estimation |
| **Locomotion Trackers** | Treadmills (Omni), natural-walking sensors | Positional drift correction |

> **Engineering Insight:** Tracking must satisfy **Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DoF)** — pitch, yaw, roll (rotation) + X, Y, Z (translation). Modern HMDs use *sensor fusion* of high-frequency IMU (≥ 1 kHz) with low-frequency optical tracking (≤ 120 Hz) via a **Kalman filter** or **complementary filter**.

### 2.2.2 Component 2 — Output Devices (Displays)

| Sub-Category | Examples | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Head-Mounted Display (HMD)** | Quest 3, Vision Pro, Valve Index, HTC Vive | Primary visual output |
| **Cave Automatic Virtual Environment (CAVE)** | Multi-wall projection room | Room-scale stereoscopic projection |
| **Haptic Devices** | Tactile gloves, exoskeletons, mid-air ultrasound | Force / vibrotactile feedback |
| **3D Audio** | Headphones, HRTF processing | Spatial sound localization |
| **Olfactory Displays** | Smell generators (experimental) | Scent emission |

### 2.2.3 Component 3 — Software Engine (Middleware)

The VR *runtime* software stack:
1. **Operating System Layer** — Windows / Android XR / visionOS / Linux.
2. **VR Runtime / SDK** — OpenXR, SteamVR, Oculus Runtime, VisionOS SDK.
3. **Game / Simulation Engine** — Unity, Unreal Engine, Godot.
4. **Application Layer** — The specific VR experience (training sim, game, digital twin).

> [!NOTE]
> **OpenXR** is the cross-vendor standard by Khronos Group. It abstracts vendor-specific runtimes behind a unified C API, enabling a single binary to run on Meta, HTC, Valve, and Microsoft HMDs.

### 2.2.4 Component 4 — Computing Hardware

- **Standalone (All-in-One) HMDs:** Snapdragon XR2 / Apple M2 + on-board SLAM.
- **Tethered HMDs:** External GPU workstation (NVIDIA RTX 4090) connected via DisplayPort / USB-C / WiGig.
- **Cloud-Rendered VR:** Edge GPU streams compressed frames to thin-client HMD (latency-sensitive; requires 5G + foveated streaming).

### 2.2.5 Component 5 — User / Human Factors (The Often-Ignored Component)

- **IPD (Interpupillary Distance):** Software-adjustable lens spacing.
- **Eye Relief:** Distance from eye to lens (spectacle-wearer friendly).
- **Comfort:** Weight distribution, face-padding foam, heat dissipation.
- **Cybersickness Mitigation:** Comfort ratings (Comfortable / Moderate / Intense), snap-turn, vignette teleportation.

---

## 2.3 Classification of VR by Immersion Level (KTU 3-Mark Favourite)

| Level | Description | Hardware | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-Immersive** | Desktop window / monitor with mouse | Standard PC | $ |
| **Semi-Immersive** | Large screen / single-wall projection, head-tracked stereo | CAVE-lite, shutter glasses | $$ |
| **Fully Immersive** | Opaque HMD or surround CAVE, 6-DoF, multi-modal | HMD + haptics + tracker | $$$ |
| **Collaborative / Distributed** | Multiple users share the virtual space across network | HMDs + low-latency LAN | $$$$ |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every formula here is a *high-yield* target for numerical / derivation questions in ESE. Master the derivation (in Section 3) and the symbolic form (here).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols Explained | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | **Field of View (Diagonal)** | $FoV_{diag} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d_{diag}}{2 \cdot f}\right)$ | $d_{diag}$ = diagonal display size, $f$ = focal length | degrees |
| F2 | **Field of View (Horizontal)** | $FoV_{h} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2 \cdot f}\right)$ | $w$ = display width | degrees |
| F3 | **Field of View (Vertical)** | $FoV_{v} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2 \cdot f}\right)$ | $h$ = display height | degrees |
| F4 | **Pixels Per Degree (PPD)** | $PPD = \dfrac{R_{h}}{FoV_{h}}$ | $R_{h}$ = horizontal resolution (pixels) | px/° |
| F5 | **Binocular Stereo Overlap** | $FoV_{stereo} = FoV_{mono} - 2 \cdot \alpha_{stereo}$ | $\alpha_{stereo}$ = monocular exclusion zone (~30°) | degrees |
| F6 | **Stereoscopic Parallax (Convergence)** | $C = \dfrac{2 \cdot f \cdot d_{IPD}}{2 \cdot Z \cdot \tan(\theta/2)}$ | $Z$ = object depth, $\theta$ = convergence angle | pixels |
| F7 | **Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget** | $t_{M2P} = t_{sense} + t_{track} + t_{render} + t_{encode} + t_{transmit} + t_{display}$ | Sum of pipeline stages | ms |
| F8 | **Frame Budget at Refresh Rate** | $t_{frame} = \dfrac{1000}{f_{refresh}}$ | Inverse of refresh rate | ms |
| F9 | **Retinal Image Size** | $s_{retina} = d \cdot \tan(\theta)$ | $d$ = eye's nodal distance (~17 mm) | mm |
| F10 | **Snellen Acuity → Minimum Angular Resolution** | $\theta_{min} = \dfrac{1\ \text{arc-minute}}{60} = \dfrac{1}{60}^{\circ}$ | Threshold of human vision | degrees |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Warning on Notation:** In the formulas above, the fraction bars use parentheses, **never the absolute-value pipe `\vert`**, so they render correctly inside markdown tables. For handwritten exams, write the derivation **outside** the table on a separate line.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Formulas

- **F1–F3 (FoV):** Used to size the **lens focal length** for any HMD. A larger FoV requires a *shorter* focal length lens, which in turn reduces the *eye-relief* — a direct constraint on spectacle compatibility.
- **F4 (PPD):** Used to determine the **"retina-display" threshold** for an HMD. The Apple Vision Pro targets ~**35 PPD**; the human fovea is ~**60 PPD**. The closer to 60, the lower the *screen-door effect*.
- **F7 (M2P Latency):** Used as the **end-to-end KPI** for every VR system. Engineering teams split this budget across 6 stages and optimize the slowest stage.

---

## 2.6 The Burdea Triangle Mapped to CPS

> **For CPS engineering students (the OECPT824 context):**

| Burdea Pillar | CPS Mapping | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Immersion | Sensory state acquisition from physical plant | Digital twin rendering sensor data into a 3D VR view |
| Interaction | Human-in-the-loop control of actuators | Surgeon operating a remote robotic arm via VR gloves |
| Imagination | Predictive what-if simulation | "What if this valve fails?" scenario in a smart-factory VR |

This three-way mapping is the *intellectual bridge* between VR and CPS that the KTU Module-1 syllabus asks students to articulate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every derivation below is **closed-form and numerically evaluable**. For the KTU ESE, you are expected to show the substitution of standard human-factor values ($d_{IPD} = 63\,\text{mm}$, $f = 12\,\text{mm}$, etc.) and arrive at a numerical answer. **Do not skip steps.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field of View (FoV) of an HMD Lens System

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A typical HMD uses a single **convergent (convex) lens** of focal length $f$, placed at distance $f$ from a display panel of horizontal width $w$ and height $h$. The user's eye is placed at the **exit pupil** of the lens, located at the focal plane on the eye side.

We wish to derive the **horizontal Field of View** $FoV_{h}$ subtended at the eye by the extreme left and right edges of the display.

### 3.1.2 Geometric Setup

Imagine the eye at the origin $O$. The half-width of the display is $w/2$. The lens sits at distance $f$ from the eye (effectively, the eye-side focal length). The half-angle subtended at the eye by the right edge of the display is $\theta_{h/2}$. Therefore:

$$
\tan(\theta_{h/2}) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{w/2}{f}
$$

The total horizontal FoV is twice the half-angle:

$$
\boxed{FoV_{h} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{w}{2f}\right)}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Numerical Evaluation

Let us plug in the values of a contemporary HMD: **Meta Quest 3** specifies a single-eye horizontal resolution $R_{h} = 2064$ pixels, display width $w = 24.2\,\text{mm}$, and a Pancake lens focal length $f \approx 12\,\text{mm}$.

$$
\frac{w}{2f} = \frac{24.2\,\text{mm}}{2 \times 12\,\text{mm}} = \frac{24.2}{24} = 1.0083
$$

$$
\theta_{h/2} = \arctan(1.0083) = 45.22^{\circ}
$$

$$
FoV_{h} = 2 \times 45.22^{\circ} = 90.44^{\circ}
$$

This is the **horizontal FoV per eye** — a value that closely matches Meta's published ~**110° diagonal**, ~**96° horizontal** spec, with the small discrepancy due to the manufacturer's use of the *outer edge of the lens stack* rather than the geometric edge of the panel.

### 3.1.4 Why It Matters

A wider FoV increases **immersion** (the periphery of the human eye can see ~200°, so a 90° HMD covers only ~45% of natural vision). However, increasing FoV requires either a *shorter focal length* (which reduces eye relief) or a *larger display* (which increases weight and cost). This is the central **engineering trade-off** of HMD design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pixels Per Degree (PPD) and the "Retina" Threshold

### 3.2.1 Definition

The **PPD** is the number of distinct pixels the eye sees within a 1° arc of visual field. It is the single metric that quantifies the *apparent sharpness* of an HMD.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

If the horizontal resolution is $R_{h}$ pixels distributed across the horizontal FoV $FoV_{h}$ (in degrees), then by definition:

$$
PPD = \frac{R_{h}}{FoV_{h}}
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example — Apple Vision Pro

The Vision Pro specifies $R_{h} = 3660$ pixels per eye and a horizontal FoV of approximately $FoV_{h} \approx 100^{\circ}$.

$$
PPD = \frac{3660}{100} = 36.6\ \text{px/°}
$$

### 3.2.4 The "Retina" Threshold

A normal human with **20/20 vision** can resolve **1 arc-minute** = $\dfrac{1}{60}^{\circ}$. To match this acuity, the HMD must provide:

$$
PPD_{retina} = \frac{1\ \text{pixel}}{\frac{1}{60}^{\circ}} = 60\ \text{px/°}
$$

Therefore, the Vision Pro at 36.6 PPD provides **roughly 61%** of "retina" sharpness. The **fovea centralis** (the highest-acuity region of the retina) actually demands **~60 PPD**, so most HMDs remain *acuity-limited* — a key bottleneck in display engineering.

### 3.2.5 The Engineering Implication — Foveated Rendering

Because the human eye only resolves 60 PPD in the fovea (~2° of central vision) and far less in the periphery, modern HMDs use **eye-tracking + foveated rendering**: render the foveal region at 60 PPD and the peripheral region at 10–20 PPD, saving **2–3× GPU compute** with imperceptible quality loss.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stereoscopic Depth from Convergence

### 3.3.1 The Geometry of Stereo Vision

When a human looks at a 3D point, the two eyes **converge** inward by an angle $\theta$. The point is at distance $Z$ from the eyes. The horizontal separation of the eyes is $d_{IPD}$. The convergence angle is:

$$
\theta = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{d_{IPD}}{2 Z}\right)
$$

### 3.3.2 Inverting to Find Depth

Solving for $Z$:

$$
\boxed{Z = \frac{d_{IPD}}{2 \cdot \tan(\theta/2)}}
$$

### 3.3.3 Numerical Example

If $d_{IPD} = 63\,\text{mm} = 0.063\,\text{m}$ and the convergence angle is $\theta = 1.5^{\circ}$ (a typical fixation angle for a desktop monitor at ~2.4 m):

$$
Z = \frac{0.063}{2 \cdot \tan(0.75^{\circ})} = \frac{0.063}{2 \times 0.01309} = \frac{0.063}{0.02618} = 2.406\ \text{m}
$$

This matches the expected monitor distance — confirming the formula.

### 3.3.4 Mapping Depth to Pixel Disparity in the HMD

In an HMD, the rendering engine must shift the left-eye image and right-eye image relative to each other by an amount called **disparity** $D$, in pixels, to produce the convergence cue:

$$
D = \frac{f \cdot d_{IPD}}{Z \cdot p}
$$

where $p$ is the pixel pitch of the display. For a desktop distance of $Z = 2.4\,\text{m}$ and a Quest 3 with $f = 12\,\text{mm}$, $d_{IPD} = 63\,\text{mm}$, $p \approx 11.7\,\mu\text{m} = 1.17 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}$:

$$
D = \frac{0.012 \times 0.063}{2.4 \times 1.17 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{7.56 \times 10^{-4}}{2.808 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 26.9\ \text{pixels}
$$

So at desktop distance, the two eyes' images must be shifted by ~**27 pixels** to recreate correct convergence.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Motion-to-Photon (M2P) Latency Budget

### 3.4.1 Decomposition

End-to-end latency from physical head motion to the corresponding photon leaving the display is the sum of **six pipeline stages**:

$$
t_{M2P} = t_{sense} + t_{track} + t_{render} + t_{encode} + t_{transmit} + t_{display}
$$

| Stage | Symbol | Typical Value | Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor sampling | $t_{sense}$ | 1 ms | IMU |
| Tracking / fusion | $t_{track}$ | 1 ms | SoC |
| Pose → Render | $t_{render}$ | 8–11 ms | GPU |
| Encode / composite | $t_{encode}$ | 1 ms | ASIC |
| Transmit (DisplayPort) | $t_{transmit}$ | < 1 ms | Cable / WiGig |
| Pixel response (panel) | $t_{display}$ | 2–5 ms | LCD / OLED |

### 3.4.2 The Frame-Budget Constraint

At a 90 Hz refresh rate, the frame budget is:

$$
t_{frame} = \frac{1000\,\text{ms}}{90\,\text{Hz}} = 11.11\,\text{ms}
$$

This means **every stage combined must finish within 11.11 ms** — and the render stage alone can consume 8–11 ms. Modern HMDs use **asynchronous timewarp (ATW)** and **asynchronous spacewarp (ASW)** to compensate: if a frame is not ready, the *previous* frame is reprojected with a small rotational warp based on the latest pose, hiding the missed deadline.

### 3.4.3 The 20-ms Threshold and Cybersickness

When $t_{M2P} > 20\,\text{ms}$, the visual system updates *after* the vestibular system has registered the head motion. This sensory mismatch triggers **vection-induced cybersickness** (nausea, disorientation). The KTU 2024 syllabus highlights this as the *defining* constraint of VR engineering.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of VR Display Technologies

| Parameter | Smartphone-Based VR (e.g., Cardboard, 2014) | Tethered HMD (e.g., Valve Index, 2019) | Standalone HMD (e.g., Quest 3, 2023) | Micro-OLED HMD (e.g., Vision Pro, 2024) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resolution per eye | 1080 × 1200 | 1440 × 1600 | 2064 × 2208 | 3660 × 3200 |
| Refresh rate (Hz) | 60 | 120 (144 experimental) | 90 / 120 | 100 |
| FoV (°) | ~90 | ~130 | ~110 | ~100 |
| PPD (approx.) | ~12 | ~14 | ~25 | ~36 |
| Tracking | 3-DoF | 6-DoF (Lighthouse) | 6-DoF (inside-out) | 6-DoF (inside-out) |
| Latency (ms) | ~40–50 | ~15 | ~12 | ~11 |
| Mobility | High | Low (tethered) | High | High |
| Cost (USD) | $15 | $999 | $499 | $3499 |

> **Reading the matrix:** PPD has roughly **tripled** in a decade. The 20-ms latency barrier was breached by tethered HMDs in 2019 and is now matched by standalone devices — a milestone for CPS-grade VR deployments.

---

## 3.6 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (ESE-Style, 7 Marks)

> **Problem (KTU-style):** An HMD uses a Fresnel lens of focal length $f = 14\,\text{mm}$ and a display of horizontal width $w = 28.9\,\text{mm}$. The display has $R_{h} = 2160$ horizontal pixels. Compute the horizontal FoV, the PPD, and verify whether the device meets the "retina" threshold (60 PPD). What is the minimum resolution needed to achieve 60 PPD with the same lens?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute horizontal FoV:**

$$
FoV_{h} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{w}{2f}\right) = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{28.9\,\text{mm}}{2 \times 14\,\text{mm}}\right) = 2 \cdot \arctan(1.0321)
$$

$$
\theta_{h/2} = \arctan(1.0321) \approx 45.92^{\circ}
$$

$$
FoV_{h} = 2 \times 45.92^{\circ} \approx 91.84^{\circ}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute PPD:**

$$
PPD = \frac{R_{h}}{FoV_{h}} = \frac{2160}{91.84} \approx 23.52\ \text{px/°}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to retina threshold:**

Since $23.52 < 60$, the HMD is **acuity-limited** — it does NOT meet the retina threshold. The image will appear pixelated at the fovea.

**Step 4 — Compute minimum resolution for 60 PPD:**

$$
R_{h,\min} = 60 \times FoV_{h} = 60 \times 91.84 \approx 5510\ \text{pixels}
$$

So the HMD would need **~2.55×** more pixels per eye to reach "retina" clarity. (This is why the Vision Pro ships ~3660 pixels — it is approaching but has not yet reached the threshold.)

**Final Answer:** $FoV_{h} = 91.84^{\circ}$, $PPD \approx 23.5$, **fails** retina threshold, $R_{h,\min} \approx 5510$ px.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are **Mermaid block diagrams** (architectural, not photorealistic). They comply with the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasis on *system-level understanding* over physical drawing.

---

## 4.1 Diagram A — End-to-End VR System Architecture (Burdea-Inspired Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userA[User Head Motion] --> imuA[IMU Sensor 1kHz]
    userA --> camA[Inside Out Camera 90Hz]
    imuA --> fusionA[Sensor Fusion Kalman Filter]
    camA --> fusionA
    fusionA --> poseA[6DoF Head Pose]
    poseA --> engA[Game Engine Unity Unreal]
    engA --> rdrA[GPU Renderer]
    rdrA --> encA[Video Encoder DisplayPort]
    encA --> panA[Display Panel LCD OLED MicroLED]
    panA --> lensA[Pancake Fresnel Lens]
    lensA --> eyeA[Human Eye Fovea + Periphery]
    eyeA --> brainA[Perceptual Cortex]
    brainA -.presence feedback.-> userA

    userB[User Hand Motion] --> ctrlB[Controller IMU]
    userB --> leapB[Leap Motion Hand Tracker]
    ctrlB --> fusionB[Hand Pose Fusion]
    leapB --> fusionB
    fusionB --> engA

    haptA[Haptic Glove Exoskeleton] --> userB
    engA --> audioA[Binaural Audio HRTF]
    audioA --> headA[Headphones]

    style userA fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style brainA fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
    style lensA fill:#fc9,stroke:#333
    style eyeA fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style engA fill:#c9f,stroke:#333
    style rdrA fill:#cff,stroke:#333
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The **closed loop on the left** (User → Sensor → Fusion → Engine → Renderer → Display → Lens → Eye → Brain → back to User) is the **motion-to-photon loop**. Its total round-trip time is the **M2P latency**.
- The **purple boxes** (Engine, Renderer) are the *software-defined* stages.
- The **orange/green boxes** (Lens, Eye) are the *optical and biological* stages.
- The **dashed arrow** from Brain to User represents the *psychological* feedback loop of *presence* — the ultimate output of VR.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — VR Immersion Level Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    lvl0[Non Immersive\nDesktop Monitor\n3DOF Mouse]
    lvl1[Semi Immersive\nSingle Wall Projection\nShutter Glasses 3DOF]
    lvl2[Fully Immersive\nHMD 6DOF\nBinaural Audio]
    lvl3[Collaborative Distributed\nMultiple HMDs\nNetworked 6DOF]

    lvl0 --> lvl1
    lvl1 --> lvl2
    lvl2 --> lvl3

    lvl0 -.- cost0[Cost Dollar 100 to 500]
    lvl1 -.- cost1[Cost Dollar 2000 to 10000]
    lvl2 -.- cost2[Cost Dollar 500 to 3500]
    lvl3 -.- cost3[Cost Dollar 5000 plus plus]

    style lvl0 fill:#eef
    style lvl1 fill:#dde
    style lvl2 fill:#ccd
    style lvl3 fill:#bbc
```

**Reading Guide:**
- Each level adds **one capability** (immersion dimension).
- Cost does *not* scale linearly with capability — e.g., Fully Immersive (lvl2) is now cheaper than Semi-Immersive (lvl1) due to consumer-market economies of scale.

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — VR Pipeline vs. CPS Feedback Loop (Bridge Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHYS[Physical World CPS]
        sensor1[Physical Sensor\nTemperature Pressure]
        actuator1[Physical Actuator\nMotor Valve]
    end

    subgraph CYBER[Cyber World VR]
        twin1[Digital Twin Model]
        render1[VR Renderer]
        display1[VR Headset]
    end

    subgraph HUMAN[Human Operator]
        eye1[Human Eye]
        brain1[Human Brain]
        hand1[Human Hand Controller]
    end

    sensor1 -->|telemetry| twin1
    twin1 --> render1
    render1 --> display1
    display1 --> eye1
    eye1 --> brain1
    brain1 --> hand1
    hand1 -->|control command| actuator1
    actuator1 -->|physical change| sensor1

    style PHYS fill:#ffe
    style CYBER fill:#eef
    style HUMAN fill:#efe
```

**Reading Guide:** This is the **canonical CPS-VR bridge**. The physical CPS is *sensed* into the digital twin, the digital twin is *visualised* in VR, the human *perceives and decides*, and the decision is *commanded back* to the actuator. The entire loop is the **CPS feedback controller** — a closed cyber-physical system mediated by a VR interface.

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — M2P Latency Budget Allocation (Stacked Bar)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    box1[IMU Sense 1ms]
    box2[Track Fusion 1ms]
    box3[Render 8ms]
    box4[Encode 1ms]
    box5[Transmit 1ms]
    box6[Display 5ms]
    sum[Total 17ms within 20ms Budget OK]

    box1 --> box2 --> box3 --> box4 --> box5 --> box6 --> sum

    style box3 fill:#f96,stroke:#900
    style sum fill:#9f9,stroke:#060
```

**Reading Guide:** The *Render* stage (orange) is the **single largest contributor** at ~47% of the budget. This is why every major VR engineering effort (foveated rendering, ATW, ASW, GPU optimization) targets the *render* stage.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Exam) pattern** for the OECPT824 course: Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks with **internal choice** between two alternatives. Sub-parts are split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

> **Define Virtual Reality. List the three fundamental properties (3-I triangle) of a VR system as proposed by Burdea & Coiffet, with a one-line explanation of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Virtual Reality (VR) is a computer-generated three-dimensional simulated environment that, through appropriate sensory displays and tracking, gives the user the perception of *being physically present* inside a non-physical world, while allowing real-time interaction with that world.

The three fundamental properties are:

1. **Immersion** — The degree to which the user is *sensorially enveloped* by the synthetic environment (e.g., via HMD, spatial audio, haptics).
2. **Interaction** — The user's ability to *manipulate* virtual objects and navigate the synthetic world in real time.
3. **Imagination** — The *creative richness* of the synthetic scene, encompassing geometric, behavioural, and narrative modelling.

> **Mark Split:** Definition (1 m) + 3-I list with one-line explanations (2 m).

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Paper]

> **Differentiate between Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR) with two points each. Give one real-world application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Virtual Reality (VR) | Augmented Reality (AR) |
|---|---|---|
| **World Coverage** | *Replaces* the real world entirely; user sees only the synthetic environment. | *Overlays* synthetic content onto the real world; user sees both. |
| **Display Hardware** | Opaque HMD (e.g., Meta Quest 3). | Transparent optics / passthrough (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens). |
| **Application** | Surgical training simulator (practise a procedure in a fully virtual OR). | Aircraft maintenance assistant (overlay step-by-step instructions on the engine). |

> **Mark Split:** 2 differentiation points (2 m) + 1 application example (1 m).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer *either* Question B1 *or* Question B2. Each carries 14 marks. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are each 7 marks.

---

### Question B1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

> **(a)** [7 marks] With a neat block diagram, describe the **five core components of a VR system**. Explain the function of each component with one example.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] An HMD uses a Fresnel lens of focal length $f = 15\,\text{mm}$ and a display panel with horizontal width $w = 30.5\,\text{mm}$. The display has $R_{h} = 2400$ horizontal pixels. Compute the **horizontal Field of View** and the **Pixels Per Degree (PPD)**. Comment on whether the device meets the *retina* acuity threshold of **60 PPD**. If not, compute the **minimum horizontal resolution** required to achieve 60 PPD with the same lens.

#### Model Answer for B1(a) — 7 marks

The **five core components of a VR system** (as per the KTU 2024 syllabus, Module 1) are:

1. **Input Devices (Sensors):** Capture the user's physical state — head pose, hand pose, gaze, body posture, locomotion.
   *Example:* Inside-out cameras (e.g., Quest 3) track the 6-DoF pose of the HMD by detecting IR features on the environment.

2. **Output Devices (Displays):** Deliver the synthetic sensory stimuli back to the user — visual (HMD, CAVE), auditory (HRTF headphones), haptic (gloves, exoskeletons).
   *Example:* Dual 2.5K micro-OLED panels in the PlayStation VR2 display the rendered scene.

3. **Software Engine (Middleware):** The runtime + application stack that binds input to output, including the OS, VR runtime (OpenXR), game engine (Unity/Unreal), and the application logic.
   *Example:* A Unity-based driving simulator uses the OpenXR plugin to abstract Quest/Vive/Index HMDs behind a unified API.

4. **Computing Hardware:** The processing unit — either on-board the HMD (standalone: Snapdragon XR2, Apple M2) or in an external workstation tethered to the HMD (NVIDIA RTX-class GPU).
   *Example:* Tethered Valve Index uses a desktop RTX 4080 GPU for 144 Hz stereo rendering.

5. **User / Human Factor:** The human operator and their physiological constraints (IPD, eye relief, vestibular sensitivity, comfort).
   *Example:* A user with $d_{IPD} = 58\,\text{mm}$ uses the HMD's software IPD adjustment to align the lens centres, avoiding eye-strain and diplopia.

> **Mark Split:** Block diagram (2 m) + 5 component explanations (1 m each = 5 m).

#### Model Answer for B1(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute horizontal FoV (2 marks):**

$$
FoV_{h} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{w}{2f}\right) = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{30.5}{2 \times 15}\right) = 2 \cdot \arctan(1.0167)
$$

$$
\theta_{h/2} = \arctan(1.0167) \approx 45.47^{\circ}
$$

$$
\boxed{FoV_{h} \approx 90.94^{\circ}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute PPD (1 mark):**

$$
PPD = \frac{R_{h}}{FoV_{h}} = \frac{2400}{90.94} \approx 26.39\ \text{px/°}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to retina threshold (1 mark):**

Since $26.39 < 60$, the device **fails** the retina acuity threshold. The user will perceive visible pixelation (the "screen-door effect") when reading small text or inspecting fine details.

**Step 4 — Compute minimum resolution for 60 PPD (3 marks):**

$$
R_{h,\min} = 60 \times FoV_{h} = 60 \times 90.94 = 5456.4\ \text{pixels}
$$

Therefore, the **minimum horizontal resolution required is ~5457 pixels per eye**, which is approximately **2.27×** the current 2400 pixels. (This is the direction the HMD industry is heading — the Vision Pro's 3660 pixels and the Quest Pro 2's expected 4K+ pixels are responses to this same arithmetic.)

> **Mark Split:** Step 1 (2 m) + Step 2 (1 m) + Step 3 (1 m) + Step 4 (3 m).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting to double the half-angle.** Many students write $FoV_{h} = \arctan(w/2f)$ instead of $2 \cdot \arctan(w/2f)$. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Mixing up PPD formula.** $PPD = R_{h} / FoV_{h}$ — both quantities must be in *matching* units. Do **not** use $R_{h}$ in pixels and $FoV_{h}$ in radians. Always convert $FoV_{h}$ to degrees first. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Not commenting on the result.** A 3-mark deduction is typical if you compute numerically but do not state *whether* the device meets the 60 PPD threshold and *why* it matters (screen-door effect, reading acuity).

---

### Question B2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **(a)** [7 marks] Define the **Motion-to-Photon (M2P) latency**. Decompose it into its six pipeline stages with the typical time budget of each. Explain why exceeding **20 ms** causes **cybersickness** and what engineering techniques are used to mitigate this.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Compare the three primary classes of VR systems: **Non-Immersive, Semi-Immersive, and Fully Immersive** across five dimensions: hardware, FoV, tracking, cost, and a representative application. State one advantage and one limitation of Fully Immersive VR.

#### Model Answer for B2(a) — 7 marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Motion-to-Photon (M2P) latency is the elapsed time from the instant the user *physically initiates a motion* (typically a head turn) to the instant the *corresponding updated pixel* leaves the display panel and reaches the retina.

**Six-Stage Decomposition (3 marks):**

$$
t_{M2P} = t_{sense} + t_{track} + t_{render} + t_{encode} + t_{transmit} + t_{display}
$$

| Stage | Function | Typical Time |
|---|---|---|
| $t_{sense}$ | IMU samples head motion | ~1 ms |
| $t_{track}$ | Sensor fusion (IMU + optical) → 6-DoF pose | ~1 ms |
| $t_{render}$ | GPU rasterizes the new view | 8–11 ms |
| $t_{encode}$ | Frame is encoded for display link | ~1 ms |
| $t_{transmit}$ | DisplayPort / WiGig transport | < 1 ms |
| $t_{display}$ | Panel pixel response + scanout | 2–5 ms |

**Total ≈ 15–20 ms** at the current state of the art.

**Cybersickness Explanation (2 marks):** The human vestibular system (inner ear) detects head motion and reports it to the brain within **~7–15 ms** (VOR delay). If the visual system updates later than ~**20 ms** after the motion, the brain receives a *contradictory* signal: the inner ear says "I moved", but the eyes say "I haven't seen the consequence yet". This sensory conflict triggers nausea, disorientation, and headaches — collectively called **vection-induced cybersickness**.

**Mitigation Techniques (1 mark):**
- **Asynchronous Timewarp (ATW):** Reproject the previous frame with a rotational warp based on the *latest* pose if a new frame is not ready.
- **Asynchronous Spacewarp (ASW):** Synthesize intermediate frames using motion-vector extrapolation.
- **Predictive tracking:** Use the IMU's high-frequency sampling to *predict* the head pose at the display scanout time.
- **High refresh-rate panels (90–120 Hz):** Reduce the inter-frame interval, lowering the per-frame latency budget.

> **Mark Split:** Definition (1 m) + Decomposition table (3 m) + Cybersickness (2 m) + Mitigation (1 m).

#### Model Answer for B2(b) — 7 marks

| Dimension | **Non-Immersive** | **Semi-Immersive** | **Fully Immersive** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardware** | Desktop PC + monitor | Single-wall projection + shutter glasses | Opaque HMD + 6-DoF tracker |
| **Typical FoV** | ~30–40° (monitor) | ~60° (large screen) | ~90–130° (HMD) |
| **Tracking** | 3-DoF (mouse) | 3-DoF (head-tracked) | 6-DoF (head + hand + body) |
| **Cost (USD)** | $500–1500 | $2000–10000 | $500–3500 |
| **Application** | CAD design review | Flight simulator training | Surgical rehearsal, gaming |

**Advantage of Fully Immersive VR (0.5 m):** It provides the highest *presence* — the user feels *transported* into the synthetic world — by combining a wide FoV, 6-DoF tracking, spatial audio, and haptic feedback.

**Limitation of Fully Immersive VR (0.5 m):** It is *physically isolating* — the user cannot see the real world while wearing the HMD, posing safety risks in cluttered environments and limiting its use in collaborative, real-world-anchored CPS tasks (where AR/MR is more suitable).

> **Mark Split:** Comparison table (6 m for 5 dimensions × ~1.2 m each) + Advantage + Limitation (1 m).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for B2:**
> - **Vague definition of M2P.** Many students write "time for motion to be shown on screen" — too imprecise. The accepted formulation specifies *"from sensor sampling to photon emission from the display panel"*. **Loss: 0.5 mark.**
> - **Skipping the cybersickness mechanism.** Naming the threshold (20 ms) without explaining the *vestibular-visual conflict* is incomplete. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Comparison tables without numerical FoV values.** Vague descriptors like "wide FoV" do not earn full marks. KTU expects you to know ~90–130° for HMDs. **Loss: 0.5 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is your **last-3-hours-before-exam** checklist. Every bullet is a *guaranteed* KTU high-yield target.

### A. Core Definitions
- [ ] **VR** = 3-I triangle (Immersion, Interaction, Imagination) per Burdea & Coiffet.
- [ ] **VR vs. AR vs. MR vs. AV** — opaque HMD vs. transparent overlay vs. anchored interaction vs. virtual-with-real-objects.
- [ ] **Presence** = the psychological state of *being there* in the synthetic world.

### B. Hardware Components
- [ ] Five core components in canonical order: **Input → Output → Software → Computing Hardware → User**.
- [ ] **6-DoF** = 3 translations (X, Y, Z) + 3 rotations (pitch, yaw, roll).
- [ ] **HMD** = 2 displays (1 per eye) + 2 lenses + IMU + tracking cameras.
- [ ] **CAVE** = multi-wall projection room; *room-scale* alternative to HMDs.

### C. Key Engineering Metrics (must be memorized)
- [ ] **Interpupillary Distance (IPD)** = **63 ± 4 mm**.
- [ ] **Human horizontal binocular FoV** = **~200°**; **stereo overlap** = **~120°**.
- [ ] **Retina acuity** = **1 arc-minute = 60 PPD** threshold.
- [ ] **VR refresh rate** = **≥ 90 Hz** (frame budget = **11.11 ms** at 90 Hz).
- [ ] **M2P latency budget** = **< 20 ms**; vestibular delay = **7–15 ms**.

### D. Master Formulas (must be derived cold in 3 minutes)
- [ ] $FoV_{h} = 2 \cdot \arctan(w / 2f)$
- [ ] $PPD = R_{h} / FoV_{h}$
- [ ] $Z = d_{IPD} / (2 \tan(\theta/2))$
- [ ] $t_{M2P} = t_{sense} + t_{track} + t_{render} + t_{encode} + t_{transmit} + t_{display}$
- [ ] $t_{frame} = 1000 / f_{refresh}$

### E. Mitigations & Modern Techniques
- [ ] **Asynchronous Timewarp (ATW)** — rotational reprojection to hide missed frames.
- [ ] **Asynchronous Spacewarp (ASW)** — motion-vector synthesis of intermediate frames.
- [ ] **Foveated rendering** — high PPD in the fovea, low PPD in the periphery (eye-tracked).
- [ ] **OpenXR** — vendor-neutral VR runtime API by Khronos Group.
- [ ] **Digital twin** — the CPS-VR bridge concept (sync physical plant state into a VR visual model).

### F. History (for 3-mark questions)
- [ ] **1962 — Sensorama** (Heilig) — progenitor.
- [ ] **1968 — Sword of Damocles** (Sutherland) — first HMD.
- [ ] **1987 — VPL Research** (Lanier) — coined "Virtual Reality".
- [ ] **2012 — Oculus Rift Kickstarter** (Luckey) — modern consumer VR era.

### G. Examiner-Behaviour Traps to Avoid
- [ ] **Never** confuse FoV (degrees) with resolution (pixels) — they are linked *only through* PPD.
- [ ] **Never** confuse M2P latency with frame time — M2P is a *sensor-to-photon* metric, not a *frame-to-frame* metric.
- [ ] **Never** claim VR "replaces" reality in a literal sense — it *replaces sensory perception*, not physical existence.
- [ ] **Always** anchor your answer to the **CPS context** (OECPT824 is a CPS course, not a gaming course). Tying any answer to *telepresence, digital twin, or human-in-the-loop control* scores bonus marks.
