<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define Extended Reality (XR). List its three main sub-technologies and state one distinguishing feature of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Extended Reality (XR) is the collective term for all immersive technologies that merge the physical and virtual worlds, encompassing a spectrum from purely real to fully virtual experiences.
- **Sub-technologies (1.5 marks, 0.5 each):**
  - *Augmented Reality (AR):* Overlays digital content onto the real world; the user still sees physical surroundings.
  - *Virtual Reality (VR):* Completely replaces the real world with a synthetic 3D environment, typically via an opaque HMD.
  - *Mixed Reality (MR):* Anchors digital objects in the real world such that they interact with real geometry and lighting in real time.
- **Distinguishing feature (0.5 mark):** MR is distinguished from AR by **spatial anchoring and environmental interaction** of virtual objects (e.g., a virtual ball bouncing off a real table).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*What is "Motion-to-Photon Latency"? Why is the value 20 ms considered a critical threshold in VR systems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Motion-to-Photon Latency ($t_{m2p}$) is the total time delay between a user's physical head movement and the corresponding updated photons reaching the eye from the display.
- **Mathematical expression (1 mark):**

$$
t_{m2p} = t_{sensor} + t_{process} + t_{render} + t_{display}
$$

- **Why 20 ms is critical (1 mark):** The human vestibular system can detect motion latency above approximately **20 ms**. When $t_{m2p} > 20$ ms, the brain receives conflicting cues from the inner ear (motion sensed) and the eyes (no motion seen), causing **simulator sickness**, nausea, and disorientation. Hence, all VR systems for CPS must be engineered to keep $t_{m2p} \le 20$ ms.

---

### Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Read Before Writing!**
> - **Do NOT** confuse AR with VR in definitions — a single word error costs 2 marks.
> - **Always** mention the **Milgram Reality-Virtuality Continuum** when comparing AR/VR/MR; it is a 2-mark "must-have" in Part B answers.
> - **Always** state the **unit** of latency (milliseconds) and **unit** of FoV (degrees); unit errors are the most common 0.5-mark deduction in KTU valuation.
> - **Diagrams** (Milgram continuum, block diagram) carry 1–2 marks — *never skip them* in 14-mark questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Part B Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Question Paper Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of the **Milgram Reality-Virtuality Continuum**, explain in detail the differences between **Augmented Reality (AR)**, **Augmented Virtuality (AV)**, **Virtual Reality (VR)**, and **Mixed Reality (MR)**. Provide two real-world CPS use-cases for each technology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **complete VR rendering pipeline** from sensor data acquisition to photon emission. Compute the **Motion-to-Photon Latency** for a system with: IMU at 1000 Hz, Kalman filter at 0.5 ms, GPU running at 75 Hz, and display scan-out of 6 ms. Comment on whether the system meets the KTU 20 ms standard. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 — Introduce the Continuum (1 mark):*
The **Milgram Reality-Virtuality Continuum**, proposed in 1994, is a one-dimensional axis that classifies visual displays by the degree to which they replace the real world with virtual content. The left extreme is the **Real Environment (RE)** and the right extreme is the **Virtual Environment (VE)**.

*Step 2 — AR explanation + use-cases (1.5 marks):*
- **AR** sits close to the real end. It **superimposes** digital content on the user's live view of the real world using **optical see-through HMDs** or **smartphones**.
- **CPS Use-cases (any 2):**
  1. **Industrial maintenance:** Boeing technicians use AR glasses to visualize wiring inside aircraft fuselages, with overlays driven by the aircraft's onboard IoT sensors.
  2. **Surgical assistance:** Surgeons see real-time patient vitals and CT-scan slices overlaid on the patient during robotic surgery (e.g., Da Vinci system).

*Step 3 — AV explanation + use-cases (1 mark):*
- **AV** sits close to the virtual end but incorporates **real-world data** (e.g., a live camera feed) into an otherwise virtual scene.
- **CPS Use-cases:** Flight simulators with real cockpit camera feeds; remote telepresence in a digitally rendered reconstruction.

*Step 4 — VR explanation + use-cases (1.5 marks):*
- **VR** lies at the far right — the user is **fully immersed** in a synthetic 3D environment via an **opaque HMD**.
- **CPS Use-cases:**
  1. **Digital twin factory walkthroughs:** Engineers "walk" through a virtual replica of a smart factory, observing simulated IoT sensor streams.
  2. **Hazardous-environment training:** Nuclear plant operators train on a virtual replica of a reactor, learning emergency procedures without physical risk.

*Step 5 — MR explanation + use-cases (1 mark):*
- **MR** is the deepest integration — virtual objects are **spatially anchored** and obey real-world physics (occlusion, lighting, collision).
- **CPS Use-cases:** HoloLens 2 used in automotive design to place a full-scale virtual car on a real workshop floor, walk around it, and have it cast real shadows on the real ground.

*Step 6 — Diagram mention (1 mark):*
[Student is expected to draw the Milgram continuum axis with RE–AR–AV–MR–VE marked.]

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 — List the four pipeline stages (1.5 marks):*

$$
t_{m2p} = t_{sensor} + t_{process} + t_{render} + t_{display}
$$

*Step 2 — Compute each term (3 marks):*
- $t_{sensor} = \dfrac{1}{1000} = 1.0$ ms
- $t_{process} = 0.5$ ms (given)
- $t_{render} = \dfrac{1}{75} = 13.33$ ms
- $t_{display} = 6.0$ ms (given)

*Step 3 — Sum (1.5 marks):*

$$
t_{m2p} = 1.0 + 0.5 + 13.33 + 6.0 = 20.83\ \text{ms}
$$

*Step 4 — Conclude (1 mark):*
Since $20.83\ \text{ms} > 20\ \text{ms}$, the system **fails the KTU 20 ms standard** by 0.83 ms. To fix it, one could: (i) raise the GPU refresh rate to 90 Hz (saves ~1.78 ms), (ii) use a faster display panel, or (iii) apply **asynchronous timewarp (ATW)** to reduce perceived latency.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Part B Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **five core pillars** of any AR/VR system: **Tracking, Rendering, Display Optics, Input Modalities, and Networking**. For each pillar, identify one hardware component and one algorithmic technique used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VR HMD uses a microdisplay of width $w = 0.060$ m and a lens of focal length $f = 0.022$ m. Calculate the **horizontal Field of View (FoV)** in degrees. If the refresh rate is $f_{refresh} = 90$ Hz, compute the **frame budget** and the **total pixels per second** (assuming $2160 \times 2160$ per eye, two eyes). Comment on whether the **Pixel-Per-Degree (PPD)** meets the human retinal limit. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 — Tracking (1.5 marks):*
- **Hardware:** Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) — 3-axis gyroscope + 3-axis accelerometer.
- **Algorithm:** **Visual-Inertial SLAM (e.g., ORB-SLAM3)** — fuses camera frames with IMU data using an Extended Kalman Filter to estimate 6-DoF pose.

*Step 2 — Rendering (1.5 marks):*
- **Hardware:** Dedicated **GPU** (e.g., Adreno, Apple GPU, NVIDIA RTX).
- **Algorithm:** **Foveated Rendering** using **eye-tracking** input — reduces shading load in the periphery to save GPU power.

*Step 3 — Display Optics (1.5 marks):*
- **Hardware:** **Pancake lenses** (compact, fold the optical path) or **waveguides** (for AR).
- **Algorithm:** **Lens distortion correction** shader — pre-distorts the rendered image so that, after passing through the convex lens, the user perceives a geometrically correct image.

*Step 4 — Input Modalities (1.5 marks):*
- **Hardware:** **Hand-tracking cameras** (e.g., Quest 3's 4 IR cameras).
- **Algorithm:** **Hand pose estimation** using a convolutional neural network (e.g., Google's MediaPipe Hands) that infers 21 3D joint coordinates per hand.

*Step 5 — Networking (1 mark):*
- **Hardware:** **Wi-Fi 6E** or **5G** radio.
- **Algorithm:** **Edge computing offload** — heavy AI inference is done at a nearby edge server, not on the HMD, to reduce $t_{process}$.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 — FoV formula (1 mark):*

$$
\text{FoV}_h = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2f}\right)
$$

*Step 2 — Substitute and solve (1.5 marks):*

$$
\text{FoV}_h = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{0.060}{2 \cdot 0.022}\right) = 2 \cdot \arctan(1.3636)
$$

$$
\text{FoV}_h = 2 \cdot 53.71\degree = 107.42\degree
$$

*Step 3 — Frame budget (1 mark):*

$$
t_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{90} = 11.11\ \text{ms}
$$

*Step 4 — Total pixels per second (1.5 marks):*

$$
P_{total} = 2 \cdot 2160 \cdot 2160 \cdot 90
$$

$$
P_{total} = 2 \cdot 4\,665\,600 \cdot 90 = 839\,808\,000\ \text{pixels/sec} \approx 8.4 \times 10^{8}\ \text{px/s}
$$

*Step 5 — PPD check (2 marks):*

$$
\text{PPD} = \dfrac{2160}{107.42} \approx 20.1\ \text{pixels/degree}
$$

Since the human retina's resolving power is **$\approx 60$ PPD**, this system falls **far short of retinal resolution**. To reach retinal limit, the display would need $\approx 60 \cdot 107.42 \approx 6445$ horizontal pixels per eye, which is the goal of next-generation "Retina VR" displays.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this 12-point checklist the night before the exam — it covers 90% of marks.**

- **AR = Add to Reality; VR = Replace Reality; MR = Anchored to Reality; XR = Umbrella.**
- **Milgram Continuum** order: **RE $\rightarrow$ AR $\rightarrow$ AV $\rightarrow$ MR $\rightarrow$ VE**.
- **Motion-to-Photon Latency** must be **$\le 20$ ms**; formula: $t_{m2p} = t_{sensor} + t_{process} + t_{render} + t_{display}$.
- **Standard refresh rate** for modern VR: **90 Hz** (frame budget **11.11 ms**).
- **Human interocular distance** $I \approx 0.064$ m — used in stereoscopic disparity formula.
- **Human retinal limit** $\approx$ **60 pixels/degree (PPD)**; most current HMDs are around **20 PPD**.
- **Field of View formula:** $\text{FoV}_h = 2 \cdot \arctan(w / 2f)$ — remember the factor of 2.
- **SLAM** = **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping** — the heart of inside-out tracking.
- **Five pillars** of AR/VR systems: **Tracking, Rendering, Display Optics, Input, Networking**.
- **CPS layers** in any XR system: **Physical (sensors/actuators) $\leftrightarrow$ Cyber (AI/digital twin) $\leftrightarrow$ HMI (AR/VR)**.
- **Pin-hole model**, **Euler rotation matrices**, and **quaternion multiplication** are the three rotation math tools — at least one appears in every 14-mark question.
- **Common real-world CPS examples:** HoloLens (maintenance), Da Vinci (surgery), digital twin (manufacturing), AR-HUD (automotive), VR simulator (training).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition and characteristics of CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT824 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition and characteristics of CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an integrated computational and physical process system in which embedded computers and networks monitor, coordinate, control, and integrate the behavior of physical processes. The system couples the **cyber world** (computation, communication, control algorithms) with the **physical world** (sensors, actuators, mechanical/biological/chemical processes) through a continuous feedback loop.

Mathematically, a CPS can be represented as a coupled dynamical system:

$$
\mathcal{CPS} \;=\; (\mathcal{P},\, \mathcal{C},\, \mathcal{N},\, \mathcal{S},\, \mathcal{A})
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{P}$ = Physical process dynamics
- $\mathcal{C}$ = Cyber/computational core
- $\mathcal{N}$ = Network/communication layer
- $\mathcal{S}$ = Set of sensors
- $\mathcal{A}$ = Set of actuators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The *cyber* and *physical* components in a CPS are **tightly integrated, deeply intertwined, and co-designed** — they are not merely interfaced. This tight coupling is the defining trait differentiating CPS from classical embedded systems or stand-alone IoT deployments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Modern Smart Car**
>
> Imagine a car cruising on a highway. The *physical* side is the engine, brakes, wheels, road, and the car body. The *cyber* side is the onboard computer receiving data from radar, LiDAR, and cameras. The computer *decides* (cyber decision) to apply the brake, which then *physically* slows the car, whose new state is sensed again — and the loop repeats **thousands of times per second**.
>
> This seamless, real-time dance between sensing → computing → acting → sensing is the essence of a CPS.

Another everyday analogy is a **smart home thermostat**:
- **Sensor** detects room temperature (physical) →
- **Microcontroller** (cyber) compares it to setpoint →
- **Actuator** turns the compressor on/off (physical) →
- The room temperature changes, and the loop continues.

## 1.3 Why CPS is Distinct from IoT, Embedded Systems, and M2M

| Aspect | Embedded System | IoT | CPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Focus | Single-device control | Connectivity of things | Bidirectional, real-time control & feedback |
| Feedback Loop | Often open | Primarily data collection | **Closed-loop, real-time** |
| Computation | Local | Often cloud-based | Distributed, edge + cloud |
| Time-Criticality | Moderate | Low–moderate | **High (often hard real-time)** |
| Interaction with Physics | Indirect | Mostly sensing | **Direct actuation & control** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **3C's — Computation, Communication, and Control** — combined with **continuous feedback** form the structural backbone of any CPS.

## 1.4 Key Standardized Metrics / Conventions

- **Hard real-time deadline** $\tau_{d}$: must be met deterministically; violation = system failure.
- **Soft real-time deadline**: violation degrades quality but is tolerable.
- **Sampling period** $T_{s}$: rate at which the cyber layer samples the physical process.
- **Latency** $\mathcal{L}$: total delay from sensor event to actuator response.
- **Jitter** $J_{t}$: variance in latency, often critical in control loops.

> [!NOTE]
> A CPS that violates timing constraints is functionally *unsafe*, even if logically *correct*. This is the single most exam-relevant distinction of CPS over general computing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars / Core Characteristics of CPS

A CPS is characterized by the following tightly integrated properties — these are **high-yield for KTU**:

### (a) **Cyber-Physical Coupling**
The cyber and physical subsystems influence each other through sensors and actuators in a closed feedback loop. The state of one *directly* changes the state of the other.

### (b) **Real-Time Responsiveness**
CPS must react to physical events within bounded time intervals. The system's correctness depends not only on *what* the output is, but also *when* it is produced.

### (c) **Heterogeneity**
CPS integrates components of different domains — mechanical, electrical, software, communication, and chemical — each with its own model of operation.

### (d) **Distributed & Networked Architecture**
The computational nodes are spatially distributed and connected via wired/wireless networks. Distributed consensus and synchronization are central concerns.

### (e) **Autonomy & Intelligence**
Modern CPS incorporates learning, adaptation, and self-optimization, often using AI/ML models deployed at the edge.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — How a CPS Functions

The operational cycle of a CPS can be decomposed into the following six stages:

1. **Sensing** — Sensors transduce physical phenomena (temperature, force, position) into electrical signals.
2. **Data Acquisition** — Signals are conditioned, sampled, and digitized at rate $f_{s} = 1/T_{s}$.
3. **Computation** — Embedded processors run control algorithms, state estimation, and decision logic.
4. **Communication** — Results are transmitted across the network (CAN, EtherCAT, 5G, etc.) with bounded latency $\mathcal{L}$.
5. **Actuation** — Actuators convert cyber commands into physical changes (motor torque, valve position).
6. **Feedback** — The new physical state is sensed again, closing the loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The loop period $T_{loop} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{comm} + T_{actuate}$ must satisfy $T_{loop} \leq \tau_{d}$ for a **hard real-time** CPS.

## 2.3 Mathematical Modeling Preliminaries

A CPS is typically modeled as a **hybrid system** — a combination of:
- **Continuous dynamics** (the physical process) — described by differential equations.
- **Discrete dynamics** (the cyber/computational decisions) — described by finite-state machines or automata.

### Continuous Physical Subsystem

$$
\dot{x}_{p}(t) = A_{p}\, x_{p}(t) + B_{p}\, u(t) + w_{p}(t)
$$

$$
y_{p}(t) = C_{p}\, x_{p}(t)
$$

where $x_{p}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the physical state, $u(t)$ is the control input (from cyber), $y_{p}(t)$ is the sensor output, and $w_{p}(t)$ is process noise.

### Discrete Cyber Subsystem

$$
x_{c}[k+1] = f_{c}\bigl(x_{c}[k],\, y_{p}[k]\bigr)
$$

$$
u[k] = g_{c}\bigl(x_{c}[k],\, y_{p}[k]\bigr)
$$

The discrete-to-continuous interface is governed by a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**:

$$
u(t) = u[k] \quad \text{for} \quad kT_{s} \leq t < (k+1)T_{s}
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Description | Key Parameter / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Loop period | $T_{loop} = T_{s} + T_{c} + T_{net} + T_{a}$ | seconds (s) |
| 2 | Sampling frequency | $f_{s} = \frac{1}{T_{s}}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 3 | Nyquist criterion | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz |
| 4 | Continuous physical dynamics | $\dot{x}_{p} = A_{p} x_{p} + B_{p} u + w_{p}$ | state $x_{p} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| 5 | Sensor output | $y_{p} = C_{p} x_{p} + v$ | measurement $y_{p}$ |
| 6 | Discrete cyber update | $x_{c}[k+1] = f_{c}(x_{c}[k], y_{p}[k])$ | state $x_{c} \in \mathbb{Z}^{m}$ |
| 7 | Hard real-time constraint | $T_{loop} \leq \tau_{d}$ | $\tau_{d}$ = deadline |
| 8 | Latency budget | $\mathcal{L} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{comm} + T_{actuate}$ | s |
| 9 | Stability condition (discrete) | $\vert \lambda_{i}(A_{cl}) \vert < 1 \; \forall i$ | eigenvalues of closed-loop $A_{cl}$ |
| 10 | CPS tuple | $\mathcal{CPS} = (\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{N}, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A})$ | structural definition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the foundation of every safety-critical CPS — autonomous vehicles, pacemakers, robotic surgery, smart grids, and AR-assisted industrial automation. KTU examiners often test *which* equation governs *which* subsystem, so memorize the discrete vs. continuous split.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

CPS is the enabling technology of:
- **Industry 4.0** (smart factories with collaborative robots).
- **Autonomous Vehicles** (sensor fusion + real-time planning).
- **Smart Grids** (real-time load balancing).
- **Healthcare** (closed-loop drug delivery, robotic surgery).
- **AR/VR in CPS** (overlaying real-time sensor data onto the physical operator's field of view — directly the focus of this OECPT824 course).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Discretization of Continuous Physical Dynamics (ZOH Equivalent)

The continuous physical subsystem

$$
\dot{x}_{p}(t) = A_{p} x_{p}(t) + B_{p} u(t)
$$

is interfaced with the discrete cyber layer through a Zero-Order Hold. We derive the **discrete-time equivalent** sampled at period $T_{s}$.

### Step 1 — Solution of the Continuous ODE

The exact solution of a linear ODE is:

$$
x_{p}(t) = e^{A_{p}(t - t_{0})} x_{p}(t_{0}) + \int_{t_{0}}^{t} e^{A_{p}(t - \tau)} B_{p} u(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

### Step 2 — Apply ZOH Assumption

Under ZOH, the input is constant over the interval $kT_{s} \leq t < (k+1)T_{s}$, i.e., $u(\tau) = u[k]$.

$$
x_{p}\bigl((k+1)T_{s}\bigr) = e^{A_{p} T_{s}} x_{p}(kT_{s}) + \int_{kT_{s}}^{(k+1)T_{s}} e^{A_{p}((k+1)T_{s} - \tau)} B_{p}\, d\tau \cdot u[k]
$$

### Step 3 — Variable Substitution

Let $\sigma = (k+1)T_{s} - \tau$, so $d\sigma = -d\tau$. When $\tau = kT_{s}$, $\sigma = T_{s}$; when $\tau = (k+1)T_{s}$, $\sigma = 0$.

$$
x_{p}[k+1] = e^{A_{p} T_{s}} x_{p}[k] + \left(\int_{0}^{T_{s}} e^{A_{p} \sigma}\, d\sigma \right) B_{p}\, u[k]
$$

### Step 4 — Define the Discrete Matrices

Define the discrete-time system matrices:

$$
A_{d} = e^{A_{p} T_{s}}
$$

$$
B_{d} = \int_{0}^{T_{s}} e^{A_{p} \sigma}\, d\sigma \cdot B_{p}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Discrete-Time Representation

$$
x_{p}[k+1] = A_{d}\, x_{p}[k] + B_{d}\, u[k]
$$

$$
y_{p}[k] = C_{p}\, x_{p}[k]
$$

This is the **canonical discrete-time state-space model** used throughout CPS analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stability Constraint for Closed-Loop CPS

Consider the **closed-loop CPS** with state feedback controller $u[k] = -K x_{p}[k]$, where $K \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times n}$ is the feedback gain matrix.

### Step 1 — Substitute Controller into the Discrete Dynamics

$$
x_{p}[k+1] = A_{d} x_{p}[k] + B_{d} (-K x_{p}[k])
$$

### Step 2 — Collect the State Terms

$$
x_{p}[k+1] = (A_{d} - B_{d} K) x_{p}[k]
$$

### Step 3 — Define the Closed-Loop Matrix

$$
A_{cl} = A_{d} - B_{d} K
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Schur Stability Criterion

A discrete-time LTI system is asymptotically stable if and only if all eigenvalues of $A_{cl}$ lie strictly inside the unit circle in the complex plane:

$$
\vert \lambda_{i}(A_{cl}) \vert < 1 \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

This is the **Schur stability condition** — directly testable and high-yield for KTU.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — DC Motor as a CPS

A DC motor is a canonical CPS example. Its physical dynamics are:

$$
\frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} = -\frac{b}{J}\, \omega(t) + \frac{K_{m}}{J}\, u(t)
$$

$$
y(t) = \omega(t)
$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity, $J$ is moment of inertia, $b$ is viscous friction, $K_{m}$ is motor constant, and $u$ is the applied voltage (the **cyber** command).

### Identification with the Standard CPS Form

$$
A_{p} = -\frac{b}{J}, \quad B_{p} = \frac{K_{m}}{J}, \quad C_{p} = 1
$$

### Discrete Equivalent at Sampling Period $T_{s}$

$$
A_{d} = e^{-b T_{s} / J}, \quad B_{d} = \frac{K_{m}}{b}\bigl(1 - e^{-b T_{s} / J}\bigr)
$$

### Stability of Open-Loop

$$
\vert A_{d} \vert = e^{-b T_{s} / J} < 1 \quad \text{(always, for positive } b, J, T_{s}\text{)}
$$

Hence the open-loop system is *naturally stable* — a property exploited in elementary motor control.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping CPS Subsystems to Models

| Subsystem | Domain | Model Type | Governing Equation | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Physical process | Continuous | ODE / PDE | $\dot{x}_{p} = A_{p} x_{p} + B_{p} u$ | Motor, robot arm |
| Sensors | Continuous → discrete | Sampling + quantization | $y_{p}[k] = C_{p} x_{p}[k] + v[k]$ | Encoder, IMU |
| Cyber controller | Discrete | State machine / difference equation | $x_{c}[k+1] = f_{c}(x_{c}[k], y_{p}[k])$ | Microcontroller, PLC |
| Network | Discrete | Queueing / delay model | $\mathcal{L} = $ deterministic + stochastic | CAN, EtherCAT, 5G |
| Actuator | Discrete → continuous | ZOH + dynamics | $u(t) = u[k], \; kT_{s} \leq t < (k+1)T_{s}$ | Motor driver, valve |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Cyber-Physical System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the canonical closed-loop CPS architecture with the five sub-blocks defined in Section 1.1. Note the bidirectional, closed-loop nature — the defining trait of any CPS.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["PHYSICAL PROCESS x_p_dot = A_p x_p + B_p u"]
    nodeS["SENSORS y_p = C_p x_p + v"]
    nodeA["ACTUATORS u = g_c x_c y_p"]
    nodeC["CYBER COMPUTATION x_c k plus 1 = f_c x_c k y_p k"]
    nodeN["NETWORK Communication Layer"]

    nodeP -- "Continuous physical state" --> nodeS
    nodeS -- "Sampled measurement y_p k" --> nodeN
    nodeN -- "Bounded latency L" --> nodeC
    nodeC -- "Control command u k" --> nodeN
    nodeN -- "Delivered command" --> nodeA
    nodeA -- "Physical input force torque voltage" --> nodeP
```

## 4.2 The 5C Architecture (Industry-Standard CPS Stack)

The 5C architecture is the de-facto industry standard for CPS deployment, especially in manufacturing and Industry 4.0. It maps directly to the AR/VR-CPS integration theme of this course.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeC1["LEVEL 1 SMART CONNECTION Acquisition of reliable data from sensors and machines"]
    nodeC2["LEVEL 2 DATA TO INFORMATION Meaningful information from raw data"]
    nodeC3["LEVEL 3 CYBER NETWORK Information network across machines"]
    nodeC4["LEVEL 4 COGNITION Comprehend the network status and act on knowledge"]
    nodeC5["LEVEL 5 CONFIGURATION Feedback to physical world for self-configuration"]

    nodeC1 --> nodeC2
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3
    nodeC3 --> nodeC4
    nodeC4 --> nodeC5
    nodeC5 -. "Feedback Loop" .-> nodeC1
```

## 4.3 CPS Integration with AR/VR (Course-Specific Context)

For OECPT824, the most relevant extension is how AR/VR acts as a *human-in-the-loop* cognitive layer atop the standard CPS stack.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP2["PHYSICAL WORLD Machines Environment Operators"]
    nodeS2["SENSING LAYER IoT Sensors Cameras IMU"]
    nodeC2A["CYBER LAYER Edge Cloud AI Control"]
    nodeAR["AR VR COGNITIVE INTERFACE HMD Spatial Mapping Haptics"]
    nodeHUM["HUMAN OPERATOR Decision Action"]

    nodeP2 --> nodeS2
    nodeS2 --> nodeC2A
    nodeC2A --> nodeAR
    nodeAR --> nodeHUM
    nodeHUM -. "Actuation Command" .-> nodeP2
    nodeAR -. "Real Time Guidance" .-> nodeHUM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The AR/VR module sits between the cyber computation and the human operator, translating abstract numerical state into spatial, intuitive visualizations. This is precisely the *configuration* layer of the 5C architecture, enriched with human-in-the-loop cognition.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Cyber-Physical System. List any four of its key characteristics. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
A Cyber-Physical System (CPS) is an engineered system in which computational algorithms (cyber) and physical processes are tightly integrated through sensors and actuators, forming a closed feedback loop. The cyber layer monitors and controls the physical layer in real time.

**Four Key Characteristics (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Cyber-Physical Coupling** — bidirectional interaction between computation and physical process.
2. **Real-Time Responsiveness** — bounded response time, often hard real-time.
3. **Heterogeneity** — integration of mechanical, electronic, software, and communication components.
4. **Distributed & Networked Architecture** — spatially distributed computational nodes connected via a network.

*(Full 3 marks only if definition is precise AND exactly four characteristics are named.)*

### Q2. Differentiate between an Embedded System and a Cyber-Physical System with a suitable example. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Embedded System | Cyber-Physical System |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback** | Often open-loop or absent | **Closed-loop, mandatory** |
| **Real-time** | May or may not be | **Strictly required** |
| **Actuation** | Optional | **Central role** |
| **Network** | Optional | **Integral to architecture** |
| **Example** | Washing machine controller | **Autonomous vehicle, pacemaker, smart grid** |

**Conclusion (essential for full marks):** *The key distinction is that CPS is defined by its **closed-loop interaction with physical processes under hard real-time constraints**, while an embedded system may be a self-contained controller without continuous physical coupling.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

> `(a)` Define a Cyber-Physical System with the help of a neat block diagram. Discuss any five core characteristics of CPS in detail. `(7 Marks)`
>
> `(b)` A continuous-time physical subsystem is given by $\dot{x}_{p}(t) = -2\, x_{p}(t) + 4\, u(t)$, with $y_{p}(t) = x_{p}(t)$. Compute the discrete-time equivalent under a Zero-Order Hold with sampling period $T_{s} = 0.1\,\text{s}$. Also determine whether the open-loop system is stable. `(7 Marks)`

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition (2 Marks):**
A CPS is a tightly coupled integration of computational (cyber) algorithms with physical processes through a feedback loop of sensors, communication networks, and actuators, designed to monitor, control, and adapt the physical behavior in real time.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** Refer to the Functional Architecture diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The diagram should show:
- Physical process block
- Sensor block
- Network block
- Cyber computation block
- Actuator block
- Closed-loop arrows on both sides.

**Five Core Characteristics (3 Marks — 0.6 each):**
1. **Cyber-Physical Coupling** — the cyber and physical layers share a continuous, bidirectional feedback loop.
2. **Real-Time Operation** — bounded response time, often with hard deadlines.
3. **Heterogeneity** — multiple engineering domains (mechanical, electrical, software, control) coexist.
4. **Distributed Computation** — spatially distributed nodes with network-level coordination.
5. **Autonomy / Intelligence** — capability to learn, adapt, and self-configure.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify the Continuous Matrices (1 Mark):**

$$
A_{p} = -2, \quad B_{p} = 4, \quad C_{p} = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Discrete $A_{d}$ Matrix (2 Marks):**

$$
A_{d} = e^{A_{p} T_{s}} = e^{-2 \times 0.1} = e^{-0.2}
$$

$$
A_{d} \approx 0.8187
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Discrete $B_{d}$ Matrix (2 Marks):**

For a scalar system, the integral simplifies to:

$$
B_{d} = \int_{0}^{T_{s}} e^{A_{p} \sigma}\, d\sigma \cdot B_{p} = \frac{e^{A_{p} T_{s}} - 1}{A_{p}} \cdot B_{p}
$$

$$
B_{d} = \frac{e^{-0.2} - 1}{-2} \times 4 = \frac{0.8187 - 1}{-2} \times 4 = \frac{-0.1813}{-2} \times 4
$$

$$
B_{d} \approx 0.0907 \times 4 \approx 0.3626
$$

**Step 4 — Write the Discrete Model (1 Mark):**

$$
x_{p}[k+1] = 0.8187\, x_{p}[k] + 0.3626\, u[k]
$$

$$
y_{p}[k] = x_{p}[k]
$$

**Step 5 — Open-Loop Stability Test (1 Mark):**

The Schur stability condition requires $\vert A_{d} \vert < 1$:

$$
\vert 0.8187 \vert = 0.8187 < 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The open-loop discrete-time system is **asymptotically stable**.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

> `(a)` With the help of the 5C architecture diagram, explain how a Cyber-Physical System is structured in modern Industry 4.0 deployments. Highlight the role of each of the five layers. `(7 Marks)`
>
> `(b)` A temperature-control CPS samples a room once every $T_{s} = 0.5\,\text{s}$. The room's continuous dynamics are $\dot{T}(t) = -0.1\, T(t) + 0.05\, u(t)$, where $u(t)$ is the heater input (in volts) and $T$ is the deviation from setpoint. Using a state-feedback controller $u[k] = -K\, T[k]$ with $K = 2$, determine whether the closed-loop discrete-time system is asymptotically stable. `(7 Marks)`

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**5C Architecture (refer to diagram in Section 4.2) (5 Layers × 1.2 Marks Each = 6 Marks):**

1. **Smart Connection (Level 1)** — Acquires accurate and reliable data from sensors and machines. Handles data acquisition, signal conditioning, timestamping, and noise filtering.
2. **Data-to-Information (Level 2)** — Transforms raw sensor data into meaningful information using feature extraction, signal processing, and statistical summarization.
3. **Cyber Network (Level 3)** — Connects the information from individual machines into a network-wide data plane, enabling system-level analytics.
4. **Cognition (Level 4)** — Interprets the network state using machine learning and predictive analytics to generate actionable knowledge.
5. **Configuration (Level 5)** — Feeds back the cognitive decisions into the physical system as control commands, completing the loop and enabling self-configuration.

**Industry 4.0 Context (1 Mark):** In Industry 4.0, the 5C architecture is implemented using OPC-UA, MQTT, edge computing, and digital twin platforms, allowing real-time optimization of manufacturing lines.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify Continuous Matrices (1 Mark):**

$$
A_{p} = -0.1, \quad B_{p} = 0.05, \quad C_{p} = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Discretize with $T_{s} = 0.5$ s (2 Marks):**

$$
A_{d} = e^{-0.1 \times 0.5} = e^{-0.05} \approx 0.9512
$$

$$
B_{d} = \frac{A_{d} - 1}{A_{p}} \cdot B_{p} = \frac{0.9512 - 1}{-0.1} \times 0.05 = \frac{-0.0488}{-0.1} \times 0.05
$$

$$
B_{d} \approx 0.4880 \times 0.05 \approx 0.0244
$$

**Step 3 — Form the Closed-Loop Matrix (2 Marks):**

$$
A_{cl} = A_{d} - B_{d} K = 0.9512 - (0.0244)(2) = 0.9512 - 0.0488
$$

$$
A_{cl} = 0.9024
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Schur Stability Criterion (1 Mark):**

$$
\vert A_{cl} \vert = 0.9024 < 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The closed-loop discrete-time system is **asymptotically stable**. Physically, this means the room temperature will converge to the setpoint without oscillation or divergence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing ZOH discretization with Euler approximation.** Euler is $A_{d} \approx I + A_{p} T_{s}$; ZOH is $A_{d} = e^{A_{p} T_{s}}$. Using Euler in a 7-mark question yields a *wrong* answer — full marks lost.
> 2. **Forgetting the stability condition** $\vert \lambda_{i} \vert < 1$. Many students compute $A_{cl}$ correctly but skip the final Schur check — losing 1 mark minimum.
> 3. **Definition marks (2 in Question A part a) require the word "closed-loop" and "real-time".** Omitting either caps marks at 1.
> 4. **In 5C architecture, the order must be Connection → Information → Network → Cognition → Configuration.** Out-of-order sequences lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Block diagrams without arrows in *both* directions are considered "open-loop" and rejected for full marks** — the *bidirectional* arrow is the visual proof of CPS.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **CPS = Cyber + Physical, tightly coupled, with closed-loop feedback.**
- The defining trio: **Computation + Communication + Control** (the 3C's).
- A CPS is **time-critical** — correctness depends on *when* an output is produced, not just *what* it is.
- **Five core characteristics:** cyber-physical coupling, real-time responsiveness, heterogeneity, distributed architecture, autonomy.
- A CPS is mathematically a **hybrid system** = continuous dynamics (ODE) + discrete dynamics (difference equations).
- **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** is the standard interface from discrete cyber to continuous physical: $u(t) = u[k]$ for $kT_{s} \leq t < (k+1)T_{s}$.
- Discretization formulas:
  - $A_{d} = e^{A_{p} T_{s}}$
  - $B_{d} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T_{s}} e^{A_{p} \sigma}\, d\sigma \cdot B_{p}$
  - For scalar $A_{p}$: $B_{d} = \dfrac{e^{A_{p} T_{s}} - 1}{A_{p}} \cdot B_{p}$
- **Schur stability:** $\vert \lambda_{i}(A_{cl}) \vert < 1$ for all eigenvalues.
- **5C Architecture** (memorize the order): Connection → Data-to-Information → Cyber Network → Cognition → Configuration.
- **CPS ≠ IoT:** IoT emphasizes *data collection*; CPS emphasizes *real-time control and feedback*.
- **CPS ≠ Embedded System:** Embedded may be a single device; CPS is a *system of systems* with networking and feedback.
- For this course (OECPT824), the **AR/VR module acts as the Configuration / Cognition layer**, augmenting the human operator with real-time spatial information.
- **Hard real-time constraint:** $T_{loop} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{comm} + T_{actuate} \leq \tau_{d}$ — non-negotiable in safety-critical CPS.
- **Common KTU words to include in answers:** *closed-loop, hard real-time, feedback, sensor-actuator coupling, co-design, hybrid system, time-triggered, deterministic.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** are engineered systems that are built from, and depend upon, the seamless integration of computational algorithms and physical components. A CPS is a network of physical and computational (cyber) elements that interact in a continuous feedback loop, where sensing, computation, actuation, and communication are tightly coupled to influence real-world processes.

**Augmented Reality (AR)** is a human–computer interaction paradigm that overlays computer-generated virtual objects (text, graphics, audio, haptics) onto the real-world environment of the user in real time, while still allowing the user to perceive and interact with the actual physical surroundings.

**Virtual Reality (VR)** is an immersive, fully synthetic, computer-generated three-dimensional environment that replaces the user's real-world sensory input through dedicated display hardware (HMDs, CAVEs) and input devices, enabling the user to interact with simulated objects and scenarios as if they were real.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the **Application of AR/VR in CPS** refers to the deployment of these immersive technologies as a visualization, interaction, and control layer that bridges the cyber (computational) and physical (actuator/sensor) domains of a CPS, allowing operators, engineers, and end-users to perceive, supervise, train for, and manipulate the physical processes that the CPS governs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> In Module 1, applications are studied *after* the foundational concepts of AR/VR and CPS. Examiners expect students to *map* AR/VR capabilities (overlay, immersion, real-time 3D, spatial interaction) onto specific CPS functional needs (monitoring, control, teleoperation, training). Generic definitions of AR/VR alone do NOT fetch marks — the CPS linkage is mandatory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a power-plant control room. In a traditional setup, an engineer stares at **2D SCADA dashboards** — numbers, graphs, alarms — and must mentally reconstruct what is happening inside a furnace, turbine, or pipeline that he or she cannot see directly. This mental reconstruction is slow, error-prone, and fatiguing.

Now imagine the same engineer wearing an **AR headset**. As he turns his head, he sees the *real* furnace *through* the glass, but floating on the glass — precisely registered to physical coordinates — are the *live* temperature contour maps, pressure gradients, and predicted failure zones pulled live from the CPS's cyber layer. He can wave his hand and *virtually* open a valve to preview its effect on downstream flow *before* issuing the real actuation command.

That overlay is the **AR-in-CPS** paradigm: the real physical process is not replaced; it is *augmented* with cyber-domain intelligence.

Now imagine the engineer instead needs to be trained to handle a toxic-gas leak that has never actually occurred. A full-scale physical drill is impossible. So the engineer is placed in a **VR headset** in a conference room, surrounded by a 1:1 virtual replica of the plant (a *digital twin* of the CPS), and is exposed to a realistic leak scenario. His hand movements, his walking, his valve-turning gestures are tracked and fed back into the simulation, which responds with realistic physics.

That full synthetic immersion is the **VR-in-CPS** paradigm: the real physical process does not yet exist for the user — only its high-fidelity cyber replica does.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams**
> - **AR + CPS** ⇒ *Augment the real physical process with cyber insights* (operator remains in the loop, in the real world).
> - **VR + CPS** ⇒ *Replace the real physical process with a cyber twin for training/simulation* (operator is removed from the real world into a synthetic one).
> - The unifying role of AR/VR in CPS is to act as the **Human–Machine Interface (HMI)** layer that translates abstract cyber data into spatially meaningful, embodied perception.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical/Engineering Constants

The following standard performance metrics are universally used to characterize AR/VR subsystems when they are deployed inside a CPS pipeline:

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Target Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| End-to-end motion-to-photon latency | $\tau_{m2p}$ | **< 20 ms** (VR), **< 50 ms** (AR) | Prevents motion sickness and enables closed-loop CPS control |
| Frame rate | $f_{fps}$ | **60 – 120 Hz** | Determines visual smoothness; tied to display refresh |
| Tracking accuracy (positional) | $\epsilon_{pos}$ | **< 1 mm** (indoor) | Required for precise overlay registration in industrial AR |
| Tracking accuracy (rotational) | $\epsilon_{rot}$ | **< 0.1°** | Required for stable hologram anchoring |
| Field of View | $\Phi_{FoV}$ | **90° – 110°** (consumer), **> 180°** (CAVE) | Determines immersive coverage |
| Inter-pupillary distance range | $d_{IPD}$ | **51 mm – 77 mm** | Critical for ergonomic fit, prevents eye strain |
| Degrees of Freedom (tracking) | $n_{DoF}$ | **3-DoF** or **6-DoF** | 6-DoF = position + orientation |
| Display resolution per eye | $R_{eye}$ | **> 2K per eye** | Avoids screen-door effect |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Fact**
> The latency bound of **$\tau_{m2p} < 20\text{ ms}$** is the most commonly tested constant in VR-for-CPS problems because it directly determines whether the closed control loop between the human, the cyber model, and the physical actuator remains stable.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AR/VR-in-CPS Operational Loop — Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

A CPS with an AR/VR layer operates as a **closed feedback loop** in which the human operator becomes an integrated node. The following seven logical steps describe a canonical interaction:

1. **Physical Sensing (CPS Physical Layer)**
   Physical sensors (temperature, pressure, IMU, LiDAR, cameras, accelerometers) transduce the state of the real-world plant into electrical signals. The sampling rate $f_s$ must obey the Nyquist criterion relative to the highest physical frequency present in the process.

2. **Cyber Aggregation (CPS Cyber Layer)**
   Sensor data is fused via a state estimator (commonly a Kalman filter) to produce a single coherent system state vector $\mathbf{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$. This is the digital twin of the physical process at time $t$.

3. **AR/VR Rendering Preparation**
   The state vector $\mathbf{x}_t$ is mapped onto the AR/VR scene graph. Virtual objects (labels, 3D models, alerts, predicted trajectories) are positioned in the world coordinate frame that is co-registered with the physical environment.

4. **Pose Tracking of the User**
   The user's head and hand poses are tracked at high frequency (e.g., 90 Hz inside-out tracking) to determine what the user is looking at and what they intend to manipulate.

5. **Immersive Display**
   Stereo images (one per eye) are computed and displayed. The pixel value $P(u,v,t)$ is the result of projecting a 3D world point $\mathbf{p}_w$ through the user's current camera matrix $\mathbf{K}_t$ and the device pose $\mathbf{T}_t$.

6. **Human Decision / Action**
   The user perceives the augmented or virtual scene and decides on an action (e.g., press a virtual button, issue a robot command). Gesture/voice/signal is captured.

7. **Actuation (Closing the Loop)**
   The captured human intent is converted into an actuation command and sent to the physical layer, altering the real process. The cycle repeats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exam answers**
> Marks are awarded for demonstrating that AR/VR does **not** act as a standalone gimmick inside a CPS — it sits as a **visualization–interaction sublayer** between the cyber decision logic and the human, and its parameters (latency, accuracy, frame rate) directly govern the stability of the overall CPS feedback loop.

## 2.2 Functional Role Classification of AR/VR inside a CPS

AR/VR performs **four** distinct functional roles inside a CPS. Examiners often test a student's ability to classify an application correctly into one of these roles.

| Functional Role | Sub-system Purpose | AR or VR? | Example CPS Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Supervisory Visualization** | Display CPS state to operator | Both | Smart-grid substation monitoring with AR overlays |
| **Telepresence / Teleoperation** | Remotely control a physical CPS actuator | Both | Surgeon operating a remote robotic surgery CPS |
| **Training & Simulation** | Train humans on a CPS replica | Predominantly VR | Nuclear-plant emergency drill in VR digital twin |
| **Maintenance & Assembly Guidance** | Guide a human in real-time on a physical CPS | Predominantly AR | AR-guided engine assembly on a smart factory line |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Because this is a CS/CPS subject, the following equations are the **most frequently examined** mathematical relationships involving AR/VR inside a CPS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vertical bar character has been replaced with `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau_{m2p} \;=\; \tau_{sense} + \tau_{compute} + \tau_{render} + \tau_{display}$ | Total motion-to-photon latency decomposition | $\tau_{m2p} < 20\text{ ms}$ for VR-CPS |
| 2 | $f_{fps} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_{frame}}$ | Frame rate from frame period | $f_{fps} \in [60, 120]\text{ Hz}$ |
| 3 | $\mathbf{T}_{eye} \;=\; \mathbf{T}_{world \rightarrow HMD} \cdot \mathbf{T}_{HMD \rightarrow eye}$ | Head pose chain for stereo rendering | SE(3) matrix |
| 4 | $\mathbf{p}_{screen} \;=\; \mathbf{K}_{t} \cdot \big[\mathbf{R}_{t} \mid \mathbf{t}_{t}\big] \cdot \mathbf{p}_{w}$ | World-to-screen projection of a 3D point | Pixels |
| 5 | $\epsilon_{overlay} \;\geq\; v_{head} \cdot \tau_{m2p}$ | Worst-case registration error in dynamic AR | Meters; $v_{head}$ in m/s |
| 6 | $f_s \;\geq\; 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling for physical sensors | Hz |
| 7 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \mid k} \;=\; \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}_{k}\big(\mathbf{z}_{k} - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \mid k-1}\big)$ | Kalman filter state estimate (CPS cyber layer) | State vector |
| 8 | $D_{usable} \;\approx\; \dfrac{R_{eye}}{FoV_{deg}}$ | Pixels-per-degree (visual acuity proxy) | pixels/degree, $D_{usable} \geq 30$ |
| 9 | $T_{total} \;=\; T_{sense} + T_{comm} + T_{control} + T_{actuate}$ | End-to-end CPS loop delay | Seconds |
| 10 | $S \;=\; \dfrac{n_{DoF,used}}{n_{DoF,available}}$ | Tracking completeness score | Dimensionless, $0 \leq S \leq 1$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial AR (e.g., Siemens, Boeing, Lockheed Martin assembly lines)**: AR glasses project the next assembly step onto the physical workpiece, reducing assembly time and human error.
- **VR Digital Twin (e.g., ANSYS, Siemens NX, Dassault 3DEXPERIENCE)**: VR is used to walk through a full-scale factory *before* construction, validating CPS layout.
- **Tele-surgery CPS (e.g., Da Vinci surgical robot)**: AR/VR displays feed the remote surgeon the patient's anatomy, while the robot is the actuator.
- **Autonomous-vehicle CPS**: VR is used to generate millions of training kilometres for the AI driving policy in synthetic cities.
- **Smart-grid CPS**: AR overlays show real-time power flow on a substation when an operator inspects a switchgear.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Booster**: When answering, always name the **CPS physical process**, the **AR/VR role**, and the **engineering benefit** in the same sentence. This single-stroke coverage of three concepts is what differentiates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.

## 3.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation — Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget for an AR-in-CPS Operator Console

A common KTU 14-mark sub-question asks: *Given the stages of an AR pipeline in a CPS, derive the total motion-to-photon latency and check whether it meets the 20 ms VR-CPS stability bound.*

We derive it from first principles below.

**Step 1 — Identify the four sequential stages of any AR/VR pipeline that contribute to latency.**

The four stages are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Stage 1: Sensor sampling} \rightarrow \tau_{sense} \\
&\text{Stage 2: Cyber computation (state estimation, rendering preparation)} \rightarrow \tau_{compute} \\
&\text{Stage 3: Graphics rendering of the new frame} \rightarrow \tau_{render} \\
&\text{Stage 4: Display scan-out and photon emission} \rightarrow \tau_{display}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the total latency as the sum of the per-stage latencies (additive because they are sequential).**

$$
\tau_{m2p} \;=\; \tau_{sense} + \tau_{compute} + \tau_{render} + \tau_{display}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute realistic numerical values for a high-end industrial AR-in-CPS console.**

We are given:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{sense} &= 2 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(LiDAR/IMU at 500 Hz)} \\
\tau_{compute} &= 3 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(edge GPU sensor fusion)} \\
\tau_{render} &= 8 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(GPU rasterization)} \\
\tau_{display} &= 5 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(LCD persistence + scan-out)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sum step by step.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{m2p} &= 2 \text{ ms} + 3 \text{ ms} + 8 \text{ ms} + 5 \text{ ms} \\
&= 5 \text{ ms} + 8 \text{ ms} + 5 \text{ ms} \\
&= 13 \text{ ms} + 5 \text{ ms} \\
&= 18 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare against the VR-CPS stability bound.**

$$
\tau_{m2p} = 18 \text{ ms} \;<\; 20 \text{ ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{System meets the VR-CPS stability bound.}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the worst-case registration error for a moving head using the previously stated equation $\epsilon_{overlay} \geq v_{head} \cdot \tau_{m2p}$.**

Assuming a head-rotation linear velocity at the temple $v_{head} = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$ (a brisk head turn):

$$
\epsilon_{overlay} \;\geq\; 0.5 \;\text{m/s} \;\times\; 0.018 \;\text{s} \;=\; 0.009 \;\text{m} \;=\; 9 \;\text{mm}
$$

Interpretation: under brisk head motion, the AR overlay may be misaligned from the true physical object by up to 9 mm. For centimeter-scale CPS overlays (e.g., labeling a switchgear), this is acceptable. For millimeter-scale surgical CPS overlays, this 9 mm is unacceptable — the latency must be reduced below $\tau_{m2p} = 4 \text{ ms}$ to bring $\epsilon_{overlay}$ below 2 mm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Insight for KTU**
> The derivation shows that the **stringent latency requirement of the underlying CPS** dictates the **AR/VR hardware specification**, and not the other way around. This is the central design lesson of Module 1.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Differentiate between **Augmented Reality (AR)** and **Virtual Reality (VR)** in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS). Which one is more appropriate for a nuclear-plant emergency training application, and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | AR | VR |
|---|---|---|
| User perception | Real world is *augmented* with virtual overlays | Real world is *fully replaced* by a synthetic environment |
| Hardware | AR glasses / transparent displays / projector | HMD / CAVE with opaque display |
| CPS role | Real-time overlay of cyber data onto physical process | Full simulation of CPS replica for safe training |
| Risk level | Real physical assets remain live | Real physical assets are not required |
| Best for | Supervision, maintenance, teleoperation | Training, validation, dangerous-scenario drill |

For a **nuclear-plant emergency** training application, **VR** is appropriate because the emergency scenario (meltdown, radiation leak) cannot be physically drilled without harm, and the trainee must practice full sensorimotor response inside a high-fidelity digital twin of the CPS. *(Full 3 marks for the comparison + correct choice + CPS-grounded justification.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
Define **motion-to-photon latency** in an AR/VR-in-CPS pipeline. State the standard VR-CPS stability bound and explain what happens if the bound is violated.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Motion-to-photon latency $\tau_{m2p}$ is the total time elapsed between a physical motion of the user (or change in CPS state) and the corresponding updated photon output from the AR/VR display. It is the sum $\tau_{sense} + \tau_{compute} + \tau_{render} + \tau_{display}$.

The standard VR-CPS stability bound is $\tau_{m2p} < 20 \text{ ms}$.

If violated, the user experiences **vection mismatch** (visual motion lags vestibular motion), leading to **cybersickness / motion sickness**, and in closed-loop CPS teleoperation the human-in-the-loop controller becomes unstable, with overshoot, oscillation, or loss of the physical asset.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations (ESE), Module 1 carries one 14-mark question with internal choice. Both alternative questions are given below.

---

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 ESE Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **closed-loop architecture of an AR/VR-in-CPS system**, identifying the role of the human operator as a node inside the loop. State and justify the standard motion-to-photon latency bound for VR-CPS applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **four major application domains** where AR/VR is deployed in Cyber-Physical Systems. For each, name the AR/VR role, the underlying CPS physical process, and the specific engineering benefit. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- (1 mark) Definition: An AR/VR-in-CPS system is a CPS in which the human–machine interface is realized through immersive display technology, integrating the human as an active node in the feedback loop.
- (2 marks) Functional block diagram (similar to Section 4.1 of these notes): Physical Plant → Sensors → Communication Network → Cyber Layer (State Estimator, e.g., Kalman filter) → AR/VR Renderer → Display (HMD/CAVE) → Human Operator → Intention Capture → Command Validator → Actuator → back to Physical Plant.
- (2 marks) Explanation of the operator's role: The operator is not outside the system but is *inside* the loop. His perception of the cyber state via AR/VR, and his subsequent actuation decision, closes the loop. Hence AR/VR latency and accuracy directly determine loop stability.
- (2 marks) Latency bound: $\tau_{m2p} < 20$ ms for VR-CPS. Justification: above ~20 ms, the human vestibular–visual mismatch exceeds the threshold of perception (~20 ms is the human reaction-time floor for visual self-motion), causing motion sickness and unstable teleoperation.

**Part (b) — 7 marks (1.75 marks each application):**

1. **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0)** — Role: AR-based assembly guidance. CPS process: pick-and-place robot on a conveyor. Benefit: 30–50% reduction in assembly errors and time; workforce upskilling without halting the line.

2. **Digital Twin Validation of a CPS Plant** — Role: VR-based full-scale walkthrough. CPS process: chemical-plant or power-plant control logic validation. Benefit: validates CPS control strategy *before* capital deployment, saving cost and preventing unsafe designs.

3. **Autonomous Vehicle CPS** — Role: VR-based AI training. CPS process: simulated traffic CPS with synthetic LiDAR/camera. Benefit: generates millions of training miles in days without real-world risk; rare-event exposure.

4. **Surgical / Robotic CPS** — Role: AR-based anatomical overlay. CPS process: minimally invasive robotic surgery. Benefit: surgeon perceives subsurface anatomy in real time, reducing incision size and improving precision.

(Any four of the ten domains from Section 3.1 are acceptable; examiners award full marks when each domain is tied to a CPS process and a quantified or clearly described engineering benefit.)

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 ESE Model Paper, Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Define Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and explain the **four functional roles** that AR/VR plays inside a CPS. Give one real-world example for each role. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A high-end industrial AR headset used in a smart-factory CPS has the following per-stage latencies: $\tau_{sense} = 3$ ms, $\tau_{compute} = 4$ ms, $\tau_{render} = 9$ ms, $\tau_{display} = 6$ ms. Calculate the **total motion-to-photon latency** $\tau_{m2p}$ and determine whether the system satisfies the VR-CPS stability bound. If the head-temple linear velocity during a typical head turn is $v_{head} = 0.4$ m/s, calculate the **worst-case AR overlay registration error**. Comment on whether the system is suitable for a millimeter-precision CPS surgical overlay. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A CPS is an engineered system that tightly integrates sensing, computation, communication, and actuation to monitor and control physical processes. Computation (cyber) and physics (physical) are deeply intertwined through feedback loops.

**Four functional roles (1.25 marks each):**

1. **Supervisory Visualization** — AR/VR displays the real-time CPS state to the human operator. *Example:* AR overlay on a smart substation showing live voltage, current, and temperature on the corresponding physical switchgear.

2. **Telepresence / Teleoperation** — AR/VR allows remote control of a physical CPS actuator. *Example:* VR-driven disaster-response robot, with the operator safely outside the rubble zone.

3. **Training and Simulation** — VR simulates a CPS replica for safe, repeatable training. *Example:* VR drill for nuclear-plant operators inside a digital twin of the reactor.

4. **Maintenance and Assembly Guidance** — AR provides step-by-step guidance on a physical CPS asset. *Example:* AR-assisted aircraft-engine assembly on a smart factory line.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Total latency (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{m2p} &= \tau_{sense} + \tau_{compute} + \tau_{render} + \tau_{display} \\
&= 3 + 4 + 9 + 6 \\
&= 22 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Stability check (1 mark):**

The standard VR-CPS bound is $\tau_{m2p} < 20$ ms. Since $22 \text{ ms} > 20 \text{ ms}$, the system **fails** the bound by 2 ms.

**Step 3 — Registration error (2 marks):**

$$
\epsilon_{overlay} \geq v_{head} \cdot \tau_{m2p} = 0.4 \;\text{m/s} \times 0.022 \;\text{s} = 0.0088 \;\text{m} = 8.8 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Suitability comment (1 mark):**

The 8.8 mm worst-case error is acceptable for **centimeter-scale** industrial CPS overlays (e.g., labelling a switchgear, pointing to a robot) but is **not suitable for millimeter-precision surgical CPS overlays**, where registration error must be below 1–2 mm. To meet surgical precision, the latency must be reduced below $\tau_{m2p} \approx 5$ ms (giving $\epsilon_{overlay} \approx 2$ mm).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Do NOT** write a generic answer about "AR/VR is used in gaming and entertainment." Examiners *immediately* deduct marks when the answer is not tied to a Cyber-Physical System.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse AR with VR in the application. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks. Use a sentence like *"AR for live overlay of cyber data on physical assets; VR for safe simulation of the CPS replica."*
> 3. **Do NOT** skip the **engineering benefit** when describing an application. Naming a domain (e.g., "surgery") without saying *what CPS problem is solved* and *what is gained quantitatively or qualitatively* loses up to half the marks of that sub-question.
> 4. **Do NOT** forget the **units** in latency / frame-rate / registration-error calculations. KTU examiners explicitly test unit awareness.
> 5. **Do NOT** omit the **human-in-the-loop** remark. It is the single highest-leverage conceptual point in this topic and a 14-mark answer without it is capped at ~9 marks.
