| Scope | Single device/microcontroller | Distributed, networked system |
| Feedback | Often open-loop | Always closed-loop |
| Real-Time | May not be strict | Usually hard real-time |
| Integration | Cyber OR Physical | Cyber AND Physical (deep integration) |
| Adaptability | Static, fixed function | Dynamic, adaptive, often learning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** Definition = 1 mark, Difference table = 2 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the concept of "determinism" in the context of CPS design. Why is it critical for AR/VR applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Determinism** is the property of a system that guarantees a bounded and predictable response time for every input or event. In CPS, this is critical because the system must react to physical events (e.g., obstacle detection in a drone) within a strict deadline.

**Why critical for AR/VR:**
- AR/VR applications require **frame rates of 90–120 Hz** (8–11 ms per frame) to avoid motion sickness.
- Any missed deadline causes **judder, latency, or cybersickness**.
- Determinism ensures consistent perceptual experience, especially in **VR-based CPS training** and **AR-assisted remote operations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** Definition = 1 mark, CPS relevance = 1 mark, AR/VR relevance = 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **5C architecture of Cyber-Physical Systems** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **design principles of CPS** with specific reference to **modularity, abstraction, real-time constraints, and security**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) 5C Architecture of CPS (7 marks):**

The 5C architecture is a widely accepted reference model that organizes CPS into five functional levels:

**C1 – Smart Connection Level:**
- Responsible for acquiring accurate, reliable data from physical sensors.
- Establishes bidirectional, time-synchronized communication between cyber and physical layers.
- Common protocols: CAN bus (automotive), MQTT (IoT), OPC-UA (industrial), 5G (low-latency).
- **Marks: 1.5**

**C2 – Data-to-Information Level (Conversion):**
- Transforms raw sensor data into meaningful information using edge analytics.
- Performs noise filtering, data fusion, feature extraction, and aggregation.
- **Example:** Combining accelerometer + gyroscope data to compute orientation.
- **Marks: 1.5**

**C3 – Cyber Level:**
- Hosts the core computation: digital twins, simulations, AI/ML inference, predictive models.
- Runs on edge, fog, or cloud infrastructure depending on latency requirements.
- **Marks: 1**

**C4 – Cognitive Level:**
- Derives actionable insights from cyber-level outputs.
- Supports human decision-making, often through AR/VR dashboards or alert systems.
- **Marks: 1**

**C5 – Configuration Level:**
- Closes the loop by sending supervisory control commands back to the physical layer.
- Implements feedback control (PID, MPC, AI-based) for autonomous actuation.
- **Marks: 1**

**Block Diagram** (Expected in answer — see Section 4.1 of notes). **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Design Principles of CPS (7 marks):**

1. **Modularity (1.5 marks):**
   - CPS is decomposed into smaller, independent, reusable sub-systems (e.g., sensor module, communication module, control module).
   - Each module can be developed, tested, and replaced independently.
   - Facilitates parallel development and easier maintenance.

2. **Abstraction (1.5 marks):**
   - Each layer hides implementation details from higher layers.
   - **Example:** A sensor driver abstracts ADC details; the application just calls `read_temperature()`.
   - Reduces complexity and enables portability.

3. **Real-Time Constraints (2 marks):**
   - Hard real-time tasks must complete before their deadline.
   - Use of real-time operating systems (RTOS) like FreeRTOS, VxWorks, or QNX.
   - Scheduling algorithms: Rate Monotonic (RM), Earliest Deadline First (EDF).
   - In AR/VR CPS, the **end-to-end latency budget is often <20 ms** (motion-to-photon).

4. **Security (2 marks):**
   - CPS are vulnerable to cyber-attacks that can cause physical harm.
   - Security must be **built-in (security by design)**, not added later.
   - Measures: secure boot, encryption (TLS/DTLS), authentication, intrusion detection, hardware roots of trust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** Each principle clearly explained with example = 1.5–2 marks. Diagram = 1 mark (already counted above).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the **closed-loop architecture of CPS** with a block diagram. Explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how **real-time scheduling (Rate Monotonic)** is applied in CPS. Given 3 tasks with $C_1=1, T_1=4; C_2=2, T_2=6; C_3=3, T_3=10$, determine if the task set is schedulable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Closed-Loop Architecture (7 marks):**

A CPS operates as a **closed-loop control system** with five core blocks:

1. **Physical Plant:** The real-world process (e.g., robotic arm, autonomous vehicle, drone). **(1 mark)**
2. **Sensor:** Measures physical variables (temperature, pressure, position, velocity) and converts them to electrical/digital signals. **(1 mark)**
3. **Network/Communication:** Transports data between physical and cyber layers. May use wired (CAN, Ethernet) or wireless (Wi-Fi, 5G, BLE). **(1 mark)**
4. **Controller (Cyber Layer):** Runs control algorithms (PID, MPC, AI-based) to compute corrective actions. Often hosted on a microcontroller, edge device, or cloud. **(2 marks)**
5. **Actuator:** Receives control commands and applies physical actions (motor torque, valve position, heater power). **(1 mark)**

**Block Diagram** (Expected — see Section 4.2 of notes). **(1 mark)**

The loop closes when the actuator's effect on the plant is re-measured by the sensor, completing the feedback cycle.

---

**(b) Rate Monotonic Scheduling Example (7 marks):**

**Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** assigns higher priority to tasks with shorter periods. **(1 mark)**

**Task Set:**

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | Priority (RM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | Highest (shortest T) |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 | Medium |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 10 | Lowest |

**Step 1: Compute Total Utilization $U$** **(2 marks)**

$$
U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3}
$$

$$
U = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10}
$$

$$
U = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 = 0.883
$$

**Step 2: Compute Liu & Layland Bound for $n=3$** **(2 marks)**

$$
U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) \approx 0.7797
$$

**Step 3: Compare** **(1 mark)**

$$
U = 0.883 > U_{bound} = 0.7797
$$

**Conclusion:** The Liu & Layland test is **inconclusive**. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 4: Apply Response Time Analysis (RTA) for exact check (bonus, 0.5 marks):**

For $\tau_3$ (lowest priority):

$$
R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 3 + 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 6
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 3 + 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 7
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 3 + 2 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 9
$$

$$
R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2
$$

$$
R_3^{(4)} = 3 + 3 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 10
$$

$$
R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10
$$

Converged at $R_3 = 10 \text{ ms}$. Since $R_3 = T_3 = 10$ and $D_3 = T_3 = 10$:

$$
R_3 \leq D_3 \;\; \text{(just satisfied)}
$$

**Task set is schedulable, but with zero slack** — risky in practice.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing CPS with IoT** (–2 marks): Many students describe IoT (data collection) when asked about CPS (control). Always emphasize the *closed-loop* nature.
> 2. **Forgetting Units** in formula derivations (–1 mark): Always state units (ms, Hz) explicitly.
> 3. **Skipping diagrams** in 7-mark questions (–1 to 2 marks): KTU mandates a "neat block diagram" for full marks. Always draw one.
> 4. **Liu & Layland bound misuse**: It is a *sufficient* condition, not necessary. If $U > U_{bound}$, do NOT conclude "not schedulable" — use RTA instead.
> 5. **Conflating 3C and 5C models**: Use 3C for short answers, 5C for long answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary — Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **CPS = Computation + Communication + Control + Physical Process**, deeply integrated via feedback.
- **Key Distinction**: CPS is *closed-loop* and *real-time*; IoT is *open-loop* and often best-effort.
- **5C Architecture**: Connection → Conversion → Cyber → Cognitive → Configuration.
- **3C Model** (compact form): Computation, Communication, Control.
- **Core Design Principles** (must know all 9): Modularity, Abstraction, Hierarchy, Separation of Concerns, Determinism, Fault Tolerance, Security, Composability, Co-Simulation.
- **Real-Time**: Hard = miss-deadline = system failure; Soft = miss-deadline = degraded quality.
- **Rate Monotonic Scheduling**: Shorter period ⇒ higher priority.
- **Liu & Layland Bound**: $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. If $U \leq U_{bound}$, **guaranteed schedulable**.
- **RTA Iteration**: $R^{(k+1)} = C + \sum \lceil R^{(k)}/T_j \rceil C_j$; converges when $R^{(k+1)} = R^{(k)}$.
- **Nyquist Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. VR/AR uses 6×–10× oversampling in practice.
- **Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget for VR**: typically **< 20 ms** to avoid cybersickness.
- **PID Control Law**: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \, de/dt$.
- **Stability Criterion**: All closed-loop poles must have **negative real parts**.
- **Availability**: $A = \text{MTTF} / (\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR})$.
- **Security by Design** is non-negotiable in CPS — unlike in pure software systems.
- **AR/VR + CPS Integration**: AR/VR is typically placed at the **Cognitive Level** (C4) and **Application Layer** for human-in-the-loop supervision and tele-operation.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — CPS Architecture and Design Principles**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L1–L5).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration of AR/VR with CPS components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT824 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Integration of AR/VR with CPS components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** are engineered systems that are built from, and depend upon, the seamless integration of computational algorithms, communication networks, and physical processes. Embedded computers and networked sensors monitor and control the physical processes, usually with feedback loops where physical processes affect computations and vice versa.

**Augmented Reality (AR)** is a technology that superimposes computer-generated perceptual information (visual, auditory, haptic, olfactory) onto the real-world environment in real time, enhancing a user's perception of and interaction with the physical world.

**Virtual Reality (VR)** is a technology that generates a fully immersive, computer-simulated environment that replaces the user's real-world sensory input, enabling interactive 3D experiences typically delivered through head-mounted displays (HMDs).

**Integration of AR/VR with CPS components** refers to the architectural and functional coupling of immersive reality interfaces with the sensing, computation, communication, and actuation layers of a Cyber-Physical System, enabling real-time bidirectional data exchange between the human operator and the physical process under control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Integration is not merely a "display" relationship. It establishes a **closed-loop immersive cyber-physical interaction** where AR/VR acts as the *Human-Machine Interface (HMI)* layer of the CPS, while the CPS provides real-time multi-modal telemetry to drive the immersive rendering pipeline.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a surgeon performing a complex operation on a patient. The patient is the **physical process**. Various medical devices (heart rate monitors, oxygen sensors, infusion pumps) are the **sensors and actuators** of the CPS. Now imagine that instead of looking at multiple isolated screens, you wear AR glasses that:
- Overlay the patient's vital signs directly on your field of view
- Highlight blood vessels in real time (AR)
- Allow you to remotely control a robotic surgical arm by hand gestures
- Warn you with haptic feedback if a critical parameter crosses a threshold

This is exactly what **AR/VR-CPS integration** does. The CPS is the body; AR/VR is the nervous system interface that lets the human *feel, see, and control* the cyber-physical process intuitively and immersively.

**Another analogy — Smart Factory:**
Think of a modern smart factory where a technician wears a VR headset. Inside the VR, he sees a *digital twin* of the factory floor that updates in real time. He can see which robotic arm is overheating, virtually walk over to it, and trigger a maintenance protocol. The data flows: **Physical Machine → Sensors → CPS Backend → Digital Twin → VR Headset → Human Decision → Command Back to Actuator**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** AR/VR integration with CPS is fundamentally about three things:
> 1. **Real-time data ingestion** from CPS sensors
> 2. **Immersive visualization and decision support** for human operators
> 3. **Low-latency command feedback** from immersive interfaces to physical actuators

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in AR/VR-CPS Integration

The following engineering metrics are critical for any AR/VR-CPS integrated system (highlighted in **bold** because KTU questions often ask for these values):

- **Motion-to-Photon Latency:** **< 20 ms** (industry standard, < 11 ms for high-fidelity VR)
- **Frame Rate:** **90 Hz minimum** (modern VR HMDs run at 90–120 Hz)
- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** **6 DoF** for full spatial tracking (3 translational + 3 rotational)
- **Field of View (FoV):** **90°–120°** for consumer HMDs
- **Inter-pupillary Distance (IPD):** typically **54–74 mm**
- **Time-Synchronization Drift Tolerance:** **< 1 ms** between CPS sensor clock and VR render clock
- **Network Round-Trip Time (RTT) for teleoperation:** **< 50 ms** for stable haptic feedback
- **Spatial Registration Accuracy:** **< 1 mm** at 1 m distance for industrial AR

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of AR/VR-Integrated CPS

The integration is best understood as a **five-layer architecture** that maps the classical CPS layers to immersive interfaces:

**Layer 1 — Physical Layer (Sensors & Actuators)**
- Sensors: LiDAR, IMU, depth cameras, force/torque sensors, temperature, pressure
- Actuators: Robotic arms, motors, drones, valves, haptic devices
- Function: Source of ground-truth physical state and target of control commands

**Layer 2 — Communication Layer (Network)**
- Wired: EtherCAT, CAN, industrial Ethernet (TSN – Time-Sensitive Networking)
- Wireless: 5G/6G, Wi-Fi 6/6E, BLE, UWB
- Protocols: MQTT, OPC-UA, DDS (Data Distribution Service), ROS 2
- Function: Low-latency, deterministic, and reliable transport of CPS data

**Layer 3 — Computation Layer (Edge + Cloud)**
- Edge: On-device inference, sensor fusion, latency-critical decision making
- Cloud: Heavy analytics, ML model training, digital twin synchronization
- Function: Process raw CPS data, run digital twin, generate AR/VR scene graph

**Layer 4 — Immersive Interface Layer (AR/VR Rendering)**
- Rendering engine: Unity, Unreal Engine, OpenXR
- Tracking system: Inside-out / outside-in 6DoF
- Display: HMD, CAVE, holographic displays
- Function: Convert CPS state into immersive 3D scenes

**Layer 5 — Human Interaction & Feedback Layer**
- Input modalities: Hand tracking, eye tracking, voice, gesture, controllers
- Output modalities: Visual, auditory, haptic, olfactory
- Function: Close the human-in-the-loop control path

### 2.2 Theoretical 'Why' and 'How' of Integration

**Why integrate AR/VR with CPS?**
- Spatial reasoning: 3D environments align with 3D physical processes (manufacturing, surgery, navigation)
- Situational awareness: Operator sees system state in context, not as raw numbers
- Remote operation: Telepresence enables operation in hazardous environments (nuclear, space, deep sea)
- Training: Safe simulation of CPS behavior before real deployment
- Maintenance: AR overlays repair instructions on physical equipment

**How is integration achieved technically?**
1. **Digital Twin Synchronization** — a high-fidelity virtual replica of the CPS continuously updated via sensor streams
2. **Sensor Fusion** — combines IMU, vision, and external tracking to achieve robust 6DoF pose
3. **Co-registration (Spatial Mapping)** — aligns virtual content with physical coordinates using SLAM
4. **Closed-loop control** — VR gestures/commands translated into actuator setpoints
5. **Predictive rendering** — uses CPS state prediction to compensate for network/sensor latency

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below summarizes every formula you may need to answer numerical KTU questions on this topic. Memorize it.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Description & Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Motion-to-Photon Latency | $L_{mtp} = L_{sensor} + L_{proc} + L_{net} + L_{render} + L_{display}$ | Total delay from physical event to visual update, in **ms** |
| 2 | Frame Time | $T_f = \frac{1}{f}$ | Where $f$ is refresh rate in **Hz**; for 90 Hz, $T_f \approx 11.11$ ms |
| 3 | Sensor Fusion (Kalman) State Update | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})$ | Posteriori state estimate; $K_k$ = Kalman gain |
| 4 | Kalman Gain | $K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Optimal weighting of measurement vs prediction |
| 5 | Pose Composition (6DoF) | $T_{world} = T_{world \to camera} \cdot T_{camera \to HMD}$ | $T \in SE(3)$, 4x4 homogeneous transform |
| 6 | Projection (Camera) | $s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R \vert t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $K$ = intrinsic matrix; $R,t$ = extrinsics |
| 7 | Network Bandwidth (Digital Twin Sync) | $B = N \cdot f_s \cdot b$ | $N$ = number of channels, $f_s$ = sampling rate (Hz), $b$ = bits/sample |
| 8 | Effective Throughput (Wireless) | $T_{eff} = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon capacity, **bps** |
| 9 | Haptic Stability Bound | $f_{max} < \frac{1}{2 L_{RTT}}$ | For stable bilateral teleoperation, Nyquist on round-trip |
| 10 | SLAM Landmark Update | $\mu_{new} = g(\mu_{old}, u_t, z_t)$ | EKF-SLAM or Graph-SLAM update step |
| 11 | Refresh Rate vs Latency | $f_{min} = \frac{1}{L_{max} - L_{render}}$ | Minimum frame rate to avoid dropped frames |
| 12 | Position Error Growth (IMU) | $\sigma_p(t) = \sigma_a \cdot \frac{t^2}{2}$ | Position drift from accelerometer noise; $t$ = time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a question says "Calculate the worst-case motion-to-photon latency," you must **sum** all five components. If asked "minimum frame rate," use the inverse of the *available* time window after subtracting render and display delays.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing:** AR-assisted assembly lines; VR-based digital twin commissioning
- **Healthcare:** VR surgical training, AR-guided surgery (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens in operating rooms)
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** VR simulators for CPS validation; AR HUDs as human-vehicle interface
- **Aerospace & Defense:** VR mission rehearsal; AR helmet-mounted displays for pilots
- **Smart Cities:** AR-based infrastructure monitoring layered on IoT sensor data
- **Energy:** VR control rooms for nuclear / power grid CPS where physical access is restricted

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is mathematically rigorous. Every algebraic step is shown. Use these derivations as templates for KTU 14-mark numerical problems.

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget

**Problem Setup:** A CPS has the following components: an IMU sensor (data ready in 2 ms), a network transmission over 5G (4 ms one-way), an edge processor running sensor fusion (5 ms), a GPU render pipeline (6 ms), and a display scan-out (2 ms). Compute the total motion-to-photon latency and check if it meets the **< 20 ms** VR threshold.

**Step 1 — Identify all latency components**
The total motion-to-photon latency is the sum of all sequential delays from physical event to photon emission:

$$L_{mtp} = L_{sensor} + L_{net} + L_{proc} + L_{render} + L_{display}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values**
The problem gives us:
- $L_{sensor} = 2$ ms
- $L_{net} = 4$ ms
- $L_{proc} = 5$ ms
- $L_{render} = 6$ ms
- $L_{display} = 2$ ms

Substituting:

$$L_{mtp} = 2 + 4 + 5 + 6 + 2$$

**Step 3 — Perform the arithmetic**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{mtp} &= 2 + 4 + 5 + 6 + 2 \\
&= 6 + 5 + 6 + 2 \\
&= 11 + 6 + 2 \\
&= 17 + 2 \\
&= 19 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compare against the threshold**
The VR threshold is **< 20 ms**.
Since $19 \text{ ms} < 20 \text{ ms}$, the system **meets** the motion-to-photon requirement.

**Step 5 — Compute the safety margin**

$$M_{safety} = 20 - 19 = 1 \text{ ms}$$

This is a very tight margin; KTU examiners often award partial credit for noting that while the system *technically* passes, the 1 ms margin is unsafe in practice.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Frame Rate from Available Latency Window

**Problem Setup:** A VR rendering pipeline consumes: $L_{render} = 7$ ms for GPU work, $L_{display} = 3$ ms for scan-out, and $L_{buffer} = 2$ ms for compositor queuing. The total latency budget allowed is $L_{max} = 20$ ms. Find the minimum frame rate.

**Step 1 — State the relationship**
The available time for one frame is the total budget minus the fixed per-frame processing time:

$$L_{avail} = L_{max} - L_{render} - L_{display} - L_{buffer}$$

**Step 2 — Compute available time**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{avail} &= 20 - 7 - 3 - 2 \\
&= 13 - 3 - 2 \\
&= 10 - 2 \\
&= 8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to frame rate**
The frame rate is the inverse of the available time:

$$f_{min} = \frac{1}{L_{avail}} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1000}{8} = 125 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion**
The VR system must run at a minimum of **125 Hz** to keep motion-to-photon latency below 20 ms. This exceeds the typical 90 Hz HMD refresh rate, so a 120 Hz or higher display would be required.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Kalman Filter Sensor Fusion for HMD Pose

**Problem Setup:** An HMD fuses IMU (prediction) and optical tracking (measurement) to estimate orientation. Given the state prediction $\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} = 0.5$ rad, measurement $z_k = 0.55$ rad, $H = 1$, prior covariance $P_{k \vert k-1} = 0.04$, and measurement noise $R = 0.01$. Compute the fused orientation estimate.

**Step 1 — Compute the Kalman gain**

$$K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k \vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$$

Substitute $H = 1$:

$$K_k = \frac{P_{k \vert k-1}}{P_{k \vert k-1} + R} = \frac{0.04}{0.04 + 0.01} = \frac{0.04}{0.05} = 0.8$$

**Step 2 — Compute the innovation (measurement residual)**

$$y_k = z_k - H \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} = 0.55 - 0.5 = 0.05 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Update the state estimate**

$$\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k \cdot y_k = 0.5 + 0.8 \times 0.05 = 0.5 + 0.04 = 0.54 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Update the posterior covariance**

$$P_{k \vert k} = (1 - K_k H) P_{k \vert k-1} = (1 - 0.8) \times 0.04 = 0.2 \times 0.04 = 0.008$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result**
The fused estimate is **0.54 rad**, lying between the IMU prediction (0.5) and the optical measurement (0.55), weighted 80% toward the optical reading. The posterior variance dropped from 0.04 to **0.008**, confirming reduced uncertainty after fusion.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Network Bandwidth for Digital Twin Synchronization

**Problem Setup:** A digital twin of a CPS receives 256 channels of sensor data, each sampled at 1 kHz with 16-bit resolution. Compute the required bandwidth.

**Step 1 — Write the formula**

$$B = N \cdot f_s \cdot b$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values**
$N = 256$ channels, $f_s = 1000$ Hz, $b = 16$ bits

$$B = 256 \times 1000 \times 16$$

**Step 3 — Compute step by step**

$$\begin{aligned}
B &= 256 \times 1000 \times 16 \\
&= 256 \times 16 \times 1000 \\
&= 4096 \times 1000 \\
&= 4{,}096{,}000 \text{ bps} \\
&= 4.096 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MB/s if needed**

$$B = \frac{4.096}{8} = 0.512 \text{ MB/s}$$

This bandwidth is easily handled by modern wired (1 Gbps) or 5G wireless networks.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Haptic Teleoperation Stability Bound

**Problem Setup:** A bilateral teleoperation system for a CPS robot has a round-trip network latency of $L_{RTT} = 30$ ms. Determine the maximum stable haptic update frequency.

**Step 1 — Apply the stability condition**
For stable haptic feedback, the update frequency must be less than half the inverse of the round-trip latency:

$$f_{max} < \frac{1}{2 L_{RTT}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $L_{RTT} = 30$ ms**

$$f_{max} < \frac{1}{2 \times 0.030} = \frac{1}{0.060} \approx 16.67 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion**
The haptic loop must be updated at a rate **below ~16 Hz** to remain stable. For KTU exam purposes, this is the upper bound; in practice, designers use 10 Hz to leave safety margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse $L_{RTT}$ with one-way latency. **Always** use the *round-trip* time in the stability formula for bilateral teleoperation. One-way latency would give an erroneously high $f_{max}$.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Mapping CPS Layers to AR/VR Components

| CPS Layer | AR/VR Counterpart | Data Type | Typical Latency Budget |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------|------------------------|
| Physical (Sensors) | Tracking Cameras, IMU on HMD | Raw 3D points, IMU samples | 1–2 ms |
| Physical (Actuators) | Haptic Gloves, Cane, Motors | Force/position commands | 1–5 ms |
| Communication | 5G, Wi-Fi 6E, USB-C tether | Packets, ROS topics, UDP streams | 4–10 ms |
| Computation (Edge) | Sensor fusion, SLAM, pose estimation | Pose graph, landmarks | 3–8 ms |
| Computation (Cloud) | Digital twin server, ML inference | Twin state, predictions | 20–100 ms (non-real-time) |
| Immersive Interface | Unity/Unreal renderer, HMD display | Frame buffers, audio | 6–11 ms |
| Human Interaction | Eye tracking, hand tracking, voice | Gaze rays, gestures | 1–5 ms |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Cyber-Physical Systems. How does AR/VR act as an interface layer to a CPS?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system in which computational algorithms, communication networks, and physical processes are tightly integrated via closed-loop feedback. Sensors monitor the physical world, computations make decisions, and actuators alter the physical world.
**AR/VR as CPS Interface (2 marks):** AR/VR serves as the *Human-Machine Interface (HMI)* layer. It ingests real-time CPS telemetry, renders it into immersive 3D visualizations (digital twins, spatial overlays), captures human gestures/voice/eye inputs, and translates them into actuator commands. This closes the human-in-the-loop path, enabling intuitive spatial reasoning, remote teleoperation, and predictive decision support in CPS.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any six engineering metrics critical for AR/VR-CPS integration and state their typical values.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):** Any six of the following (½ mark each):
1. **Motion-to-Photon Latency:** < 20 ms
2. **Frame Rate:** ≥ 90 Hz
3. **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** 6 DoF
4. **Field of View (FoV):** 90°–120°
5. **Spatial Registration Accuracy:** < 1 mm
6. **Network RTT (teleoperation):** < 50 ms
7. **Time-Sync Drift:** < 1 ms
8. **Inter-pupillary Distance:** 54–74 mm

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions in this module offer internal choice. Both alternatives below carry equal marks.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-layer architecture for integrating AR/VR with CPS components**. Describe the function of each layer and identify the typical data flow between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VR-based digital twin of a manufacturing CPS receives **512 sensor channels**, each sampled at **2 kHz** with **24-bit resolution**. Compute the required network bandwidth in **Mbps** and determine whether a standard **1 Gbps Ethernet** link is sufficient. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Five-Layer AR/VR-CPS Architecture (7 marks):**

The integration follows a five-layer architecture, presented in the block diagram below (see Section 4.1).

**Layer 1 — Physical Layer (Sensors & Actuators):** Contains the actual physical process (e.g., robotic arm, turbine) along with sensors (IMU, LiDAR, force sensors) that digitize physical state, and actuators (motors, valves) that receive control commands. *Function:* Source of ground truth and target of control. *(1 mark)*

**Layer 2 — Communication Layer:** Wired (EtherCAT, TSN) or wireless (5G, Wi-Fi 6E) networks transport data deterministically. Protocols such as OPC-UA, MQTT, and DDS are used. *Function:* Low-latency, reliable data transport. *(1 mark)*

**Layer 3 — Computation Layer:** Edge nodes perform real-time sensor fusion and pose estimation; cloud servers maintain the digital twin and run ML analytics. *Function:* Process raw data, maintain twin, generate scene graph. *(1 mark)*

**Layer 4 — Immersive Interface Layer:** Rendering engines (Unity, Unreal, OpenXR) convert CPS state into 3D immersive scenes. Tracking systems provide 6DoF pose. *Function:* Immersive visualization. *(1 mark)*

**Layer 5 — Human Interaction & Feedback Layer:** Hand/eye/voice trackers and haptic devices allow the operator to interact with the immersive scene; their inputs are converted to actuator commands. *Function:* Close the human-in-the-loop. *(1 mark)*

**Data Flow (2 marks):** Telemetry flows upward (Physical → Communication → Computation → Interface → Human). Commands flow downward (Human → Interface → Computation → Communication → Physical). The data flow is **bidirectional** and **closed-loop**, ensuring real-time synchronization between the virtual twin and the physical process.

---

**(b) Bandwidth Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula**

$$B = N \cdot f_s \cdot b$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values**
$N = 512$ channels, $f_s = 2000$ Hz, $b = 24$ bits

$$B = 512 \times 2000 \times 24$$

**Step 3 — Compute stepwise**

$$\begin{aligned}
512 \times 2000 &= 1{,}024{,}000 \\
1{,}024{,}000 \times 24 &= 24{,}576{,}000 \text{ bps} \\
&= 24.576 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with 1 Gbps link**

$$1 \text{ Gbps} = 1000 \text{ Mbps}$$

Since $24.576 \text{ Mbps} \ll 1000 \text{ Mbps}$, the 1 Gbps Ethernet link is **more than sufficient**. The link utilization is only:

$$\eta = \frac{24.576}{1000} \times 100\% = 2.46\%$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (½ mark):** The 1 Gbps link comfortably supports the digital twin's bandwidth demand with significant headroom for additional channels, error correction, and protocol overhead.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — ALTERNATIVE

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **digital twin** in the context of AR/VR-CPS integration. Discuss how sensor fusion (e.g., Kalman filter) is used to estimate the **6DoF pose of an HMD** in such a system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A telepresence CPS robot has a **round-trip network latency of 40 ms**. Compute the **maximum stable haptic update frequency**. If a designer wants haptic feedback at **1 kHz**, what is the maximum allowable one-way network latency? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Digital Twin & Sensor Fusion (7 marks):**

**Digital Twin Definition (2 marks):** A *digital twin* is a continuously updated, high-fidelity virtual replica of a physical CPS asset, synchronized in real time using sensor telemetry. In AR/VR-CPS integration, the digital twin serves as the immersive 3D model that the operator sees, manipulates, and uses to control the physical system. It is the *bridge* between the physical world (CPS) and the virtual world (AR/VR).

**6DoF Pose Estimation via Sensor Fusion (5 marks):**
6DoF pose = 3 translational $(x, y, z)$ + 3 rotational (roll, pitch, yaw) degrees of freedom. Sensors used:
- **IMU** (accelerometer + gyroscope) → high-rate (1 kHz) prediction of motion
- **Optical/Camera tracker** → absolute position fixes at lower rate (60–120 Hz)
- **Magnetometer** → heading reference to correct gyroscope drift

These are fused using a **Kalman Filter**, which has two steps:

**Predict step (high-rate IMU):**

$$\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1 \vert k-1} + B u_k$$
$$P_{k \vert k-1} = A P_{k-1 \vert k-1} A^T + Q$$

**Update step (when optical measurement arrives):**

$$K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k \vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$$
$$\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1})$$
$$P_{k \vert k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k \vert k-1}$$

The IMU gives smooth, high-rate pose but drifts over time; optical tracking is drift-free but low-rate and noisy. The Kalman filter optimally combines both, yielding a stable, low-latency 6DoF pose estimate that drives the AR/VR scene rendering and aligns virtual content with the physical world.

---

**(b) Haptic Stability Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute maximum stable haptic frequency**
Using the stability bound:

$$f_{max} < \frac{1}{2 L_{RTT}}$$

Substitute $L_{RTT} = 40 \text{ ms}$:

$$f_{max} < \frac{1}{2 \times 0.040} = \frac{1}{0.080} = 12.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Compute maximum allowable one-way latency for 1 kHz haptic**
For $f = 1000$ Hz, the round-trip latency must satisfy:

$$L_{RTT} < \frac{1}{2 f} = \frac{1}{2000} = 0.0005 \text{ s} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to one-way latency**
Assuming symmetric link, one-way latency is:

$$L_{one-way} = \frac{L_{RTT}}{2} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (½ mark):** 1 kHz haptic feedback requires one-way network latency below **0.25 ms** — this is achievable only on dedicated wired links (e.g., real-time EtherCAT, fiber with hardware timestamping) and is practically infeasible on wireless networks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse one-way and round-trip latency** in haptic stability calculations. The formula $\frac{1}{2L_{RTT}}$ uses *round-trip* time. Marks are deducted for mixing units (ms vs μs).
> 2. **Do not** report $f_{max}$ as the *desired* operating frequency; it is the *upper bound*. Operate below it (e.g., 10 Hz when bound is 12.5 Hz).
> 3. **Bandwidth questions:** Always include units (bps / Mbps) explicitly. Forgetting unit conversion (bits vs bytes) is the single most common 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Architecture questions:** Students often describe layers but *forget to draw arrows* showing data flow direction. KTU examiners specifically look for the *bidirectional* closed-loop nature.
> 5. **Kalman filter derivations:** Show the **substitution step explicitly**. Writing only the final answer with no intermediate arithmetic is marked zero even if numerically correct.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- ☐ **CPS = Computation + Communication + Physical Process**, tightly coupled with feedback loops
- ☐ **AR/VR role in CPS = HMI layer**, not just visualization
- ☐ **Five-layer architecture:** Physical → Communication → Computation → Immersive Interface → Human Interaction
- ☐ **Motion-to-photon latency formula:** $L_{mtp} = L_{sensor} + L_{net} + L_{proc} + L_{render} + L_{display}$
- ☐ **Threshold values:** $L_{mtp} < 20$ ms, frame rate $\geq 90$ Hz, 6DoF, FoV 90°–120°
- ☐ **Bandwidth formula:** $B = N \cdot f_s \cdot b$; **Always** convert to Mbps by $\div 10^6$
- ☐ **Kalman Filter:** two steps (Predict from IMU, Update from optical) — gives optimal 6DoF pose
- ☐ **Haptic stability:** $f_{max} < \frac{1}{2L_{RTT}}$ — uses *round-trip* latency
- ☐ **Digital Twin** = real-time virtual replica of CPS, driven by sensor streams
- ☐ **Co-registration / SLAM** aligns virtual content with physical world coordinates
- ☐ **Protocols to remember:** OPC-UA, MQTT, DDS, ROS 2, EtherCAT, TSN
- ☐ **Edge vs Cloud split:** edge for latency-critical pose/fusion; cloud for digital twin + ML
- ☐ **Bidirectional data flow** is the defining feature — always mention it in architecture answers
- ☐ **Predictive rendering** compensates for latency using CPS state forecasting
- ☐ **Key applications:** Industry 4.0, surgical AR, autonomous vehicles, smart grids, aerospace simulators
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial automation and control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT824 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Industrial automation and control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Industrial Automation and Control (IAC)** in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) refers to the integrated deployment of programmable control hardware, supervisory software, and Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs) to govern the operation of physical industrial processes with deterministic precision. When fused with **Augmented Reality (AR)** and **Virtual Reality (VR)**, the classical automation pyramid is elevated into an **immersive cyber-physical control architecture**, where the operator's perception, decision-making, and actuation are augmented, simulated, or teleoperated through stereoscopic visual interfaces.

The system is formally composed of three tightly-coupled loops:
1. **Physical Layer** – Sensors, actuators, motors, valves, and field instruments.
2. **Cyber Layer** – PLCs, SCADA, DCS, edge controllers, and the **Digital Twin**.
3. **Cognitive/Immersive Layer** – AR glasses, VR CAVEs, haptic gloves, and gesture-recognition modules that close the human-in-the-loop (HITL) feedback chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Tone):** "Industrial automation and control within CPS is the closed-loop orchestration of physical industrial assets through cyber computational entities, where AR/VR serves as the perceptual and decisional bridge between the human operator and the machine process."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cockpit of a Factory"

Imagine a fighter pilot flying a jet. The pilot does not directly push the control surfaces of the aircraft; instead, they look at a **Head-Up Display (HUD)** that overlays critical data (speed, altitude, target lock) on top of the real world, and manipulate a **joystick** that the onboard computer translates into precise aerodynamic movements.

**An AR/VR-enabled industrial control room works exactly the same way:**
- The factory floor is the "battlefield."
- The PLCs and SCADA are the "onboard computer."
- The AR headset is the "HUD" — it overlays live telemetry, alarm zones, and predicted failures directly on top of the real machinery.
- The VR environment is the "flight simulator" — it allows the operator to rehearse shutdowns, emergency protocols, and maintenance routines in a risk-free virtual replica (the **Digital Twin**) before touching the real plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The fundamental value of AR/VR in industrial automation is **spatial situational awareness**. Traditional 2D HMIs force the operator to mentally map flat numbers onto a 3D factory. AR/VR collapses that cognitive distance by registering the data *in-place* with the physical asset it describes.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Weightage)

The following metrics are repeatedly tested in KTU assessments:

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Motion-to-Photon Latency** | **< 20 ms** | Maximum delay to avoid VR-induced motion sickness |
| **AR Registration Accuracy** | **< 2 mm at 1 m** | Spatial alignment error of virtual overlays |
| **Field of View (FoV)** | **≥ 90°** | Human-equivalent peripheral vision |
| **Refresh Rate** | **≥ 90 Hz** | Minimum flicker-free stereoscopic rendering |
| **Update Rate of Digital Twin** | **≥ 10 Hz** | Real-time synchronization with physical asset |
| **MTTR Reduction (with AR)** | **20–40 %** | Mean Time To Repair improvement benchmark |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the rule of three:** VR latency must be **< 20 ms**, AR registration must be **< 2 mm**, and the digital twin update must be **> 10 Hz**. These three numbers are KTU-favorite "fill-in-the-blank" answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Classical Automation Pyramid — Extended with AR/VR

The traditional **ISA-95 (ANSI/ISA-95) automation hierarchy** has five levels. AR/VR does not replace any level; it serves as a **cross-cutting immersive interface** that spans Levels 0 through 3.

1. **Level 0 — Field Layer:** Physical sensors (temperature, pressure, flow) and actuators (valves, drives).
2. **Level 1 — Direct Control:** PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers) and RTUs (Remote Terminal Units) executing deterministic loops with cycle times in the **1–10 ms** range.
3. **Level 2 — Supervisory Control:** SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) and HMI panels aggregating plant-wide data.
4. **Level 3 — Operations & Manufacturing Execution:** MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems) and historians tracking production KPIs.
5. **Level 4 — Enterprise Layer:** ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) systems.
6. **Cross-Layer — Immersive CPS Overlay (NEW):** AR/VR headsets, digital twins, and cloud-rendered 3D dashboards consume data from Levels 0–3 and present it to the human operator spatially.

## 2.2 Core Sub-Systems: SCADA, DCS, and the Digital Twin

- **SCADA** is geographically distributed, optimized for **wide-area telemetry** (pipelines, power grids). It is event-driven and poll-based.
- **DCS (Distributed Control System)** is geographically concentrated, optimized for **continuous-process plants** (refineries, chemical plants). It is functionally distributed with redundant controllers.
- **Digital Twin** is a high-fidelity, real-time synchronized software replica of a physical asset. It consumes sensor streams via **OPC UA (IEC 62541)** and serves as the **3D backbone for VR visualization** and the **spatial anchor for AR overlays**.

## 2.3 Why AR/VR is Mathematically Necessary in HITL Control

In a classical PID loop, the human is excluded. In an **AR/VR-augmented HITL loop**, the human re-enters as a controller with an effective time delay $T_h$ (typically 150–300 ms for cognitive response). The total loop latency becomes:

$$T_{\text{loop}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{render}} + T_{\text{display}} + T_h + T_{\text{actuator}}$$

For closed-loop stability, the **Nyquist criterion** applied to a sampled system requires that the loop delay be a small fraction of the dominant process time constant $\tau_p$:

$$T_{\text{loop}} \ll \frac{\tau_p}{10}$$

This is precisely why **motion-to-photon latency must be below 20 ms** — otherwise $T_{\text{loop}}$ grows large enough to destabilize fast processes (e.g., robotic assembly at 1 kHz).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | **PID Controller** law (continuous-time) |
| 2 | $T_{\text{loop}} = T_s + T_c + T_r + T_d + T_h + T_a$ | **End-to-End HITL latency budget** (ms) |
| 3 | $T_{\text{loop}} \leq \dfrac{\tau_p}{10}$ | **Stability constraint** for HITL control |
| 4 | $\omega_c \leq \dfrac{\pi}{T_{\text{loop}}}$ | **Maximum closed-loop bandwidth** (rad/s) |
| 5 | $G_{\text{PM}} = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ | **Gain Margin** (unitless, ideally $\geq 2$) |
| 6 | $\phi_m = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc}) H(j\omega_{gc})$ | **Phase Margin** (degrees, ideally $\geq 45^\circ$) |
| 7 | $f_{\text{twin}} \geq 2 f_{\text{process}}$ | **Nyquist sampling for digital twin sync** (Hz) |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_{\text{reg}} = \sqrt{(x_r - x_v)^2 + (y_r - y_v)^2 + (z_r - z_v)^2}$ | **3D AR Registration Error** (mm) |
| 9 | $\text{FoV}_{\text{required}} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2D}\right)$ | **Field of View** from object size $d$ and viewing distance $D$ |
| 10 | $T_{\text{fr}} = \dfrac{1000}{f_{\text{refresh}}}$ | **Frame interval** (ms) for refresh rate $f$ in Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU answer sheets, always **state the units** beside numerical values. Examiners deduct marks for "20" without writing "20 ms".

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Predictive Maintenance:** AR overlays projected maintenance history and remaining useful life (RUL) onto a turbine during inspection.
- **Teleoperation of Hazardous Sites:** VR telepresence with haptic feedback allows operators to control robotic arms in nuclear or underwater facilities.
- **Operator Training:** VR-based simulation of plant startups reduces training time by 30–50 % and eliminates risk to real assets.
- **Cyber-Physical Security:** AR visualizes real-time intrusion-detection alerts overlaid on network switchgear, enabling rapid physical-cyber correlation.
- **Digital Twin-Driven Commissioning:** A VR replica of a not-yet-built plant allows virtual commissioning months before physical installation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industry Reference:** Siemens, ABB, Honeywell, and Bosch have all publicly demonstrated AR/VR-augmented control rooms. The KTU board often references these in "Discuss with examples" questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget for Industrial VR

**Goal:** Prove that the maximum tolerable motion-to-photon latency for an industrial teleoperation loop is **20 ms** when the dominant process time constant is $\tau_p = 200$ ms.

**Step 1 — Identify the components of total loop latency.**

The end-to-end latency is the sum of sensing, computing, rendering, display, human, and actuation delays:

$$T_{\text{loop}} = T_s + T_c + T_r + T_d + T_h + T_a$$

**Step 2 — Apply the stability constraint for HITL control.**

For the closed-loop to remain stable with at least 45° of phase margin, the total loop latency must be no more than one-tenth of the dominant process time constant:

$$T_{\text{loop}} \leq \frac{\tau_p}{10}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical value of $\tau_p$.**

$$T_{\text{loop}} \leq \frac{200 \text{ ms}}{10} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Decompose this 20 ms budget across the subsystems.**

A reasonable allocation for an industrial VR control station is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= 2 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(sensor acquisition)} \\
T_c &= 5 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(edge compute / state estimation)} \\
T_r &= 4 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(GPU stereoscopic rendering)} \\
T_d &= 3 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(display scan-out)} \\
T_h &= 4 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(AR/VR pipeline internal buffering)} \\
T_a &= 2 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(actuator command execution)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the sum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{loop}} &= 2 + 5 + 4 + 3 + 4 + 2 \\
&= 20 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclude the bandwidth limitation.**

The maximum achievable closed-loop bandwidth $\omega_c$ of the HITL system is then bounded by:

$$\omega_c \leq \frac{\pi}{T_{\text{loop}}} = \frac{\pi}{0.020} = 157 \text{ rad/s} \approx 25 \text{ Hz}$$

This means the operator can effectively control processes whose dynamics do not exceed 25 Hz — fast robotic joints are possible, but sub-millisecond motion control is not.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Digital Twin Synchronization Rate

**Goal:** Determine the minimum update frequency of a digital twin monitoring an industrial CNC spindle with a maximum vibration frequency of 800 Hz.

**Step 1 — Recall the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem.**

To reconstruct a band-limited signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest frequency component:

$$f_{\text{twin}} \geq 2 \, f_{\text{process,max}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the spindle vibration frequency.**

$$f_{\text{twin}} \geq 2 \times 800 \text{ Hz} = 1600 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Account for a practical oversampling factor of 2.5×** (industrial safety margin):

$$f_{\text{twin,design}} = 2.5 \times 1600 = 4000 \text{ Hz} = 4 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to frame interval in milliseconds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{frame}} &= \frac{1000}{f_{\text{twin,design}}} \\
&= \frac{1000}{4000} \\
&= 0.25 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the rendering implication.**

A VR scene consuming this twin at 4 kHz must therefore be streamed to the headset with sub-millisecond jitter — this is why industrial-grade VR teleoperation uses **foveated rendering** and **asynchronous timewarp** rather than consumer-grade pipelines.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — AR Registration Error Bound for Overlay Precision

**Goal:** Compute the registration error budget for overlaying a digital valve-position indicator on a real industrial valve located 0.8 m from the AR camera, given a sub-pixel SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) accuracy of $\sigma = 0.5$ mm in the X-Y plane and a depth uncertainty of $\sigma_z = 1.2$ mm.

**Step 1 — Form the 3D Euclidean error.**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{reg}} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\text{reg}} &= \sqrt{(0.5)^2 + (0.5)^2 + (1.2)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25 + 1.44} \\
&= \sqrt{1.94} \\
&\approx 1.39 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare against the KTU benchmark.**

Since $\varepsilon_{\text{reg}} \approx 1.39 \text{ mm} < 2 \text{ mm}$, the system satisfies the industrial AR registration benchmark. If the depth uncertainty doubles (e.g., to $\sigma_z = 2.4$ mm), the error becomes $\sqrt{0.25 + 0.25 + 5.76} \approx 2.5$ mm, exceeding the benchmark — a common KTU "what-if" question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Scenario | Parameter | Computed Value | KTU Benchmark | Pass/Fail |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Industrial VR teleop | $T_{\text{loop}}$ | 20 ms | < 20 ms | ✅ Pass |
| CNC spindle twin | $f_{\text{twin}}$ | 4 kHz | > 1.6 kHz | ✅ Pass |
| AR valve overlay | $\varepsilon_{\text{reg}}$ | 1.39 mm | < 2 mm | ✅ Pass |
| Degraded depth SLAM | $\varepsilon_{\text{reg}}$ | 2.5 mm | < 2 mm | ❌ Fail |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
