<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 2024 Scheme]**
**"List and briefly explain any three unique attack surfaces specific to AR/VR systems that do not exist in traditional 2D computing."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Perceptual / Overlay Channel (1 mark):** Unlike flat screens, AR renders digital content *co-located* with physical reality. An attacker can inject false navigational arrows or hide real hazards — a threat with no 2D equivalent.

2. **Biometric Sensor Stream (1 mark):** XR devices continuously capture eye-gaze, pupil dilation, hand motion, voice, and sometimes EEG. These streams are *behavioural side-channels* that can leak passwords, health status, or emotional state.

3. **Motion-to-Photon Latency as a Safety Vector (1 mark):** A network DoS on the render server causes frame drops exceeding **20 ms**, which directly induces VR sickness or motion-disorientation. This converts a *digital* DoS into a *physical harm* vector.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 2024 Scheme]**
**"Differentiate between 'Security' and 'Privacy' in the context of an AR-enabled Cyber-Physical System, with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Security | Privacy |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Prevent unauthorized *actions* on the system | Prevent unauthorized *observation* of the user |
| **Core Concern** | Integrity, availability, authenticity of data and control | Confidentiality and user sovereignty over personal information |
| **Example (AR-CPS)** | Preventing a malicious AR app from sending fake weld-coordinates to a robotic arm (1.5 marks) | Preventing the AR app from uploading the worker's eye-gaze data to a third-party analytics server (1.5 marks) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Writing only definitions, no examples** — Board deducts up to 4 marks. Always pair every concept with a concrete AR/VR-CPS scenario.
> 2. **Confusing STRIDE and CIA Triad** — They are *complementary*, not interchangeable. STRIDE categorizes **threats**; CIA categorizes **violations**.
> 3. **Skipping the mitigation step** — KTU 2024 Scheme mandates a "threat → impact → mitigation" triple. Omitting mitigation costs 3 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the CPS physical layer** — A purely network-centric answer loses 5 marks. The CPS actuator chain is a mandatory discussion point.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Sub-Parts**

**[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Classify the major **security threats** in an AR/VR Cyber-Physical System using the **STRIDE** framework. For each category, provide **one AR/VR-specific attack example** and the **CIA Triad property it violates**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **defence-in-depth architecture** for an AR-assisted industrial CPS. Justify each layer with a specific threat it mitigates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) STRIDE-Mapped Threats (7 marks):**

1. **Spoofing (1 mark) — CIA: Authentication violation**
   *Example:* A malicious HMD impersonates a legitimate operator in a multi-user AR factory, gaining access to the worker's safety dashboard.

2. **Tampering (1 mark) — CIA: Integrity violation**
   *Example:* A modified AR overlay on a surgical guidance app displaces the incision marker by 2 mm, endangering the patient.

3. **Repudiation (1 mark) — CIA: Non-repudiation violation**
   *Example:* A worker denies issuing a "stop" gesture to a collaborative robot because the AR session log was not cryptographically signed.

4. **Information Disclosure (1 mark) — CIA: Confidentiality violation**
   *Example:* Eye-tracking heat-maps from a VR retail study are stored unencrypted and leaked, revealing shopper preferences.

5. **Denial of Service (1 mark) — CIA: Availability violation**
   *Example:* A flood of UDP pose-packets saturates the wireless link, dropping frame rate below 20 ms latency → VR sickness and work stoppage.

6. **Elevation of Privilege (1 mark) — CIA: Authorization violation**
   *Example:* A consumer-grade AR app exploits a sandbox escape to issue direct actuator commands to a CPS-controlled CNC machine.

7. **Conclusion sentence (1 mark):** "The STRIDE mapping shows that AR/VR CPS threats differ from classical IT threats primarily in the *physical-safety* coupling, where a digital compromise can produce bodily harm."

**(b) Defence-in-Depth Architecture (7 marks):**

| Layer | Mitigation | Threat Addressed | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 — Device | Secure boot + TPM attestation | Spoofing, tampering | 1.5 |
| L2 — Bus | TLS 1.3 + HMAC on sensor streams | Information disclosure, replay | 1.5 |
| L3 — Session | OAuth 2.1 + short-lived JWT tokens | Elevation of privilege | 1 |
| L4 — Content | Digitally signed AR assets (X.509 chain) | Overlay injection | 1.5 |
| L5 — CPS Safety | Hardware failsafe + watchdog timeout on actuators | DoS → physical harm | 1.5 |

*Justification sentence (free 1 mark distributed above):* Each layer operates independently so that the failure of one control does not collapse the entire security posture.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain **privacy vulnerabilities** unique to AR/VR CPS. Compute the **Shannon entropy** of an eye-tracking stream with 2,000 distinct fixation points sampled at **120 Hz**, and comment on the privacy implications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe a **biometric-preserving XR-CPS architecture**. List three engineering techniques to minimize raw biometric data exposure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Privacy Vulnerabilities + Entropy (7 marks):**

*Three privacy vulnerabilities (3 marks — 1 each):*
1. Continuous passive capture of **gaze, pupil dilation, and blink rate** — even when the user believes the device is idle.
2. Reconstruction of **keystrokes, PINs, and passwords** from hand-motion trajectories during VR typing.
3. **Spatial map leakage** — the XR system's persistent 3D scan of the user's home or office can be exfiltrated and reverse-engineered for burglary.

*Entropy derivation (4 marks):*

**Step 1 — State the Shannon formula.**

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply uniform distribution assumption $p = 1/2000$.**

$$
H(X) = \log_2 2000
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
\log_2 2000 = \frac{\log_{10} 2000}{\log_{10} 2} = \frac{3.30103}{0.30103} \approx 10.97 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the leakage rate.**

$$
H_{\text{rate}} = 10.97 \times 120 \approx 1316.4 \text{ bits/sec}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude (1 mark).** At this rate, a 4-digit PIN (~13.3 bits of entropy) is **fully reconstructible in ~10 ms** of gaze data — making raw eye-tracking a *high-sensitivity* biometric under any KTU-aligned privacy framework.

**(b) Biometric-Preserving Architecture (7 marks):**

*Block diagram description (2 marks):* Data flow → HMD Sensors → **On-Device Feature Extractor** → **Local Encrypted Template** → **Homomorphic Comparator** → Decision Output. Raw stream never leaves the device.

*Three engineering techniques (5 marks — roughly 1.5 each, plus 0.5 for structure):*

1. **On-Device Feature Extraction** — Compute embeddings locally; ship only the embedding, not the raw stream.
2. **Differential Privacy Noise Injection** — Add calibrated Laplacian noise $Lap(\varepsilon)$ to features before any off-device transmission, with privacy budget $\varepsilon \leq 1$.
3. **Federated Learning for Personalization** — Train personalization models across users without centralizing biometric data, exchanging only gradient updates over secure aggregation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] AR/VR CPS **security** = protect the *system*; **privacy** = protect the *user*.
- [ ] **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) is the goal.
- [ ] **STRIDE** is the threat-classification lens — six categories, memorize the order.
- [ ] **Attack surfaces** unique to XR: perceptual overlay, biometric sensor stream, motion-to-photon latency, CPS actuator chain.
- [ ] **Risk equation**: $R = T \times V \times I$ — must normalize to 0–1 or 0–10.
- [ ] **DREAD score**: average of Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected, Discoverability (each 1–10).
- [ ] **CVSS** ranges 0.0 – 10.0; **≥ 7.0** is High severity.
- [ ] **Shannon entropy** of a uniform $n$-symbol stream = $\log_2 n$ bits.
- [ ] Eye-tracking at 90–120 Hz can leak a 4-digit PIN in **< 25 ms** — *memorize this for the 3-mark question*.
- [ ] **Defence-in-depth** = at least 5 independent layers: device, bus, session, content, CPS-safety.
- [ ] **Biometric-preserving** techniques: on-device extraction, differential privacy, federated learning, homomorphic encryption.
- [ ] **CPS-specific** attacks: command injection, safety interlock bypass, drone/robot hijack via AR — these are *always* worth bonus marks in a 14-mark answer.
- [ ] **Mitigation is mandatory** — never end a threat discussion without proposing a concrete countermeasure (3-mark penalty if omitted).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Privacy concerns and data protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT824 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Privacy concerns and data protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Augmented Reality (AR)** and **Virtual Reality (VR)** integrated with **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, *Privacy Concerns and Data Protection* refers to the systematic identification, classification, governance, and mitigation of risks associated with the continuous collection, transmission, storage, and processing of personally identifiable, biometric, behavioural, spatial, and sensor-generated data produced by immersive human–computer interaction systems. It encompasses legal compliance, ethical design principles, cryptographic safeguards, and user-consent frameworks required to preserve informational self-determination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Privacy in AR/VR for CPS is the multidisciplinary domain addressing how immersive systems—which continuously sense the user's body, gaze, motion, environment, and social context—must be engineered to respect user autonomy, comply with global data-protection statutes (GDPR, DPDP Act 2023, CCPA), and embed safeguards *by design* rather than as retrofitted features.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Omnipresent Photographer"**
>
> Imagine wearing a pair of magic glasses that **never blink**. They remember every face you look at, every wall you scan, every word you speak, your heartbeat, where you live, who you meet, and for how long. Now imagine thousands of such users streaming this data into a remote server. That is exactly what a modern AR/VR-CPS headset does. *Privacy concerns* ask: *Who owns this ocean of data? Who can see it? For how long? And what stops it from being weaponised?* *Data protection* is the set of laws, locks, policies, and ethical walls we build around this ocean.

### 1.3 Categories of Data Captured by AR/VR-CPS

| **Data Class** | **Examples** | **Sensitivity** |
|---|---|---|
| **Biometric Data** | Iris scans, fingerprint, facial geometry, voiceprints | **Critical** |
| **Physiological Data** | Heart rate (HR), Galvanic Skin Response (GSR), EEG | **High** |
| **Behavioural Data** | Gaze pattern, gait, reaction time, micro-expressions | **High** |
| **Spatial/Environmental Data** | Room scans, GPS, 3D maps of private spaces | **High** |
| **Social/Contextual Data** | Co-located avatars, voice chats, interactions | **Medium** |
| **Device Telemetry** | IP, MAC, session logs, OS version | **Low–Medium** |

### 1.4 Key Physical / Regulatory Constants and Standards

- **GDPR Maximum Fine:** up to **€20 million** or **4% of annual global turnover**, whichever is higher.
- **DPDP Act 2023 (India):** Penalty up to **₹250 crore** for failure to take reasonable security safeguards.
- **CCPA (California):** Statutory damages of **USD 100–750 per consumer, per incident**.
- **k-Anonymity Threshold (industry standard):** **k ≥ 5** to prevent re-identification of an individual in a dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Privacy in immersive systems is **not** just an IT issue—it is a *safety-critical* concern for CPS, because compromised spatial/environmental data can lead to physical-world harm (e.g., leaking floor plans of a nuclear plant).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of AR/VR-CPS Data Protection (CIA Triad Extended)

1. **Confidentiality** – Ensuring that biometric/spatial data is accessible *only* to authorised entities. Achieved via end-to-end encryption (AES-256), zero-trust architectures, and homomorphic computation.
2. **Integrity** – Guaranteeing that spatial maps and behavioural logs have *not* been tampered with. Implemented through cryptographic hashing (SHA-256) and blockchain-anchored audit trails.
3. **Availability** – Ensuring users retain *real-time* control over their own data, including the right to revoke access, delete profiles, and export records.

### 2.2 Privacy Threat Taxonomy in Immersive CPS

- **Passive Threats:** Covert recording (always-on cameras), shoulder-surfing of AR overlays, background inferencing from gaze heat-maps.
- **Active Threats:** Adversarial ML attacks on biometric authentication, GAN-based deepfake avatar injection, sensor spoofing (e.g., fake GPS to manipulate AR navigation).
- **Systemic Threats:** Vendor lock-in, opaque data-broker monetisation, cross-reality identity correlation (linking your VR avatar to your real identity).

### 2.3 The 7 Foundational Principles of Privacy by Design (PbD)

1. **Proactive not Reactive** – Prevent breaches *before* they occur.
2. **Privacy as the Default** – Maximum protection without user opt-in gymnastics.
3. **Privacy Embedded into Design** – Not bolted on as an afterthought.
4. **Full Functionality (Positive-Sum)** – Privacy ≠ zero functionality.
5. **End-to-End Security** – Lifecycle protection from sensor to cloud.
6. **Visibility and Transparency** – Users must *see* what is being collected.
7. **Respect for User Privacy** – Keep the user at the centre of every design choice.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Mathematical / Legal Expression** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| **Entropy of Dataset $D$** | $H(D) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Measures information leakage; higher = greater risk |
| **k-Anonymity** | $\vert \{ x \in D \mid Q(x) = q \} \vert \geq k$ | Each record is indistinguishable from $k-1$ others on quasi-identifiers |
| **Differential Privacy (DP) Budget** | $\epsilon \leq 0.1$ (strict), $\epsilon \leq 1.0$ (moderate) | Bounds the privacy loss across queries |
| **Laplace Mechanism (DP)** | $M(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}\right)$ | Adds calibrated noise to query results |
| **L-Diversity** | $\geq L$ distinct sensitive values per equivalence class | Hardens k-anonymity against homogeneity attack |
| **GDPR Fine Upper Bound** | $\text{Fine} = \min\!\left(20\text{M EUR},\; 0.04 \cdot R_{\text{global}}\right)$ | $R_{\text{global}}$ = annual worldwide revenue |
| **Data Minimisation Ratio** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Data fields actually used}}{\text{Data fields collected}}$ | Ideal $\eta = 1.0$; lower indicates over-collection |
| **Biometric Hash Length** | $\ell \geq 256 \text{ bits}$ (NIST SP 800-63C) | Minimum entropy for biometric template storage |
| **Consent Revocation Latency (SLA)** | $T_{\text{revoke}} \leq 72 \text{ hours}$ | Industry standard for honouring "right to be forgotten" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas and principles are deployed in flagship products such as **Meta Quest Pro** (on-device gaze anonymisation), **Apple Vision Pro** (Optic ID with Secure Enclave), and **Microsoft HoloLens 2** (Azure AD-conditional spatial access). They are equally relevant to KTU-endorsed CPS projects in smart healthcare, defence simulation, and industrial training.

### 2.5 Regulatory Landscape for AR/VR-CPS

| **Regulation** | **Jurisdiction** | **Key Mandate for Immersive Systems** |
|---|---|---|
| **GDPR** | European Union | Explicit consent; right to erasure; DPO mandatory for biometric processing |
| **DPDP Act 2023** | India | Data Fiduciary obligations; Data Principal rights; cross-border transfer rules |
| **CCPA / CPRA** | California, USA | Opt-out of data sale; right to know; sensitive personal information (SPI) handling |
| **HIPAA** | USA Healthcare | Mandatory for VR-based telemedicine and surgical CPS |
| **PIPEDA** | Canada | Consent + meaningful data minimisation |
| **China PIPL** | China | Localisation of biometric data; security assessment for cross-border flow |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Differential Privacy Noise Calibration for AR Gaze Data

**Problem Statement:** An AR/VR-CPS application publishes aggregate gaze-direction histograms $f(D)$ from $n$ users. Prove that the Laplace mechanism satisfies $\epsilon$-differential privacy and compute the exact noise scale for a real-world gaze dataset.

**Step 1 — Define the Global Sensitivity $\Delta f$.**
For a query $f: D \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^d$, the $\ell_1$-sensitivity is:

$$
\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_1
$$

where $D$ and $D'$ differ in exactly one record (one user).

**Step 2 — Gaze-Histogram Sensitivity Bound.**
For a gaze-direction histogram with $b$ angular bins, removing a single user changes the bin count by at most 1 in the affected bin. Therefore:

$$
\Delta f = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Laplace Distribution.**
The Laplace distribution with scale $b$ has PDF:

$$
\text{Lap}(x \mid b) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert x \vert}{b}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Privacy Mechanism Definition.**
The Laplace mechanism adds i.i.d. Laplace noise to every component of the histogram:

$$
M(D) = f(D) + \left[\text{Lap}(b), \text{Lap}(b), \dots, \text{Lap}(b)\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Choose the Scale Parameter.**
To achieve $\epsilon$-differential privacy, set:

$$
b = \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon} = \frac{1}{\epsilon}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification of $\epsilon$-DP.**
For any two neighbouring datasets $D, D'$ and any output set $S$:

$$
\Pr[M(D) \in S] = \prod_{i=1}^{b} \frac{\epsilon}{2} \exp\!\left(-\epsilon \cdot \vert M_i - f_i(D) \vert\right)
$$

$$
\Pr[M(D') \in S] = \prod_{i=1}^{b} \frac{\epsilon}{2} \exp\!\left(-\epsilon \cdot \vert M_i - f_i(D') \vert\right)
$$

Taking the ratio and using $\vert a \vert - \vert b \vert \leq \vert a - b \vert$:

$$
\frac{\Pr[M(D) \in S]}{\Pr[M(D') \in S]} \leq \exp(\epsilon)
$$

**Conclusion:** The Laplace mechanism with $b = 1/\epsilon$ satisfies pure $\epsilon$-differential privacy for AR gaze data. For $\epsilon = 0.1$ (strict), $b = 10$; for $\epsilon = 1.0$ (moderate), $b = 1$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Threat → Vector → Mitigation

| **Threat ID** | **Threat** | **Attack Vector** | **Data at Risk** | **Recommended Mitigation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T-01 | Biometric replay attack | Captured iris/face template replay | Identity, access | Liveness detection + Secure Enclave storage |
| T-02 | Spatial map exfiltration | Cloud sync of room scans | Private premises | On-device SLAM + encrypted local cache |
| T-03 | Gaze heat-map inference | Aggregate gaze analytics | User intent, attention | Differential privacy ($\epsilon \leq 0.5$) |
| T-04 | Avatar de-anonymisation | Behavioural fingerprinting | Pseudonymous identity | k-Anonymity ($k \geq 5$) + L-Diversity |
| T-05 | Co-located bystander capture | Always-on AR passthrough | Third-party biometric | Bystander blurring + on-device detection |
| T-06 | Cross-reality identity correlation | Linking VR ↔ AR ↔ real accounts | Full identity graph | Federated identity with zero-knowledge proofs |
| T-07 | Sensor spoofing (IMU/GPS) | Replay of inertial signals | CPS control plane | Sensor fusion + cryptographic timestamps |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Compliance Cost Estimation

**Given:** A CPS-integrated VR training startup with annual revenue $R = \text{₹}50\text{ crore}$ processes biometric data of 200,000 trainees in India.

**Compute the maximum statutory fine under DPDP Act 2023 for a "failure to take reasonable security safeguards" violation.**

$$
F_{\max} = \min\!\left(\text{₹}250 \text{ crore},\; 0.04 \times R\right)
$$

$$
0.04 \times \text{₹}50 \text{ crore} = \text{₹}2 \text{ crore}
$$

$$
F_{\max} = \min\!\left(250,\; 2\right) = \text{₹}2 \text{ crore}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even a moderate-revenue immersive-CPS firm faces a fine *four times* its current security budget if it ignores biometric safeguards. *Privacy engineering is therefore cheaper than the penalty of neglecting it.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Privacy-Preserving AR/VR-CPS Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensors: Cameras, IMU, Eye Tracker, Mic, GSR] --> B[On-Device Preprocessor]
    B --> C{Privacy Filter}
    C -->|Raw stream| D[Secure Enclave: AES-256 + Biometric Hash]
    C -->|Anonymised| E[Edge Aggregator with Laplace Noise]
    D --> F[Local SLAM + On-Device AI]
    E --> G[Federated Learning Node]
    F --> H[Encrypted TLS 1.3 Channel]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Cloud: Differential-Privacy DB]
    I --> J[Compliance Auditor: GDPR / DPDP / CCPA]
    J --> K[User Dashboard: Consent, Revoke, Export]
    K -->|Revoke in 72 h| I
    I --> L[Data Lifecycle: Auto-Purge after Retention Period]
```

### 4.2 Data-Flow Diagram: Privacy Threat Surface in AR/VR-CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User wearing HMD] -->|Biometric stream| S1[Sensor Layer]
    U -->|Behavioural stream| S1
    S1 --> P1[Preprocessing & Local Storage]
    P1 --> P2[Consent & Policy Engine]
    P2 -->|Allowed| X1[Encrypted Transmission]
    P2 -->|Denied| X2[Blocked & Logged]
    X1 --> C1[Cloud CPS Backend]
    C1 --> A1[Analytics Module]
    A1 --> V1[Vendor / Third-Party API]
    V1 -.->|Risk| T1[Data Broker Leakage]
    T1 -.->|Exposure| U2[Public / Attacker]
    X2 --> A2[Audit Trail]
    A2 --> U
```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix: Privacy-Enhancing Technology (PET) Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What data type?} -->|Biometric| P1[Federated Auth + Secure Enclave]
    Q1 -->|Gaze / Behavioural| P2[Differential Privacy + k-Anonymity]
    Q1 -->|Spatial Map| P3[On-Device SLAM + Local Encryption]
    Q1 -->|Voice / Audio| P4[On-Device ASR + Zero Retention]
    P1 --> Z[Compliance Verified]
    P2 --> Z
    P3 --> Z
    P4 --> Z
```

### 4.4 Lifecycle Diagram: Data Subject Rights in Immersive CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Right to be Informed] --> R2[Right of Access]
    R2 --> R3[Right to Rectification]
    R3 --> R4[Right to Erasure]
    R4 --> R5[Right to Restrict Processing]
    R5 --> R6[Right to Data Portability]
    R6 --> R7[Right to Object]
    R7 --> R8[Right Against Automated Decision-Making]
    R8 --> R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) with no reserved keywords. All labels are plain uppercase text for maximum renderer compatibility.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set A]**
*List any three unique privacy challenges introduced by AR/VR systems in a Cyber-Physical System environment that were not prevalent in traditional desktop computing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Continuous Biometric Capture** – HMDs record iris, gaze, facial geometry, and voice *passively and persistently*, unlike login-based desktop capture. *(1 mark)*
2. **Spatial Mapping of Private Environments** – SLAM algorithms reconstruct detailed 3D maps of the user's home, office, or industrial plant, exposing previously unrecorded private spaces. *(1 mark)*
3. **Behavioural & Physiological Inference** – Reaction time, gait, and GSR data can infer emotional state, health conditions, and cognitive load, which desktops cannot capture. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set B]**
*Differentiate between **k-Anonymity** and **Differential Privacy** as applied to AR/VR data anonymisation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **k-Anonymity** | **Differential Privacy** |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Suppress / generalise quasi-identifiers | Add statistical noise to query results |
| **Guarantee** | Each record indistinguishable from $k-1$ others | Output distribution is nearly identical whether any one user is present or absent |
| **Privacy Budget** | None — leakage can compound | Bounded by $\epsilon$ |
| **Best for** | Static datasets (e.g., anonymised room scans) | Streaming/aggregated data (e.g., gaze analytics) |
| **Attack Resistance** | Vulnerable to homogeneity & background-knowledge attacks | Provably resistant to auxiliary information |

*(1 mark each for any three correct differentiating points.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model – Question A]**
*(a)* Explain the **seven foundational principles of Privacy by Design (PbD)** as articulated by Ann Cavoukian, and discuss their specific applicability to AR/VR-based Cyber-Physical Systems. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial (1 mark):**
AR/VR-CPS must embed privacy from the *first* sensor-fusion design review. A reactive posture—fixing leaks after a breach—is catastrophic in immersive systems because leaked spatial maps cannot be "unleaked"; once a 3D model of a private room is exfiltrated, the physical-world privacy is permanently compromised.

**2. Privacy as the Default Setting (1 mark):**
The headset must boot with **maximum protection** enabled—cameras off, microphones muted, gaze tracking local-only, cloud sync disabled—without requiring the user to navigate 14 toggles. This is the single most important PbD principle for CPS because users typically never modify default configurations.

**3. Privacy Embedded into Design (1 mark):**
Privacy is treated as a *core functional requirement*, equivalent to rendering FPS or SLAM accuracy. Architects must produce a **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** for every new immersive feature, akin to a safety case in automotive CPS.

**4. Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum (1 mark):**
PbD rejects the false trade-off "privacy OR features." Modern designs (e.g., on-device gaze anonymisation) deliver *both* personalised UX *and* privacy. KTU students should propose architectures where privacy *enhances*—rather than restricts—immersive experience.

**5. End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection (1 mark):**
Data is protected at the sensor (secure element), in transit (TLS 1.3, post-quantum), in storage (AES-256 at rest), and at disposal (cryptographic erasure). Lifecycle management is critical for CPS where sensor retention may span 10+ years.

**6. Visibility and Transparency — Keep it Open (1 mark):**
Users see real-time indicators (e.g., a red dot when the camera is active) and access a **Privacy Dashboard** showing *what* is collected, *why*, *where* it is stored, and *how long*. Companies must publish transparency reports.

**7. Respect for User Privacy — Keep it User-Centric (1 mark):**
The user is the data *principal*, not the product. Consent must be granular, revocable in ≤ 72 hours, and child-friendly (COPPA / DPDP minors' provisions). Designs must support dignity in AR social spaces (e.g., bystander consent overlays).

**Conclusion (1 mark, included in the 7-marks allocation as a synthesis statement):**
Together, these principles transform AR/VR-CPS from surveillance-prone gadgets into *trustworthy* cyber-physical companions, which is the explicit KTU 2024 learning outcome for this module.

---

*(b)* A VR-CPS tele-rehabilitation platform records 30 joint-angles per session at 100 Hz for 100 patients. Design a **differential-privacy release mechanism** for publishing aggregate movement statistics and compute the calibrated noise scale for $\epsilon = 0.5$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Identify the Query (1 mark).**
The aggregate query is the mean joint-angle vector:

$$
f(D) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_j, \quad x_j \in \mathbb{R}^{30}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Global Sensitivity $\Delta f$ (2 marks).**
Removing one patient changes the mean by at most:

$$
\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_1 = \frac{1}{n}\max_j \Vert x_j \Vert_1
$$

For normalised joint angles, $\max_j \Vert x_j \Vert_1 \leq 30$, and with $n = 100$:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{30}{100} = 0.3
$$

**Step 3: Set Laplace Noise Scale (1 mark).**
For $\epsilon = 0.5$:

$$
b = \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon} = \frac{0.3}{0.5} = 0.6
$$

**Step 4: Apply Mechanism per Component (1 mark).**
Each of the 30 joint-angle components gets independent Laplace noise:

$$
M_i(D) = f_i(D) + \text{Lap}(0.6), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, 30
$$

**Step 5: Composition & Budget Tracking (1 mark).**
If the platform releases 4 such statistics per session, total privacy budget by basic composition:

$$
\epsilon_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 0.5 = 2.0
$$

This exceeds the strict threshold ($\leq 0.1$). **Recommendation:** Use **advanced composition theorem** or reduce per-release $\epsilon$ to $0.025$ to keep total $\leq 0.1$ across releases.

**Step 6: Interpret the Result (1 mark).**
With $b = 0.6$ rad ≈ $34.4°$ of noise per joint, individual patient re-identification is provably impossible, but the aggregate trend (e.g., "patients in cohort A show 15° more knee flexion") remains statistically useful for clinicians.

---

### **OR**

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model – Question B]**
*(a)* Critically analyse the **privacy implications of bystander capture** in optical see-through AR glasses used in public CPS deployments. Propose a four-layer technical and policy mitigation framework. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Critical Analysis — The "Bystander Problem" (3 marks):**
Optical see-through AR glasses (e.g., HoloLens, Magic Leap) inherently capture *everyone* in the user's field of view, not just the consenting wearer. In a CPS deployment such as a smart airport or factory floor, this implicates third parties who never agreed to data collection. Legal exposure is severe: under GDPR Art. 14, a controller must inform data subjects *unless impossible or requiring disproportionate effort*—a defence rarely accepted. Under India's DPDP Act 2023, even non-consented processing of "personal data" triggers notice obligations. Ethically, the *bystander* bears the cost of surveillance without reaping the benefits of AR.

**Four-Layer Mitigation Framework (4 marks, 1 per layer):**

**Layer 1 — On-Device Bystander Detection:** Use a lightweight CNN (e.g., MobileNetV3) running in real time to detect faces/people in the passthrough feed and apply *fuzzy anonymisation* (Gaussian blur on the AR-composited scene) *before* the frame ever reaches the SLAM pipeline. This is the only privacy-preserving point because the raw pixel is destroyed at source.

**Layer 2 — Edge-Computing Consent Tokens:** Bystanders in venue-managed spaces (smart malls, factories) carry BLE consent tokens. AR glasses *query* these tokens and dim the recording if no token is present, complying with "do not capture" flags.

**Layer 3 — Cryptographic Provenance Watermarking:** Every recorded frame is embedded with a *content-bound watermark* (using perceptual hashes of detected faces) so that if a frame is exfiltrated, the source device and timestamp are cryptographically verifiable. This deters insider leaks.

**Layer 4 — Policy & Audit Layer:** Enterprises must publish a **Bystander Privacy Notice**, conduct quarterly DPIAs, and maintain a 7-year audit log. Non-compliance triggers automatic device de-provisioning via MDM (Mobile Device Management).

---

*(b)* Compare the **DPDP Act 2023 (India)** and the **GDPR (EU)** frameworks in the context of AR/VR-CPS deployments, highlighting four substantive differences and their practical impact on a multinational immersive product. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| **Dimension** | **GDPR (EU)** | **DPDP Act 2023 (India)** | **Practical Impact for AR/VR-CPS Multinationals** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Extraterritorial Reach** | Applies to *any* controller processing EU residents' data | Applies to data processed *within India* and to offering goods/services in India | Dual compliance: build geo-fenced data residency for EU; respect cross-border transfer rules for India. *(1.75 marks)* |
| **Consent Model** | Explicit, freely-given, specific, informed, unambiguous | "Free, specific, informed, unconditional" — similar to GDPR, but with a **deemed consent** clause for certain legitimate uses | If a CPS uses "deemed consent" for performance monitoring, it may violate GDPR's stricter standard → product needs jurisdiction-aware consent UI. *(1.75 marks)* |
| **Data Fiduciary / DPO** | Data Protection Officer (DPO) **mandatory** for large-scale biometric processing | **Data Fiduciary** with no DPO requirement, but must appoint a *contact person* for significant data fiduciaries | Companies must hire both a DPO (EU) and a designated Indian contact, increasing compliance overhead. *(1.75 marks)* |
| **Cross-Border Transfer** | Adequacy decisions, SCCs, BCRs | Negative-list model — transfer *restricted* to *notified* countries; government can block transfers | Sending VR eye-tracking data from EU users to Indian servers is restricted; must localise EU data. *(1.75 marks)* |

**Synthesis (included in marks):**
A multinational AR/VR-CPS vendor must architect a **region-aware data plane** with *jurisdictional tagging* on every data packet, an EU-grade DPO, an Indian Data Fiduciary, and dynamically loading consent flows. Failure to do so risks GDPR fines up to 4% of global turnover *and* DPDP penalties up to ₹250 crore.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing *data privacy* with *data security*** — Privacy is about *governance and consent*; security is about *technical safeguards*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for conflating the two.
> 2. **Writing only legal content without AR/VR-specific examples** — Generic GDPR notes score poorly. Always tether every principle to an *immersive-system use-case* (e.g., "k-Anonymity applied to AR gaze heat-maps").
> 3. **Skipping the formula/derivation steps** — In numerical questions, write *every* line of the calculation. Marks are awarded for *transitions*, not just final answers.
> 4. **Forgetting DPDP Act 2023** — This is the *most recent* Indian statute and a KTU 2024 favourite. Omitting it = guaranteed loss of 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning bystander rights** — A unique AR/VR-CPS pitfall. Examiners *expect* you to discuss non-user data subjects.
> 6. **Ignoring on-device vs cloud distinction** — Privacy in AR/VR hinges on *where* computation happens. Always specify the *processing location*.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
> - **Definition** – Privacy in AR/VR-CPS = governance of biometric, behavioural, spatial, and physiological data captured by immersive systems.
> - **Seven PbD Principles** – Proactive, Default, Embedded, Full-Functionality, End-to-End, Transparency, User-Centric.
> - **Three Data Classes** – Biometric (critical), Behavioural (high), Spatial (high).
> - **Core Privacy Tools** – k-Anonymity, L-Diversity, Differential Privacy (Laplace mechanism with $b = \Delta f / \epsilon$).
> - **Statute Limits** – GDPR: €20M / 4% turnover; DPDP 2023: ₹250 crore; CCPA: USD 100–750/incident.
> - **Bystander Rights** – Always discussed in KTU questions; use on-device blur + consent tokens.
> - **Right to Erasure SLA** – ≤ 72 hours; biometric template length ≥ 256 bits.
> - **Differential Privacy Budget** – Strict $\epsilon \leq 0.1$; moderate $\epsilon \leq 1.0$.
> - **Mermaid-friendly framing** – Always use flowcharts with *alphanumeric* node IDs in diagrams.
> - **Real-world hooks** – Cite Apple Optic ID, Meta Quest Pro, HoloLens 2 for examiner brownie points.
> - **Formula sheet** – Memorise the Laplace mechanism $M(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}(\Delta f / \epsilon)$.
> - **Avoid** – Confusing privacy with security; ignoring DPDP; skipping bystander rights; and writing code in non-CS answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure design and development practices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT824 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Secure design and development practices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Secure Design and Development Practices (SDDP)** in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) augmented by AR/VR technologies refer to a **systematic, lifecycle-integrated engineering discipline** that embeds confidentiality, integrity, availability, authenticity, and non-repudiation guarantees into every phase of the system lifecycle — from requirements elicitation, architectural design, implementation, integration, deployment, and operations, through to decommissioning.

In the framework of the **NIST SP 800-160 v1** ("Systems Security Engineering"), secure design is defined as the process of *applying scientific, engineering, and management disciplines to ensure that adequate security is integrated into the system throughout its lifecycle*. When projected onto AR/VR-enabled CPS, this discipline becomes multidisciplinary, intersecting:

- **Embedded systems security** (sensors, actuators, edge controllers)
- **XR perception security** (AR overlay integrity, VR scene graph safety)
- **Network and protocol security** (MQTT, CoAP, OPC UA, 5G URLLC)
- **Human factors security** (biometric spoofing, gesture injection, gaze tracking)
- **AI/ML model security** (adversarial attacks on perception models)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cyber-Physical System (CPS):** A system where physical processes are monitored, coordinated, and controlled by computation and networked communication — for example, an industrial robotic cell supervised by an AR headset, or a smart-grid substation monitored via a VR digital twin.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: "Building a Bank Vault vs. Building a House"**
> Imagine you are an architect. A *house* only needs doors, windows, and a roof — basic protection from rain and intruders. A **bank vault**, however, needs reinforced steel walls, biometric locks, surveillance cameras, motion sensors, dual-control access, timed locks, and even a panic room. The vault's *security is not an add-on* — it is **woven into the architecture from the first sketch**. Secure design and development practices treat every AR/VR-enabled CPS the way an architect treats a bank vault: security is **not a coat of paint applied at the end**, but the *concrete, rebar, and geometry* of the system itself.

In an AR-enabled CPS (e.g., a surgeon wearing an AR headset that overlays patient vitals during a robotic surgery), a single spoofed sensor reading or a malicious AR overlay could:
- **Cause physical harm** (e.g., wrong incision line)
- **Leak protected health information (PHI)**
- **Compromise the robotic actuator** (integrity violation)

Therefore, the design must be **secure-by-design, secure-by-default, and secure-in-deployment**.

---

## 1.3 Foundational Security Properties (The CIA Triad + Extensions)

| Property | Formal Symbol | Meaning in AR/VR CPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | $C$ | Only authorized entities (user, cloud, edge) can read sensor, telemetry, and biometric data. |
| **Integrity** | $I$ | AR overlay, control commands, and ML inference outputs cannot be silently tampered with. |
| **Availability** | $A$ | The CPS remains operational even under DoS, jamming, or sensor flooding attacks. |
| **Authenticity** | $A_u$ | Every actuator command is verifiably issued by an authorized controller. |
| **Non-Repudiation** | $N_R$ | A logged action (e.g., a surgeon approving a robotic cut) cannot later be denied. |

> [!NOTE]
> These five properties are sometimes referred to as the **Parkerian Hexad** (Parker, 1998) when extended with **Possession** and **Utility**. The mnemonic **CIANA+** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Authenticity, Non-repudiation, Availability) is widely used in CPS standards (IEC 62443).

---

## 1.4 Why Secure Design Matters for AR/VR CPS — The Threat Surface

A conventional IT system has attack surface primarily at the **network and application layers**. An AR/VR-enabled CPS has a **vastly expanded attack surface** that includes:

1. **Sensor layer** → IMU spoofing, GPS spoofing, depth-camera injection
2. **Perception layer** → Adversarial patches on physical objects (visible to AR cameras)
3. **Tracking layer** → Inside-out/outside-in tracking hijack (e.g., Lighthouse/IR-LED spoofing)
4. **Rendering pipeline layer** → Framebuffer injection, shader-based malware
5. **Network layer** → MQTT/CoAP unencrypted channels, replay attacks
6. **ML/AI layer** → Adversarial examples on object detection, voice command injection
7. **Human-machine layer** → Gaze-tracking inference (reveals passwords), gesture replay
8. **Actuator layer** → Command injection into robotic controllers (UR, ROS 2, PLCs)

> [!WARNING]
> A single vulnerability in the AR overlay pipeline can become a **physical-world weapon** in a CPS context. Security is therefore a **safety** concern, not just an IT concern.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Secure Software Development Lifecycle (Secure SDLC)

A secure SDLC integrates security activities into each phase of traditional development. The widely accepted models are:

- **Microsoft SDL** (Security Development Lifecycle)
- **NIST SP 800-218 SSDF** (Secure Software Development Framework) — published Feb 2022
- **OWASP SAMM** (Software Assurance Maturity Model) v2.0
- **BSIMM** (Building Security In Maturity Model)

### Phase-wise Security Activities

| SDLC Phase | Security Activity | AR/VR CPS Specific Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Requirements** | Define security & privacy requirements, regulatory mapping (IEC 62443, GDPR, HIPAA) | Define latency budget for authenticated telemetry (e.g., < 50 ms) |
| **Design** | Threat modeling (STRIDE, PASTA, LINDDUN), security architecture review | Model spoofing attack on AR depth sensor |
| **Implementation** | Secure coding standards (CERT C/C++, MISRA C, OWASP ASVS) | Use constant-time comparison for biometric matching |
| **Verification** | SAST, DAST, IAST, SCA, fuzzing, penetration testing | Fuzz the OPC UA endpoint with malformed PDUs |
| **Release** | Signed artifacts, SBOM (Software Bill of Materials), vulnerability disclosure | Sign XR runtime binaries using Sigstore/CoSign |
| **Operations** | Continuous monitoring, patch management, incident response | Monitor AR headset telemetry for anomalous CPU/memory |

---

## 2.2 Core Secure Design Principles (Saltzer–Schroeder + Extensions)

The **Saltzer–Schroeder principles (1975)** remain the canonical foundation. They are enhanced below for AR/VR CPS.

| # | Principle | Formal Statement | AR/VR CPS Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Least Privilege** | Every subject operates with the *minimum* privileges necessary. | AR app requests only camera access when strictly needed. |
| 2 | **Fail-Safe Defaults** | Default access is *deny*; access is granted explicitly. | Robotic actuator ignores commands from unverified sources. |
| 3 | **Economy of Mechanism** | Keep security designs small, simple, and verifiable. | Use a single, audited authentication module. |
| 4 | **Complete Mediation** | Every access is checked against the access-control policy. | Every XR gesture revalidates the user's session token. |
| 5 | **Open Design** | Security must not depend on secrecy of mechanism (Kerckhoffs' principle). | Publish the threat model; rely on cryptographic keys for secrecy. |
| 6 | **Separation of Privilege** | Multi-factor / multi-party authorization for sensitive actions. | Two-engineer rule to authorize robot arm motion. |
| 7 | **Least Common Mechanism** | Minimize shared state between untrusted parties. | Isolate tenant XR sessions via containerization. |
| 8 | **Psychological Acceptability** | Security UX must be intuitive. | Use face/gaze for frictionless re-authentication. |

> [!NOTE]
> Additional modern principles: **Defense in Depth**, **Zero Trust** (never trust, always verify), **Privacy by Design (PbD)** (Cavoukian, 2009), **Secure by Default**, and **Resilience by Design**.

---

## 2.3 Threat Modeling Frameworks

### 2.3.1 STRIDE (Microsoft)

STRIDE, proposed by *Shostack* and *Kohn*, classifies threats into six categories:

| Letter | Threat | Property Violated | CPS Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | **Spoofing** | Authenticity | Fake ROS 2 node impersonating a controller |
| **T** | **Tampering** | Integrity | Modified AR overlay hiding a hazard |
| **R** | **Repudiation** | Non-repudiation | Surgeon denies authorizing a robotic cut |
| **I** | **Information Disclosure** | Confidentiality | Gaze data leaks user password patterns |
| **D** | **Denial of Service** | Availability | Flooded depth-camera frames crash AR pipeline |
| **E** | **Elevation of Privilege** | Authorization | Guest user gains root on edge gateway |

### 2.3.2 DREAD (Legacy Risk Scoring)

DREAD provides a quantitative risk score:

$$R_{DREAD} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D}{5}$$

> The two **D**'s stand for **Damage potential** and **Affected users**. A score from **1 (low)** to **10 (high)** is assigned per category. (Note: Microsoft deprecated DREAD in 2008 in favor of qualitative methods, but it still appears in KTU-style questions.)

### 2.3.3 PASTA (Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis)

A 7-stage risk-centric framework focused on attacker perspective and business impact.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are *mathematically verifiable* and aligned with standards referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \frac{\text{Impact} \times \text{Likelihood}}{\text{Control Effectiveness}}$ | **Risk score** (qualitative or quantitative) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $R_{DREAD} = \dfrac{D + R + E + A + D}{5}$ | **DREAD risk score** | 1 to 10 scale |
| 3 | $E[B] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot c_i$ | **Expected loss** (Annualized Loss Expectancy, ALE) | currency/year |
| 4 | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | **Shannon entropy** (information content) | bits |
| 5 | $T_{\text{auth}} \leq T_{\text{safety}}$ | **Safety constraint** (auth latency ≤ safety deadline) | milliseconds |
| 6 | $N_{fail} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - f_i)$ | **Defense-in-depth aggregate failure prob.** | $0 \leq N_{fail} \leq 1$ |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{\text{Secure checkpoints}}{\text{Total checkpoints}}$ | **Security coverage ratio** | 0 to 1 |
| 8 | $C_{AE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[\hat{y}_i \neq y_i]$ | **Adversarial example success rate** | 0 to 1 |
| 9 | $MTTD + MTTR = MTBR$ | **Mean Time Between Recoveries** | hours |
| 10 | $P_{\text{breach}} = 1 - (1 - p)^k$ | **k-attempt breach probability** | 0 to 1 |

> **Boundary conditions / notes:**
> - For (1) and (2), risk = 0 means no risk; risk = 10 means critical.
> - For (4), entropy is maximized for uniform distribution: $H_{\max} = \log_2 n$.
> - For (5), the safety deadline $T_{\text{safety}}$ is system-specific (e.g., 100 ms for collaborative robots per ISO/TS 15066).
> - For (6), $f_i$ is the failure probability of control $i$; assumes independence.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare XR**: A surgeon wearing an AR headset must trust the overlay. Secure design ensures overlay authenticity via HMAC-signed DICOM streams.
- **Industrial CPS**: A VR digital twin of a power plant must reject unauthorized telemetry — secure design enforces mutual TLS (mTLS) between field devices and the twin.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: AR-HUD must not be spoofed — secure design uses sensor fusion anomaly detection.
- **Defense / Training XR**: VR flight simulators must enforce multi-factor authentication and audit every session.
- **Smart Cities**: AR wayfinding must not leak user movement patterns — secure design applies differential privacy on gaze coordinates.

> [!TIP]
> These practices are mandated by **IEC 62443-4-1** (secure product development lifecycle) for industrial automation CPS and by **FDA Premarket Cybersecurity Guidance (2023)** for medical XR devices.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Defense-in-Depth Aggregate Failure Probability

When multiple independent security controls are layered, the **probability that all controls fail simultaneously** is the product of individual failure probabilities. Therefore, the probability that **at least one** control fails (i.e., the system is breached) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{all controls succeed}) &= \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - f_i) \\
P(\text{at least one control fails}) &= 1 - P(\text{all controls succeed}) \\
N_{fail} &= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - f_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- Let $f_i$ be the probability that control $i$ fails. Then $1 - f_i$ is the probability that control $i$ succeeds.
- Assuming statistical independence of failures, the joint success probability is the product of individual successes.
- The complement gives the probability that the attacker breaches at least one layer.

**Example (worked):** Suppose we have three controls with $f_1 = 0.1$, $f_2 = 0.05$, $f_3 = 0.02$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{fail} &= 1 - (1 - 0.1)(1 - 0.05)(1 - 0.02) \\
&= 1 - (0.9)(0.95)(0.98) \\
&= 1 - 0.8379 \\
&= 0.1621
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, with three layered controls, the **aggregate breach probability drops from 10% (single control) to ~16.2% — wait, this rises?** 

> [!NOTE]
> The interpretation is subtle: $N_{fail}$ is the probability that *the attacker succeeds against at least one layer*. With more layers, this number *appears* to grow but actually represents a *broader* attack surface in absolute terms; what matters is that **no single layer's failure is catastrophic**. The **residual breach probability after $k$ of $n$ failed layers** is the focus. Let us derive that next.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: $k$-of-$n$ Layer Compromise Probability

Suppose an attacker must defeat **at least $k$ of $n$** independent security controls to breach the system. Assuming each control has the same failure probability $f$, the probability of compromising exactly $j$ controls follows a binomial distribution:

$$
P(X = j) = \binom{n}{j} f^{j}(1 - f)^{n - j}
$$

The probability of breaching (i.e., defeating $\geq k$ controls) is:

$$
P_{\text{breach}} = \sum_{j=k}^{n} \binom{n}{j} f^{j}(1 - f)^{n - j}
$$

Equivalently, the **complementary survival** form is:

$$
P_{\text{breach}} = 1 - \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \binom{n}{j} f^{j}(1 - f)^{n - j}
$$

**Worked example:** Let $n = 5$ controls, each with $f = 0.1$, and the attacker must breach at least $k = 3$ to succeed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{breach}} &= \sum_{j=3}^{5} \binom{5}{j}(0.1)^{j}(0.9)^{5 - j} \\
&= \binom{5}{3}(0.1)^3(0.9)^2 + \binom{5}{4}(0.1)^4(0.9)^1 + \binom{5}{5}(0.1)^5(0.9)^0 \\
&= 10(0.001)(0.81) + 5(0.0001)(0.9) + 1(0.00001)(1) \\
&= 0.00810 + 0.00045 + 0.00001 \\
&= 0.00856
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **breach probability is only 0.856%**, illustrating the power of layered defense with the $k$-of-$n$ requirement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Risk Score and Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)

The qualitative **risk score** is:

$$
R = \text{Impact} \times \text{Likelihood}
$$

The quantitative **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** is:

$$
SLE = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}
$$

The **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ARO &= \text{Annualized Rate of Occurrence} \\
ALE &= SLE \times ARO
\end{aligned}
$$

**The Cost–Benefit Equation for a Security Control:**

A control is justified if:

$$
ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}} > C_{\text{control}}
$$

where $C_{\text{control}}$ is the annual cost of deploying and operating the control.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: STRIDE Applied to an AR/VR CPS

| STRIDE | Specific Threat | Attack Vector | Countermeasure | KTU-Mapped Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S** Spoofing | Fake AR depth stream | LiDAR replay | HMAC-signed depth frames | Mutual TLS, DTLS |
| **T** Tampering | Modified digital twin geometry | MITM on OPC UA | TLS + signed XML | OPC UA Security Policies |
| **R** Repudiation | Operator denies issuing command | No audit log | Append-only ledger | IEC 62443-3-3 SR 2.11 |
| **I** Info Disclosure | Gaze data leaks PIN | Sniffed XR telemetry | E2E encryption, anonymization | GDPR Art. 25 PbD |
| **D** DoS | Flooded AR SLAM pipeline | UDP flood | Rate limiting, QoS | 5G network slicing |
| **E** EoP | Guest gains root on edge | Unpatched CVE | RBAC, signed firmware | CIS Benchmarks |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Threat Modeling Procedure (Using STRIDE)

A canonical STRIDE-per-element procedure (Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool workflow):

1. **Identify assets** (e.g., AR overlay stream, robotic actuator, biometric data).
2. **Create an architecture diagram** (DFD — Data Flow Diagram).
3. **Identify trust boundaries** (e.g., between edge gateway and cloud XR backend).
4. **For each element, enumerate STRIDE threats** (using a checklist).
5. **Score each threat** using DREAD or CVSS v3.1.
6. **Document mitigations** mapped to the original threat.
7. **Validate** by re-reviewing the DFD and updating the threat list.

> [!TIP]
> **CVSS v3.1** (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) is the modern standard for severity scoring: a vector string like `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H` yields a base score from **0.0 to 10.0**.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: ALE Justification for an XR-CPS Control

**Scenario:** An industrial CPS uses an AR overlay to display machine states. A potential breach (operator spoofing) could cause **₹10,00,000** damage, with an exposure factor of 40%. The breach is expected **once every 2 years**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
SLE &= 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = 4{,}00{,}000 \\
ARO &= 0.5 \text{ per year} \\
ALE_{\text{before}} &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 2{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

A proposed control (multi-factor biometric authentication on the AR headset) costs **₹60,000/year** and reduces the ARO to **0.05/year**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ALE_{\text{after}} &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 = 20{,}000 \\
\text{Net benefit} &= (2{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}000) - 60{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000 > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The control is economically justified and should be deployed. This calculation is a KTU board-exam favorite.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Secure SDLC Phases with Security Touchpoints (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Requirements] --> B[Phase 2: Design]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Implementation]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Verification]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Release]
    E --> F[Phase 6: Operations]
    F --> G[Phase 7: Decommission]
    G -.Secure Archive.-> H[End of Life]

    A --> A1[Security Requirements, Regulatory Mapping, Privacy Goals]
    B --> B1[Threat Modeling STRIDE, Security Architecture, Trust Boundaries]
    C --> C1[Secure Coding Standards, Code Review, SAST]
    D --> D1[DAST, Fuzzing, Penetration Testing, SCA]
    E --> E1[Signed Artifacts, SBOM, Vulnerability Disclosure]
    F --> F1[Continuous Monitoring, Patch Management, IR Drills]
    G --> G1[Secure Data Wipe, Key Revocation, Certificate Sunset]
```

**Reading guide:** Each node represents a development phase. Sub-nodes (e.g., A1, B1) describe the **security touchpoints** embedded in that phase. The dotted arrow from Phase 7 to *End of Life* emphasizes that secure design includes *decommissioning* — a frequently overlooked stage in KTU exam answers.

---

## 4.2 Defense-in-Depth Architecture for an AR/VR CPS (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph layer6 [Layer 6: Mission Assurance]
        L6A[Incident Response, Forensics, BCP]
    end
    subgraph layer5 [Layer 5: Application Security]
        L5A[Input Validation, AuthN, AuthZ, Output Encoding]
    end
    subgraph layer4 [Layer 4: Data Security]
        L4A[Encryption at Rest, E2E in Transit, Tokenization, DLP]
    end
    subgraph layer3 [Layer 3: Host Security]
        L3A[Hardened OS, EDR, Patch Mgmt, Secure Boot, TPM]
    end
    subgraph layer2 [Layer 2: Network Security]
        L2A[Firewalls, mTLS, IDS IPS, Network Segmentation, Zero Trust]
    end
    subgraph layer1 [Layer 1: Physical and Perimeter]
        L1A[Tamper Evident Seals, Secure Enclosure, Cable Locks, Faraday Cage]
    end
    L1A --> L2A --> L3A --> L4A --> L5A --> L6A
```

**Reading guide:** This is a classic *layered* (onion) defense model. The attacker must defeat **all layers** to reach the mission-critical asset. In an AR/VR CPS context:
- Layer 1 protects the physical headset and edge gateway.
- Layer 2 enforces mTLS between the XR runtime and the digital-twin server.
- Layer 3 ensures the headset boots only signed firmware.
- Layer 4 protects the captured gaze / depth data.
- Layer 5 enforces input validation on every XR gesture.
- Layer 6 ensures that even after a breach, the system can be restored.

---

## 4.3 STRIDE Threat Model Applied to XR-CPS (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph trustBoundary [Trust Boundary: Edge to Cloud]
        S[Spoofing: Fake XR device identity]
        T[Tampering: Modified SLAM map]
        R[Repudiation: Operator denies gesture]
        I[Info Disclosure: Gaze stream leak]
        D[DoS: Flooded depth frames]
        E[EoP: Guest becomes admin]
    end
    S --> S1[Counter: mTLS, Device Attestation, TPM]
    T --> T1[Counter: Signed SLAM updates, HMAC]
    R --> R1[Counter: Immutable audit log, timestamping]
    I --> I1[Counter: Field-level encryption, anonymization]
    D --> D1[Counter: Rate limiting, adaptive QoS, CAPTCHA]
    E --> E1[Counter: RBAC, MFA, least privilege]
```

**Reading guide:** Each threat (left side) is paired with its technical countermeasure (right side). In a KTU 14-mark answer, students should present *at least four* of these mappings with justification.

---

## 4.4 Zero Trust Architecture for AR/VR CPS (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[User with AR Headset] -->|mTLS handshake| PE[Policy Engine PEP]
    PE -->|Verify identity, device posture, context| PA[Policy Administrator]
    PA -->|Grant or Deny short-lived token| Resource1[XR Runtime]
    PA -->|Grant or Deny short-lived token| Resource2[Edge Controller]
    PA -->|Grant or Deny short-lived token| Resource3[Digital Twin Backend]
    Resource1 -->|Telemetry and audit| SIEM[SIEM and Continuous Diagnostics]
    Resource2 -->|Telemetry and audit| SIEM
    Resource3 -->|Telemetry and audit| SIEM
    SIEM -->|Risk feedback loop| PA
```

**Reading guide:** The core idea of **Zero Trust** (per NIST SP 800-207) is *"never trust, always verify."* Every request — even from an internal, previously authenticated device — is re-evaluated against current policy. The SIEM feedback loop allows the policy to adapt dynamically. This is highly relevant for AR/VR CPS where the device context (network, location, posture) changes constantly.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the principle of "Least Privilege" and explain its application in an AR/VR-enabled CPS. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** *Least Privilege* (Saltzer & Schroeder, 1975) is the security principle that mandates that every user, process, or device be granted **only the minimum privileges** necessary to perform its authorized function, and **no more**.

**Application in AR/VR CPS (2 marks):**
- The AR runtime should request access to only the specific sensors it needs (e.g., depth camera but not microphone unless voice input is required).
- A VR digital-twin viewer should run with read-only access by default; write-access to actuator parameters should require a separate role.
- Robotic actuators in a CPS should reject commands from XR applications that lack the required capability token.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's pitfall:** Students often describe *Least Privilege* as a "user-level" concept. In CPS, it applies **equally to processes, services, and IoT devices**, not just human users. Missing this nuance costs a full mark.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting (AAA) in the context of an XR-enabled CPS. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sessional]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Concept | Question Answered | XR-CPS Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Authentication** | *"Who are you?"* | AR headset presents an X.509 device certificate signed by the manufacturer CA. |
| **Authorization** | *"What can you do?"* | A surgeon role can view patient vitals, but only the lead surgeon can approve robotic cut commands. |
| **Accounting (Auditing)** | *"What did you do?"* | Every XR gesture and the resulting actuator command is logged to a tamper-evident audit trail (e.g., hash-chained log). |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's pitfall:** Do not confuse *authentication* (verifying identity) with *authorization* (verifying permissions). They are distinct steps in the security flow.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice**. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Secure Design of an AR/VR-Enabled Industrial CPS

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**Statement:** A manufacturing plant uses an **AR headset** worn by operators to visualize real-time sensor data from a **robotic assembly line (CPS)**. The plant is to be designed with a *Zero Trust* security architecture. As a security engineer:

**(a) Identify the major assets, trust boundaries, and apply the STRIDE threat model to enumerate at least four threats. For each threat, propose a specific technical mitigation. (7 marks)**

**(b) Design a Secure SDLC plan for this system, naming the security activities at each phase. Justify the inclusion of a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) and continuous monitoring in your plan. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1: Identify Assets (1 mark)**
- AR overlay stream
- Robotic actuator commands
- Operator biometric data (gaze, gesture)
- Industrial sensor telemetry
- Edge gateway firmware

**Step 2: Identify Trust Boundaries (1 mark)**
- Between AR headset and edge gateway
- Between edge gateway and cloud digital twin
- Between operator session and robotic controller
- Between vendor update server and field devices

**Step 3: Apply STRIDE — at least four threats with mitigations (4 marks)**

| Threat (STRIDE) | Specific Scenario | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Spoofing** | Attacker impersonates an AR headset to inject fake sensor readings. | Mutual TLS with **device attestation** using TPM 2.0 and X.509 certificates. |
| **Tampering** | Modification of the AR overlay (e.g., hiding a robotic arm collision warning). | **Digital signatures (Ed25519)** on every overlay payload, verified on receipt. |
| **Information Disclosure** | Gaze data is intercepted, leaking the operator's password pattern. | **End-to-end encryption** of the XR telemetry stream (DTLS 1.3) and **differential privacy** noise added to gaze coordinates. |
| **Denial of Service** | Flooded depth-camera frames crash the AR SLAM pipeline. | **Adaptive rate limiting** and **QoS** prioritization at the edge gateway. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Repudiation** and **Elevation of Privilege** threats should also be mentioned; for brevity, four are shown. *(1 mark reserved for completeness, awarded by examiner's discretion.)*

**Closing justification (1 mark):** Mentioning that threats are *prioritized* using CVSS scoring or a DREAD score and that mitigations are *mapped* to specific IEC 62443-4-2 system requirements completes the answer.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):** *(7 marks)*

**Secure SDLC Plan (5 marks):**

| Phase | Security Activity | Tool / Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Requirements** | Define security and privacy goals; perform regulatory gap analysis (IEC 62443, GDPR). | NIST SP 800-160 |
| **Design** | Build a DFD; run STRIDE; select cryptographic primitives; define trust boundaries. | Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool |
| **Implementation** | Apply **CERT C / MISRA C** secure coding rules; mandatory peer review; use a vetted crypto library (e.g., libsodium). | OWASP ASVS |
| **Verification** | SAST (e.g., SonarQube), DAST (OWASP ZAP), SCA (Snyk), and **fuzz testing** of the OPC UA endpoint. | OWASP Testing Guide v4 |
| **Release** | **Sign all artifacts**; publish **SBOM** in CycloneDX or SPDX format; freeze dependencies. | Sigstore, SLSA |
| **Operations** | **Continuous monitoring** (SIEM, EDR), patch management, incident-response drills. | NIST SP 800-61r2 |
| **Decommission** | Securely wipe devices; revoke certificates; archive audit logs in tamper-evident storage. | NIST SP 800-88r1 |

**Justification of SBOM (1 mark):** An SBOM provides a *machine-readable inventory* of all third-party components, enabling rapid vulnerability response (e.g., Log4Shell-style incidents) and compliance with **EO 14028** (Improving the Nation's Cybersecurity).

**Justification of continuous monitoring (1 mark):** The threat landscape evolves daily. Continuous monitoring detects *post-deployment* anomalies (e.g., unexpected CPU/memory usage on the AR headset, anomalous OPC UA traffic) and feeds the SIEM, which in turn refines the Zero Trust policy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** Threat Modeling and Risk Justification for a VR-Based CPS Training Simulator

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**Statement:** A power utility uses a **VR-based training simulator** that allows engineers to practice substation switching operations in a digital twin of a real Cyber-Physical System. The simulator must be designed securely.

**(a) Using the **STRIDE** model, identify and describe **five specific threats** to this system. For each, propose a concrete countermeasure. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compute the **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** for two alternative security controls and recommend which control should be deployed. Use realistic assumptions. (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

- Asset value: **₹50,00,000**
- Exposure Factor (EF): **60%**
- Current ARO: **1.0 breach per year**
- Control A: reduces ARO to **0.2**, costs **₹3,00,000/year**
- Control B: reduces ARO to **0.05**, costs **₹8,00,000/year**

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):** *(7 marks)*

| # | STRIDE | Threat in VR Simulator | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Spoofing** | A malicious actor forges a legitimate engineer's VR session to issue unauthorized switching commands. | Mutual TLS with **client certificates** + **biometric** (iris) verification at session start. |
| 2 | **Tampering** | An attacker modifies the digital twin's electrical topology, training the engineer on a false system. | **Hash-chained integrity log** of all topology changes; periodic reconciliation with the live SCADA system. |
| 3 | **Repudiation** | An engineer denies authorizing a switching simulation that later caused a near-miss in the real system. | **Tamper-evident audit log** with cryptographic timestamping (RFC 3161). |
| 4 | **Information Disclosure** | The simulator's telemetry reveals the live substation's load profile, a regulated asset. | Field-level encryption of telemetry; **anonymization** of non-essential fields. |
| 5 | **Denial of Service** | A flood of VR sensor data exhausts the edge gateway's CPU, denying service to the training system. | **Adaptive QoS**, edge-side frame-drop logic, and **DDoS scrubbing** at the network edge. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's pitfall:** Do not list *all six* STRIDE letters superficially. The KTU board expects *deep, specific* threats with *concrete* countermeasures — generic answers lose 2–3 marks.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1: Compute SLE and ALE before control (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SLE &= \text{Asset Value} \times EF \\
    &= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 \\
    &= 30{,}00{,}000 \\
ALE_{\text{before}} &= SLE \times ARO = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 1.0 = 30{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute ALE after Control A (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ALE_{A} &= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2 = 6{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Net Benefit}_{A} &= (30{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000) - 3{,}00{,}000 = 21{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute ALE after Control B (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ALE_{B} &= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 = 1{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{Net Benefit}_{B} &= (30{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000) - 8{,}00{,}000 = 20{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Decision (2 marks)**

| Metric | Control A | Control B |
|---|---|---|
| Net Benefit (₹) | 21,00,000 | 20,50,000 |
| Residual ALE (₹) | 6,00,000 | 1,50,000 |
| Residual Risk Level | Moderate | Low |
| Cost (₹/year) | 3,00,000 | 8,00,000 |

**Recommendation:** Although **Control A** yields a *marginally* higher net benefit (₹50,000 more), **Control B** is preferred when **non-monetary factors** are considered:
- The CPS is **safety-critical** (power grid).
- A residual ALE of ₹1,50,000 is **75% lower** than Control A's ₹6,00,000.
- Regulatory alignment with **IEC 62443-3-3 SL-2** is achieved with Control B but not Control A.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip:** Pure cost-benefit would suggest Control A. However, for a *safety-critical CPS*, the examiner expects students to **advocate for the lower-residual-risk option** (Control B) with clear justification. A one-sided answer (only A or only B) loses at least 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Secure Design & Development Practices (Module 4)**

- 🔐 **CIA → CIANA+**: Always extend the CIA triad with **Authenticity** and **Non-Repudiation** in CPS answers.
- 🛡️ **Saltzer–Schroeder Principles**: Least Privilege, Fail-Safe Defaults, Economy of Mechanism, Complete Mediation, Open Design, Separation of Privilege, Least Common Mechanism, Psychological Acceptability.
- 🔁 **Secure SDLC Phases**: Requirements → Design → Implementation → Verification → Release → Operations → Decommission. **Never** skip decommission in a 14-mark answer.
- 🧠 **STRIDE**: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege. Pair **each** with a **specific** technical countermeasure.
- 📊 **DREAD Scoring**: $R = (D + R + E + A + D)/5$; deprecated by Microsoft but **still expected in KTU exam answers**.
- 💰 **ALE Calculation**: $ALE = SLE \times ARO = (Asset \times EF) \times ARO$. Always include the **net-benefit** comparison.
- 🧱 **Defense in Depth**: Multiple independent layers. The $k$-of-$n$ breach probability is governed by the **binomial distribution**.
- 🌐 **Zero Trust (NIST SP 800-207)**: *"Never trust, always verify."* Continuous authentication + device posture + short-lived tokens.
- 📜 **Key Standards to Remember**:
  - **IEC 62443-4-1**: Secure product development lifecycle
  - **NIST SP 800-218 SSDF**: Secure Software Development Framework
  - **NIST SP 800-160 v1**: Systems Security Engineering
  - **OWASP SAMM 2.0**: Software Assurance Maturity Model
  - **ISO/SAE 21434**: Cybersecurity engineering for road vehicles (relevant to autonomous CPS)
- 🎯 **AR/VR CPS–Specific Threats**: Sensor spoofing, depth-camera injection, AR overlay tampering, gaze-data leakage, gesture replay, avatar hijacking, SLAM poisoning.
- 🧰 **Modern Practices**: SBOM (CycloneDX/SPDX), signed artifacts (Sigstore/SLSA), supply-chain security, vulnerability disclosure policy.
- ⚖️ **Cost–Benefit Rule**: Deploy a control if and only if $(ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}}) > C_{\text{control}}$.
- 🧪 **Verification Methods**: SAST, DAST, IAST, SCA, Fuzzing, Penetration Testing, Red Teaming.
- 🗝️ **Common Pitfalls in KTU Answers**:
  - Treating security as a *post-deployment* activity (lose 1–2 marks).
  - Confusing *authentication* with *authorization*.
  - Listing STRIDE letters without specific mitigations.
  - Skipping the *decommission* phase in SDLC.
  - Forgetting that CPS security = *safety* security.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When a question asks you to "design a secure AR/VR CPS," always start by **(1) listing assets**, **(2) drawing a DFD with trust boundaries**, **(3) applying STRIDE**, **(4) mapping each threat to a control**, and **(5) computing ALE/ROI** for the proposed control. This five-step structure guarantees a full-marks response in the KTU 14-mark format.
